Methodology RoundPipe has three algorithmic layers. The Computation Dispatch Paradigm stores all model weights, gradients, optimizer states, and stage-boundary activations in CPU host DRAM; GPU workers are stateless and execute whichever stage is dispatched to them. The RoundPipe Schedule divides M micro-batches into rounds of M_R ≥ N micro-batches; within each round, S_f forward stages followed by S_b backward stages form a linear dispatch sequence where stage slot i goes to GPU (g0+i) mod N, and the starting GPU g0 rotates between rounds to maintain continuous flow. Asymmetric partitioning uses 3 transformer layers per forward stage and 1 layer per backward stage (since backward with recomputation costs ~3× forward), with the first backward stage fusing forward and backward to avoid redundant recomputation. Asynchronous staleness-1 optimizer updates (via a separate optimizer worker) eliminate warm-up and cool-down bubbles at iteration boundaries. Three critical implementation challenges are addressed: (1) a priority-aware multi-stream transfer engine using four CUDA streams per device — activation transfers (critical path) proceed first, while parameter/gradient transfers are chunked via longest-processing-time-first scheduling and packed into idle windows between activation slots, preventing head-of-line blocking; (2) a fine-grained per-layer event-based consistency protocol that replaces a global FP32→FP16 weight-copy barrier with per-layer threading events, allowing the optimizer worker to release early layers before deeper layers finish synchronizing; and (3) an O(L³) automatic stage partitioning algorithm that enumerates O(L²) candidate t_max values from contiguous layer-time sums, then for each runs a greedy O(L) feasibility check against memory and time constraints. Key Results This is a preprint (arXiv:2604.27085v1, April 2026). On 8×RTX 4090 (consumer hardware): RoundPipe achieves 1.48–2.16× throughput improvement over Mobius and 4.7–7.3× longer maximum sequence length vs. the next-best non-TP baseline; RoundPipe-sync (no async optimizer) achieves 1.15–1.63× over Mobius. RoundPipe is the only system that can LoRA fine-tune Qwen3-235B (235B-A22B MoE) on 24 GB GPUs with 31K sequence length — all baselines OOM. Near-linear throughput scaling is demonstrated from 1–8 GPUs. Maximum sequence length is invariant to GPU count (e.g., 49K tokens for LLaMA-3.1-8B regardless of 1 or 8 GPUs). On 8×A800 SXM (datacenter NVLink hardware): RoundPipe delivers 0.98–1.47× vs. SOTA datacenter baselines, and the 4090 RoundPipe achieves at least 76% of A800 SOTA throughput across all models. Ablation via pipeline simulator: RoundPipe-sync reduces bubble ratio 23–55% relative to the best looped baseline; full async RoundPipe drives absolute bubble ratio below 4.5%. Fine-grained consistency protocol saves 2.6–14 seconds per iteration vs. blocking copy, scaling with trainable parameter count. Baselines are current and representative: DeepSpeed ZeRO-2, FSDP, ZeRO-Infinity, Megatron-PP, Megatron-TP, and Mobius on both hardware configurations. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The computation dispatch paradigm — using CPU offloading as the enabling mechanism to decouple pipeline stages from GPUs — is a genuine non-obvious insight that recasts a hardware constraint as a scheduling opportunity. The asymmetric forward/backward stage splitting is a concrete method contribution not present in prior pipeline schedules. The open-source Python library is a real system artifact. Individual components (CPU offloading, round-robin scheduling, async optimizer, event-based synchronization) are individually known; the novelty is in their co-design specifically for the consumer GPU / PCIe-bottlenecked setting. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover the four main design choices: pipeline schedule (bubble simulation from real per-layer timings), fine-grained consistency protocol (direct iteration-time comparison vs. blocking), sequence length sensitivity, and multi-GPU scaling. No error bars or multiple seeds are reported; throughput is averaged over 10 iterations after warm-up, which is standard practice for steady-state benchmarks. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/ITcarrot/RoundPipe with documentation. Methodological concern: the bubble ratio ablation (Figure 15) uses a pipeline simulator rather than direct hardware profiling, though it is seeded with real per-layer timing measurements. More significantly, training correctness under staleness-1 async optimizer is asserted based on citations to prior work, not verified experimentally on the tested models; no downstream task quality or loss convergence curves are provided anywhere in the paper. Deployability (SYSTEMS) - Practical: Runs on commodity 8×RTX 4090 hardware with no specialized interconnects; the open-source library exposes a two-API interface (forward_backward() and step()) that wraps standard PyTorch semantics. - Efficient: PCIe transfer overhead is fully overlapped with computation at batch sizes as small as B=8 for dense models (rigorously shown via roofline analysis in Appendix C); MoE models require B≥80 for full overlap, noted but not highlighted as a limitation. CPU optimizer for 32B models takes 9.6 seconds per step, hidden by async execution. - Effective: The evidence that RoundPipe solves its stated problem (bubble reduction on consumer GPUs) is strong: 1.48–2.16× over the best prior system on the exact target hardware, with the 235B capability as an existence proof. However, there is no measurement of training quality (loss, downstream accuracy) — the system solves throughput, not correctness, and the latter is assumed from prior work on staleness-1. - Scalable: Near-linear scaling from 1–8 GPUs on a single server is demonstrated. Multi-server scaling is not addressed; the design fundamentally assumes all GPUs share one CPU memory pool, so multi-node deployment would require a different architecture not described. Delivers: a working open-source Python library with public documentation and GitHub repository. Strengths - The computation dispatch paradigm is a clean and non-obvious insight: the same PCIe transfers already required for CPU offloading can be redirected to any GPU, turning an unavoidable cost into a scheduling degree of freedom. The insight simultaneously resolves both types of pipeline bubbles rather than trading one for the other. - Asymmetric stage splitting — maintaining separate layer partitions for forward and backward passes — addresses a real asymmetry (backward with recomputation ≈ 3× forward) that all prior symmetric pipeline schedules ignore, and the bubble formula is rigorously derived and verified. - The fine-grained per-layer event-based consistency protocol is a concrete engineering contribution whose impact (2.6–14 seconds per iteration saved) is quantified directly, and the design is clearly specified with five explicit ordering constraints. - The 235B MoE fine-tuning result on 24 GB consumer GPUs is a qualitatively new capability — no prior system achieves this — demonstrating that the memory architecture is strictly superior to existing offloading approaches, not merely faster. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge: (1) staleness-1 async optimizer introduces gradient delay, with correctness relying on prior work's empirical finding that one-step staleness does not harm convergence; (2) MoE models require larger batch sizes (B≥80) to achieve compute-bound PCIe overlap, acknowledged in the roofline appendix; (3) the RoundPipe-sync variant (no async optimizer) pays a CPU optimizer step overhead, quantified but not fully hidden. The authors do not directly confront the single-server limitation as a limitation. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No convergence or task-quality evaluation is provided. For a training systems paper proposing a schedule with async optimizer updates and non-standard gradient ordering, the absence of loss curves or fine-tuning accuracy metrics is a significant omission — throughput without correctness verification is incomplete. - The single-server scope is understated. The computation dispatch paradigm requires all stage weights and activations to be accessible in one host memory pool. Extending to multi-node settings would require inter-node activation transfers (with latency far worse than PCIe), and this architectural constraint is never discussed as a limitation. - Bubble ratio analysis uses simulation (Figure 15) rather than hardware-measured GPU idle time. While the simulator is driven by real per-layer timings, real-world effects (NCCL synchronization, CUDA stream contention under four concurrent streams) may differ. - No comparison to LoHan [26], a contemporaneous system for fine-tuning 100B models on consumer GPUs, despite it being cited in related work. - The staleness-1 correctness argument is borrowed entirely from ZeRO-Offload and HeteropipelinePP citations; RoundPipe's stage ordering and gradient accumulation across rounds may interact with staleness differently than those systems, and this is not analyzed. Bottom Line RoundPipe delivers a genuine systems insight — repurposing the CPU offloading data path as a stage-dispatch mechanism — with strong throughput results and a working open-source artifact, but practitioners should independently verify training correctness (convergence) before adopting it for production fine-tuning workflows.
Recommended 2604.27085 PDF
#19 — Innovative (74)

Temporal Contrastive Decoding: A Training-Free Method for Large Audio-Language Models

arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Temporal Contrastive Decoding (TCD), a training-free inference-time decoding method that improves large audio-language models' sensitivity to transient acoustic cues by contrasting next-token logits from original and temporally blurred (slow-path) audio encodings. - Why it's important: Unified LALMs exhibit a temporal smoothing bias — brief, localized acoustic events (ring counts, chord durations, prosodic stress) are underweighted relative to language priors during autoregressive generation, degrading audio-grounded accuracy in ways that neither retraining nor prior modality-level contrastive decoding methods target. - How it does it: At each decoding step TCD re-encodes a Hann-window-blurred waveform, computes the rectified logit difference over a small candidate set, and injects this signal through a gate conditioned on per-step audio-attention reliance and prediction uncertainty — no gradient or parameter update required. - The good: TCD improves Qwen2.5-Omni from 71.5% to 73.2% on MMAU test-mini and yields +6.0 / +7.7 accuracy on SLURP / CochlScene temporal tasks, with 0.99× decode-step latency overhead; the architectural applicability study cleanly confirms gains are confined to unified (temporally aligned) architectures, exactly as predicted. - The missing: All evaluations are multiple-choice QA on short clips; open-ended generation quality is never tested, one per-backbone hyperparameter (γ_gate) requires manual tuning without a principled selection rule, and no error bars or significance tests are reported. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building or deploying unified LALMs who want inference-time grounding improvements without retraining; practitioners using bottleneck-encoder architectures (SALMONN, AudioFlamingo) or focused on open-ended generation can skip it.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (80) is the strongest dimension — clean ablations, Gaussian-noise negative control, and the falsifiable architectural-applicability prediction (gains only on unified architectures) cleanly confirmed. Non-Obviousness (80): multi-timescale slow/fast contrast at the waveform level is a non-obvious twist on contrastive decoding. Novelty (70) solid but within an established paradigm. Completeness (65)/Resilience (65) limited by MC-only evaluation, no error bars, and per-backbone γ_gate tuning. Impact (75) lifted by training-free, near-zero decode overhead applicable to deployed unified LALMs, though no code released. Weighted ≈ 74.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 65 Resilience 65 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Unified large audio-language models that keep temporally aligned audio tokens visible to the decoder throughout autoregressive generation can still under-utilize brief, transient acoustic events — ring onsets, chord transitions, prosodic inflections. The decoder's token predictions are shaped by temporally smooth context and language priors, which can dominate over time-local cues. This "temporal smoothing bias" is distinct from general audio hallucination (generating audio-unsupported content) and from modality imbalance (attending to audio vs. not). Prior contrastive decoding methods for LALMs (Audio-Aware Decoding contrasts audio vs. no-audio; AVCD contrasts audio vs. visual modalities) address modality-level grounding but do not explicitly contrast audio at different temporal resolutions, leaving this timescale-specific failure mode unaddressed. The problem is particularly acute for sound counting, acoustic scene classification, and musical temporal reasoning tasks where single brief events determine the correct answer. Key Idea The core insight is that convolving the input waveform with a Hann window and re-encoding the smoothed signal produces a "slow-path" reference that preserves coarse acoustic context but attenuates transient variation; the difference between original and slow-path logits isolates next-token preferences driven by short-duration acoustic evidence rather than by temporally smooth context or language priors. Applying only the rectified (positive) difference as a sparse, gated logit boost amplifies transient-supported tokens while leaving language-dominated decoding steps unchanged. Methodology TCD operates in four stages at each decoding step. (1) Slow-path construction: the input waveform x is convolved with a normalized Hann window of duration W ∈ [8, 30] ms and amplitude-normalized, then re-encoded to obtain H̃ = E(K(x)). (2) Stability-guided parameterization: a self-normalized stability score S is computed per encoder layer as S_ℓ = M_ℓ / (M_ℓ + F_ℓ), where M_ℓ is mean activation magnitude and F_ℓ is mean temporal flux; layers are aggregated with softmax weights proportional to each layer's audio-attention ratio r_ℓ, so depth emphasis tracks where the decoder actually queries audio. S linearly maps to both the blur window W and update scale λ without any held-out calibration data. (3) Contrastive evidence score: the rectified logit difference d⁺_t = max(z_t − z̃_t, 0) is computed over candidate set Ω_t = TopK(z_t, K_orig=16) ∪ TopK(z̃_t, K_blur=8), decoupling candidate selection from contrastive scoring. (4) Gated logit fusion: step-wise gate g_t = min(γ_gate · r_t · Ĥ_t^0.5, 1.0) multiplies audio-reliance r_t (fraction of decoder attention mass on audio tokens, top-4 layers) by normalized top-K entropy Ĥ_t; the final update is z_t^TCD(j) = z_t(j) + λ · g_t · d⁺_t(j) for j ∈ Ω_t. Critical design choices: (a) Positive-only (ReLU) update is well-justified — signed updates degrade Music and Speech (63.0 vs. 63.8 avg) by suppressing tokens supported by the textual prefix. (b) Dual gating on audio reliance and uncertainty is well-justified — ungated TCD gains Sound but loses Speech (62.6 vs. 62.3 overall). (c) Structured temporal blur is necessary — replacing blur with Gaussian noise drops below baseline (59.9 vs. 62.3), validating that the slow-path must encode a coarser temporal view of the same signal, not arbitrary perturbation. Key Results MMAU test-mini (multiple-choice): Qwen2.5-Omni 71.5% → 73.2% (+1.7); Qwen2-Audio-Instruct 62.3% → 63.8% (+1.5); Mini-Omni 41.2% → 42.8% (+1.6). Music gains dominate on the strongest backbone (Qwen2.5-Omni +5.1). Both AAD contrast strengths (α=0.5: 71.0%, α=1.0: 70.4%) score below Qwen2.5-Omni baseline (71.5%), while TCD exceeds it. AIR-Bench Foundation: Qwen2.5-Omni 64.8% → 66.7% (+1.9), with Sound gaining more than Speech (71.6%→74.5% vs. 61.8%→63.2%). Temporally structured tasks (Qwen2.5-Omni, AIR-Bench protocol, 1k-example subsets): SLURP +6.0 (75.5→81.5), CochlScene +7.7 (73.8→81.5), Clotho-AQA +2.7 (71.7→74.4). Non-unified architectures (SALMONN, AudioFlamingo3, DeSTA2.5-Audio, MiMo-Audio-7B): changes within ±0.3%, consistent with TCD's design requirement. Compute (Qwen2-Audio-7B on A800-80GB, eager attention): 2.04× prefill overhead; 0.99× decode-step latency; 1.01× memory (15.85→16.05 GB). Baselines include current strong systems; Qwen2.5-Omni (2025) is a top open unified LALM. Scale is standard benchmark — no stress-scale evaluation. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The temporal slow-path / fast-path contrast applied at the waveform encoding level during autoregressive audio-language decoding is a new idea within the LALM inference space. The self-normalized stability score that adapts blur and scale without calibration data is a new engineering contribution. The general contrastive decoding framework is well-established (DoLa, AAD, AVCD); the specific multi-timescale acoustic contrast targeting transient cues in unified decoders is what is genuinely novel, not the broader decoding paradigm. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations in Table 4 isolate all three design choices (structured blur, gating, positive-only update) on Qwen2-Audio-Instruct / MMAU test-mini. The Gaussian-noise negative control is a strong methodological choice. No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple-run variance are reported anywhere. Code is not mentioned as released; there is no repository link. The paper is an arXiv preprint (v1, 16 Apr 2026), not peer-reviewed. The per-backbone γ_gate parameter is set once and held fixed, but no sensitivity analysis is provided. The efficiency analysis disables FlashAttention to isolate algorithmic overhead, which is methodologically careful. Evaluation strictly follows official benchmark protocols (test-mini split, official scripts), reducing cherry-picking risk. One potential concern: the temporal-task gains (SLURP +6.0, CochlScene +7.7) are large but come from benchmark subsets included within AIR-Bench, which is also used for the main Foundation evaluation — the tasks are not fully independent test sets. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — inference-time algorithm evaluated by running existing models on public benchmarks. - Practical: Runs on 4× A100-40GB for Qwen2.5-Omni and benchmarked on a single A800-80GB; all hardware is commercially available and no new parameters are introduced. - Efficient: Decode-step overhead is negligible (0.99×) due to the memory-bound nature of LLM decoding at small batch sizes; prefill overhead is 2.04× and adds real latency for interactive or low-latency applications, partially mitigable with KV-cache reuse (noted but not implemented). - Effective: Demonstrates consistent accuracy improvements across three unified LALMs on MMAU and AIR-Bench multiple-choice tasks; qualitative examples confirm temporal-event error correction, but open-ended generation quality is never evaluated. - Scalable: Not tested beyond the standard benchmark clips (latency analysis uses 3-second audio); behavior on longer audio, streaming, or larger batch sizes is stated as future work and not measured. The paper delivers no software artifact; it stops at paper form with full hyperparameter documentation in Appendix A. Strengths - The architectural applicability study (Table 5) is the paper's strongest contribution: TCD produces negligible change (≤0.3%) on four bottleneck/patch-based encoders and consistent gains on three unified architectures, providing clean empirical validation of the hypothesis that temporally aligned decoder access is necessary. This falsifiable prediction being confirmed substantially strengthens the paper's mechanistic claims. - The self-normalized stability score (Eq. 3) is a principled, data-free way to adapt blur window and update scale across encoder architectures with different latent scales, avoiding the need for held-out calibration data that would undermine the training-free claim. - The Gaussian-noise ablation (accuracy drops to 59.9, below the 62.3 baseline) is a rigorous negative control establishing that any perturbation does not suffice — the slow-path must be a coarser temporal view of the same acoustic content. - Decode-step latency overhead is genuinely zero in practice (0.99×), making TCD deployable as a drop-in inference wrapper without pipeline changes. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly state: TCD requires temporally aligned decoder access and does not generalize to bottleneck/compressed-encoder architectures; the dual forward pass adds prefill latency reducible with KV-cache reuse or selective-step application; heavy audio downsampling limits temporal contrast room; streaming settings are future work. These are confronted directly and honestly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All evaluations are multiple-choice QA on short audio clips; open-ended generation is never tested, leaving open whether TCD introduces artifacts or produces over-amplification of transient cues in free-form responses. - γ_gate is set once per backbone with no principled selection criterion beyond "matching attention sharpness," no sensitivity analysis, and no guidance for a new backbone — this is an underdocumented per-model tuning step that complicates deployment. - No error bars or significance tests are reported; the +1.5 gain on Qwen2-Audio MMAU test-mini and several domain-level results could plausibly fall within noise, and the test-mini split size is not stated in the paper. - The paper compares against AAD but not against simpler inference-time alternatives (temperature scaling, attention re-weighting, beam search), which might partially address the same uncertain-step problem at lower engineering cost. - The large CochlScene (+7.7) and SLURP (+6.0) gains come from tasks embedded within the same AIR-Bench evaluation suite used for the main Foundation results — the test sets are not fully independent, and selecting tasks to highlight post-hoc is not flagged. - Audio duration range in the main benchmarks is unstated; the claim that prefill overhead is a "minor fraction" for long-form generation is asserted but not measured. Bottom Line A researcher building or studying unified LALMs gains a well-ablated, deployable inference-time technique with a clean mechanistic story and honest scope characterization; practitioners using bottleneck encoders, requiring open-ended generation quality guarantees, or working in low-latency streaming settings will find the paper's evidence too narrow to justify adoption.
Recommended 2604.15383 PDF
#20 — Innovative (74)

Scepsy: Serving Agentic Workflows Using Aggregate LLM Pipelines

arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Scepsy, a GPU cluster scheduling system for multi-LLM agentic workflows that jointly optimizes fractional GPU allocation, tensor parallelism degrees, and replica counts via a lightweight Aggregate LLM Pipeline abstraction built from execution traces. - Why it's important: Self-managed GPU clusters serving agentic workflows waste resources and incur high latency because existing systems either optimize each LLM in isolation (missing cross-LLM dependencies) or require custom programming models and leave GPU allocation to users. - How it does it: Exploits the empirical observation that per-LLM execution-time fractions are up to 4× more stable across requests than absolute latencies, enabling a pipeline-style throughput/latency predictor that drives a three-dimensional GPU allocation search without per-candidate profiling. - The good: Up to 27× latency reduction and 2.4× throughput improvement over Kubernetes autoscaler, and up to 14.1× latency and 7.3× throughput improvement over Aegaeon (SOSP'25), on a 16-GPU on-premise cluster with real workloads. - The missing: All evaluations use 1B–8B parameter models on a single 16-GPU cluster; profiling overhead is never quantified; the Ayo comparison uses an older vLLM version (v0.2.2 vs. v0.17 for Scepsy), potentially inflating advantage. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML systems researchers and infrastructure engineers building multi-LLM serving platforms; skippable if your deployment is single-LLM or if you operate hyperscaler-scale infrastructure.
Why this rank: Systems profile. The Aggregate LLM Pipeline abstraction — exploiting empirically-stable per-LLM time fractions to convert a control-flow-aware scheduling problem into a tractable pipeline allocation problem — is genuinely non-obvious (Non-Obviousness 80) and the joint 3D search over fractional GPU × TP × replicas is novel in this space (Novelty 75). Rigor (65) is solid for systems: clean ablation isolating co-location vs. TP, current SOSP'25/EuroSys'25 baselines, but the Ayo vLLM v0.2.2 vs. v0.17 mismatch and lack of error bars/profiling-overhead measurement are real concerns. Impact (80) is high — the system is a deployed 25K-LOC artifact promised public. Completeness (70) and Resilience (65) are decent given honest limitations section. Weighted: 0.15*75 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*80 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.30*80 = 73.
systems Novelty 75 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Operators of small-to-medium on-premise GPU clusters need to serve agentic workflows that chain multiple heterogeneous LLMs — generators, verifiers, embedders, rerankers — with data-dependent branching, fan-out, and looping. End-to-end latency for a single beam-search request can range from 9 to 264 seconds depending on input, with invocation counts ranging from 24 to 844. Existing multi-LLM systems (AlpaServe, MuxServe, Aegaeon) treat each LLM as an independent service and miss cross-LLM resource coupling; workflow-aware systems (Parrot, Ayo, Autellix) exploit structure but require custom programming models and leave GPU allocation to users. No prior system jointly allocates GPUs across heterogeneous LLMs for arbitrary-framework workflows while co-optimizing latency and throughput. Key Idea While absolute per-LLM execution times vary enormously across requests, the fraction of total workflow execution time spent in each LLM is substantially more stable (empirically up to 4× lower variance, shown in Fig. 3). Because workflow LLMs are causally coupled, they are affected similarly by per-request cost. This relative stability means a simplified pipeline abstraction — where each LLM contributes a latency term scaled by its average invocation count and parallelism, and throughput is governed by the bottleneck LLM — can faithfully predict workflow performance under different GPU allocations without modeling explicit control flow. Methodology Scepsy builds and uses Aggregate LLM Pipelines in four steps. (1) Workflow tracing: an HTTP proxy intercepts completions API calls to each LLM serving engine, recording request/response contents and timestamps engine-agnostically, without framework-specific instrumentation. (2) Statistical aggregation: for each LLM m, compute average invocations per workflow request n_m and average request-level parallelism p_m from overlapping timestamps in traces. (3) LLM profiling: replay all traced LLM-level requests at varying arrival rates and multiple tensor parallelism degrees (TP=1,2,4), sweeping to saturation, to build empirical throughput–latency profiles per LLM. (4) ALP synthesis: transform per-LLM profiles to workflow-level units using L_wm(λ_w) = L_m(λ_w · n_m) · n_m/p_m for latency and T_w = min_m(T_m/n_m) for throughput. Predictions then require only profile lookups and arithmetic, making exploration of large allocation spaces cheap. The GPU scheduler performs a three-step search: enumerate fractional GPU assignments ordered by LLM latency ratios (proxy for compute demand) with contiguity and TP-limit pruning; pack fractions onto physical GPUs contiguously; resolve TP degree × replica count combinations such that TP × replicas evenly divides the assigned GPU count. Search time grows exponentially but stays below 70s for 128 GPUs with 3 LLMs in micro-benchmarks. Topology-aware placement uses a most-constrained-first heuristic: place large TP-requiring models into NVLink domains first (inter-node), then pack smaller fractions within nodes (intra-node). Realized via custom Kubernetes device plugin; Nvidia MPS enforces SM/memory limits per fraction. Three critical design decisions: (i) latency ratio as compute-demand proxy — reasonable approximation but not theoretically grounded; (ii) fractional GPU granularity (10 fractions/GPU) — well-justified to avoid over-provisioning small models; (iii) contiguity-only allocation — justified by symmetry exploitation but may miss valid non-contiguous placements. Key Results Cluster: 4 nodes × 4 NVIDIA RTX A6000 GPUs (PCIe 4.0 intra-node, 3rd-gen NVLink between GPU pairs, 100 Gbps InfiniBand inter-node). Workloads: RAG+reranker (e5-base-v2 + Llama-3-8B) and beam search (LLaMA-3.2-1B + LLaMA-3.1-8B-PRM). vs. Kubernetes HPA (autoscaler): beam search, 4 GPUs — 2.4× throughput, 1.4×–7.6× latency improvement; 16 GPUs — 1.8× throughput, 1.7×–10.4× latency. RAG+reranker, 16 GPUs — 1.2× throughput, 1.4×–27× latency improvement. vs. Aegaeon (multi-LLM multiplexing, SOSP'25): beam search, 4 GPUs — 7.3× throughput, 14.1× latency; 8 GPUs — 6.8× throughput, 12.9× latency. RAG, 4 GPUs — 2.5× throughput, 3.4× latency. Every Aegaeon prefill/decode split configuration loses to Scepsy. vs. Ayo (workflow-aware, EuroSys'25, using vLLM v0.2.2 for compatibility): beam search, 8 GPUs — 8.2× throughput; RAG, 4/8 GPUs — 2.1×/2.4× throughput. Ablation (§7.4): fractional co-location is the primary driver for RAG improvements; tensor parallelism is the primary driver for beam search latency. Both are required for full performance. A counter-intuitive finding: request-level scheduling optimizations (Ayo's primary mechanism) matter less than resource allocation at higher loads. Baselines are current and strong for the systems domain (Aegaeon from SOSP'25, Ayo from EuroSys'25). Scale is modest (16 GPUs, 1B–8B models). Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The Aggregate LLM Pipeline abstraction — treating relative per-LLM time fractions as stable, workflow-level schedulable signals — is genuinely novel. The three-dimensional joint search over fractional allocation, tensor parallelism, and replica count for multi-LLM workflows has not been done before. Individual components (HTTP proxy tracing, LLM profiling, pipeline modeling, Kubernetes deployment, MPS enforcement) are standard; the novelty lies in their integration under the relative-statistics insight and the unified scheduling formulation. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation study present (§7.4) with four configurations clearly decomposing contributions. No error bars or statistical significance tests on throughput/latency curves; single-run results are reported. Code promised post-acceptance at github.com/anon/Scepsy (anonymous placeholder, not yet public at time of submission). Two methodological concerns: (1) The Ayo comparison uses vLLM v0.2.2 while Scepsy uses v0.17 — vLLM made substantial throughput improvements across these versions, and the version mismatch is acknowledged but not corrected with a fair-comparison experiment. (2) Profiling overhead (time to trace + profile all LLMs before deployment) is never measured or reported, which is operationally significant for dynamic workloads. No evaluation under workload distribution shift. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built tool (25K Python LOC) evaluated by deployment. - Practical: Runs on commodity on-premise hardware using standard Kubernetes, vLLM, and SGLang; requires Nvidia MPS support and a custom device plugin, both straightforward to deploy. - Efficient: Fractional GPU allocation demonstrably reduces over-provisioning of small LLMs; profiling overhead is unquantified, creating an operational blind spot for workloads that change frequently. - Effective: End-to-end experiments show real latency and throughput improvements on the stated problem with current baselines; the Ayo engine-version mismatch partially undermines the workflow-aware comparison. - Scalable: Scheduler search scales to 128-GPU simulations in under 70s; actual deployment evaluation is limited to 16 GPUs with 1B–8B models, and behavior with 30B+ models requiring multi-GPU loading is not demonstrated. Delivers a software system (not yet public). Strengths 1. The relative-stability observation (Fig. 3) is non-obvious and empirically grounded: it turns an apparently intractable workflow scheduling problem into a tractable pipeline allocation problem without requiring control-flow analysis. 2. HTTP-proxy tracing achieves genuine framework agnosticism — any agentic framework that speaks the completions API is supported without instrumentation hooks, a significant practical advantage over Parrot/Ayo/TokenCake. 3. Joint three-dimensional search (fractional GPU × TP × replicas) closes a real gap: prior systems optimize at most one dimension, leading to systematic resource misallocation for heterogeneous LLM mixes. 4. The ablation (§7.4) cleanly demonstrates that fractional co-location and tensor parallelism are complementary — co-location helps throughput for heterogeneous-size workflows (RAG), TP helps latency for compute-heavy workflows (beam search) — providing genuine insight rather than just confirming that more features help. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly identify three limitations in §9: (1) cross-LLM fan-out (different LLMs running in parallel) is modeled as serial, causing latency over-estimation and potential suboptimal TP allocation for the non-critical-path LLM; (2) an LLM reused in multiple roles induces bimodal load distributions that a single aggregate profile misrepresents; (3) long-running tool executions (database queries, experiment monitors) are discarded from the ALP, potentially causing large prediction errors when tools dominate end-to-end latency. The authors propose concrete extensions for each. This direct acknowledgment is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The Ayo comparison uses vLLM v0.2.2 for Ayo vs. v0.17 for Scepsy; the paper acknowledges this but offers no corrective experiment. Given vLLM's performance trajectory, the 8.2× beam-search throughput advantage over Ayo may be substantially inflated by the engine version gap. - Profiling overhead is never measured: the paper does not report how long tracing + per-LLM profiling takes before an allocation can be deployed, nor how often re-profiling is needed as workloads shift. For organizations with rapidly changing agentic pipelines, this is a deployment-blocking question. - All models are 1B–8B; no experiment involves a model that requires tensor parallelism just to fit in GPU memory (e.g., 70B). The TP search logic has not been stress-tested in a regime where TP is forced rather than optional. - The cluster is a single homogeneous 4×4 topology with symmetric NVLink pairs; the placement heuristic's effectiveness on asymmetric or multi-hop topologies common in larger clusters is untested. - No measurement of allocation re-computation latency when arrival rates change dynamically; §7.3 shows co-serving two workflows but not how quickly Scepsy re-adapts when one workflow's demand spikes. Bottom Line ML systems researchers and infrastructure engineers building multi-LLM serving platforms will find the Aggregate LLM Pipeline abstraction and the joint allocation framework both practically grounded and non-obvious, making this a worthwhile read; deployment teams should independently verify Ayo comparisons on a common engine version and benchmark profiling overhead before committing to Scepsy for dynamic production workloads.
Recommended 2604.15186 PDF
#21 — Innovative (74)

Thermal-GEMs: Generalized Models for Building Thermal Dynamics

arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces the first comprehensive empirical comparison of multi-source general models (MSGMs) versus time series foundation models (TSFMs) for building indoor temperature forecasting, validated on real-world data across three datasets and four MSGM architectures, and identifies a threshold of 16 same-sensor source buildings for MSGMs to consistently outperform TSFMs. - Why it's important: Buildings require months-to-years of sensor data to fit accurate thermal models, yet each building is unique — an unsolved cold-start problem that blocks deployment of MPC and fault-detection systems in real estates. - How it does it: MSGMs are trained on shuffled windows from up to 200 heterogeneous source buildings and applied zero-shot (or with minimal fine-tuning) to unseen target buildings, with a seasonal four-split evaluation scheme that averages performance across spring, summer, autumn, and winter. - The good: MSGMs reduce average MAE by 33–63% over single-source TL across all 40 real-world Ecobee target buildings, with larger gains in lower-data regimes, and the threshold experiment (1–128 source buildings) provides a directly actionable deployment decision rule. - The missing: TSFMs are tested exclusively in zero-shot mode — no LoRA or curriculum fine-tuning is evaluated, which prior work has shown changes the ranking, leaving the MSGM vs. fine-tuned TSFM comparison unaddressed. - Read it or not: Practitioners deploying building energy management systems and researchers working on data-efficient time series forecasting for the built environment should read this; it provides concrete, actionable guidance on when to choose an MSGM over a TSFM based on available source buildings.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (75) is solid — four-season evaluation split, real-world datasets, hyperparameter tuning across architectures, code promised — but undermined by best-of-4 reporting in the critical low-source regime and no significance tests. Novelty (65) reflects a genuine first real-world MSGM validation and first MSGM-vs-TSFM head-to-head, though built from existing components. Non-Obviousness (75) high — the 16-building threshold and the counter-intuitive fine-tuning-hurts finding are surprising and actionable. Completeness (75) and Resilience (70) strong with directly acknowledged limitations. Impact (75) reflects an actionable deployment decision rule and trained weights, though no end-user library. Weighted: 0.20*65 + 0.25*75 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*75 = 72.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 75 Resilience 70 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Building operators who wish to deploy model-predictive control or fault-detection systems face a universal cold-start problem: each building has its own thermal signature driven by construction year, envelope parameters, occupancy, climate zone, and HVAC configuration, yet accurate ML-based thermal models require months to years of logged sensor data that does not exist at installation time. Transfer learning from a single source building mitigates this partially but inherits sensitivity to source-target mismatch. Multi-source general models (MSGMs) trained across many buildings promise better generalization, but prior work (Raisch et al. 2025) demonstrated MSGMs only on synthetic data. Independently, time series foundation models pre-trained on large heterogeneous corpora have been proposed as an alternative for sparse-data settings, but no head-to-head comparison between MSGMs and TSFMs for building thermal dynamics existed, and the practically critical question — how many same-sensor source buildings does a deployer need before MSGMs beat TSFMs — was unanswered. Key Idea: The core insight is that a model pre-trained on shuffled sliding windows from hundreds of heterogeneous buildings learns a general-purpose thermal dynamics representation that transfers zero-shot to unseen buildings better than both single-building pre-training and domain-agnostic TSFMs, because the MSGM is exclusively exposed to building physics while TSFMs are diluted across unrelated domains. The secondary insight is that this advantage has a data threshold: below ~16 same-sensor buildings, the MSGM's domain specificity cannot overcome the TSFM's breadth. Methodology: The pipeline compares three strategies — single-source TL, MSGM, and zero-shot TSFM — on 1-hour-ahead indoor temperature forecasting (4 × 15-minute steps, 96-step lookback). Three datasets are used: D1 EcoBee (real-world, 932 North American homes, 5 climate zones), D2 Ideal Households (real-world, 32 UK homes, 255 rooms), D3 European Households (non-public simulated, 450 Central European homes built 1949–present). Each dataset is split into source (88–96%) and target (4–11%) buildings; the target set is stratified by location. Data is downsampled to 15-minute intervals. Four MSGM architectures are evaluated: LSTM (baseline from prior work), standard Transformer with cosine positional embedding, Mamba (structured state-space model), and xLSTM. Hyperparameters are tuned per architecture per dataset using Bayesian optimization with Optuna; each configuration is trained 4 times and the best validation-RMSE model is selected. Source training uses up to 200 time series (180 train / 20 val), AdamW optimizer. Three TSFMs are evaluated zero-shot: Chronos-2 (encoder Transformer, synthetic pretraining), TimesFM 2.5 (decoder-only autoregressive, univariate mode), Toto (decoder-only, 151M parameters, 2T training points). Evaluation follows a four-way seasonal fine-tuning split: each year is partitioned into spring/summer/autumn/winter segments, each with a variable training set (0.5–2.5 months), 0.5-month validation, and 3-month test. Final MAE and RMSE are averaged across four seasons per target building. Three critical design decisions: (1) weight initialization (not layer freezing) for fine-tuning — justified by prior ablation in [38]; (2) zero-shot deployment mode for MSGMs on real-world data, motivated by the finding that fine-tuning increases error; (3) reporting best-of-4 runs for MSGM with ≤16 sources in the sparsity experiment — favorably biases MSGM in low-source regime. Key Results: Architecture ablation (zero-shot, all-in-one scenario): LSTM and Transformer outperform Mamba and xLSTM; Mamba is the clear outlier. Architecture choice among LSTM/Transformer/xLSTM has minor effect. Best architecture is dataset-dependent: Transformer for European Households, LSTM for EcoBee and Ideal. Average 1-hour MAE is ~0.1 K on Ideal and Euro datasets, ~0.25 K on EcoBee. MSGM vs. single-source TL (EcoBee, real-world): MSGM outperforms single-source TL on all 40 target buildings across all four data-sparsity scenarios. Zero-shot: MSGM MAE 0.254 K vs. single-source 0.683 K (63% reduction); RMSE 0.382 vs. 1.089 K (65% reduction). With 2.5 months of fine-tuning data, single-source TL MAE drops to 0.475 K, still well above the zero-shot MSGM at 0.269 K. Surprisingly, fine-tuning the MSGM on real-world data consistently increases error regardless of fine-tuning data volume; the authors hypothesize seasonal overfitting but do not verify this. MSGM vs. TSFMs (all three datasets, zero-shot): MSGM outperforms all three TSFMs on average across all datasets. TSFMs exceed the MSGM only on 12.5% of EcoBee buildings and 8% of Euro buildings by MAE; they lose on all Ideal buildings. Chronos wins best average rank across TSFMs and datasets. Unexpectedly, univariate TimesFM outperforms multivariate Chronos and Toto on the Ideal dataset — the authors attribute this to multivariate TSFMs failing to capture building thermal inertia from heating power signals. Same-sensor building sparsity (1–128 sources, 2× power steps): MSGM performance improves monotonically with source count, with diminishing returns above 64 sources. MSGM crosses the TSFM baseline at 16 sources (EcoBee, Euro) and 32 sources (Ideal) for MAE; at only 2–8 sources for RMSE. The Ideal dataset requires more sources because rooms from the same building introduce within-building homogeneity that limits diversity benefit. Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW BENCHMARK EVALUATION. The genuine novelty is the first real-world validation of multi-source TL for building thermal dynamics and the first head-to-head MSGM vs. TSFM comparison on this task. The source-count threshold experiment is a novel empirical contribution with direct practical value. The architectures, TSFMs, and transfer learning protocol are all drawn from existing work; this paper's contribution is the empirical synthesis and the actionable threshold finding. Rigor & Reproducibility: The seasonal fine-tuning split with 4-way averaging is a meaningful robustness improvement over single-season evaluations in prior work. Models are trained 4 times from different random seeds; the best is selected rather than averaged, which inflates reported MSGM performance. No statistical significance tests or confidence intervals are reported for cross-building comparisons, making it impossible to judge whether the MSGM's wins on individual buildings are meaningful or noise. The EcoBee target set is only 40 buildings (4.3% of available data). For the source-count experiment with ≤16 sources, best-of-4-trials reporting selects the luckiest random sample, creating an optimistic bias for MSGMs in the critical low-data regime. Code is promised on GitHub [anonymous — anonymized for review]. The European Households dataset is non-public, limiting full reproducibility for that split. Both real-world datasets (EcoBee, Ideal) are publicly available. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL — multiple trained models evaluated across real and simulated building datasets. - Practical: Training runs on a single H100 GPU (94 GB VRAM); inference for MSGMs takes 0.0002–0.0004s per forecast, well within 15-minute control cycle requirements for real deployment. - Efficient: MSGMs are 100× faster at inference than TSFMs (0.03–0.05s per forecast for TSFMs vs. sub-millisecond for MSGMs); MSGM pretraining takes 1.2–20.4 hours depending on architecture and dataset, a one-time cost amortized over many target buildings. - Effective: Evidence on real-world buildings (EcoBee, Ideal) shows that zero-shot MSGMs substantially outperform single-source TL and TSFMs for 1-hour temperature forecasting; the surprising finding that fine-tuning hurts performance on real-world data is unexplained and limits confident deployment of the fine-tuning pathway. - Scalable: Only tested up to 128 source buildings and 1-hour forecast horizons; longer prediction horizons needed for MPC (typically 6–24 hours) are not evaluated and represent an untested assumption. The work delivers trained model weights (promised via GitHub) but no software library or CLI tool for end-user deployment. Strengths: - First real-world validation closes the critical gap left by prior work (Raisch et al. 2025) that relied on simulated data only, substantially strengthening the case for MSGM deployment. - The source-count threshold finding (16 buildings for MAE, 2–8 for RMSE) gives practitioners a concrete, actionable decision rule that was previously absent from the literature. - The four-season evaluation design is a genuine methodological improvement over prior work that ignores seasonal effects; averaging across all four seasons reduces cherry-picking risk. - The zero-shot MSGM fine-tuning degradation finding is a counter-intuitive and practically important result not previously reported in the building thermal dynamics literature. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) TSFMs were tested in zero-shot only and naive fine-tuning increases errors, leaving advanced fine-tuning (LoRA, curriculum learning) as future work; (2) single-source TL experiments were capped at 3 months of fine-tuning data; (3) same-sensor building sparsity constrains MSGM applicability, and feature-invariant MSGMs that handle heterogeneous sensors are a future direction. These are genuine limitations confronted directly, which adds credibility. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The explanation for fine-tuning degradation (seasonal overfitting) is a post-hoc hypothesis; no ablation disentangles seasonal overfitting from distribution shift or regularization mismatch, and this finding fundamentally affects the practical deployment recommendation. - Reporting the best of 4 random source samples for ≤16-source MSGM experiments introduces optimistic bias precisely where the result is most commercially sensitive; median-of-4 would be more defensible. - Forecast horizon is fixed at 1 hour (4 × 15-minute steps); practical MPC requires 6–24-hour horizons, and the relative performance of MSGMs vs. TSFMs likely changes at longer horizons where TSFMs' broader temporal representations may become more competitive. - The Ideal dataset's source-count anomaly (needing more sources due to within-building room homogeneity) exposes that the diversity measure for source buildings is building count rather than thermal diversity — a potentially misleading metric when rooms and buildings are mixed. - No significance testing is applied to per-building win rates (12.5% TSFM wins on EcoBee), making it unclear whether those wins are systematic or random. - Single-source TL uses LSTM only, not the best MSGM architecture per dataset; it is possible the architecture choice, not the multi-source pretraining, drives part of the observed gap. Bottom Line: Any practitioner deploying data-driven building thermal models who can assemble 16+ same-sensor buildings for pretraining will find specific, real-world-validated guidance here — but the unexplained fine-tuning degradation and the absence of longer-horizon and fine-tuned-TSFM evaluations mean the deployment recipe is still incomplete.
Citations 2604.16443 PDF
#22 — Innovative (73)

An Agentic Approach to Metadata Reasoning

arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces the Metadata Reasoner, an LLM-orchestrated agentic system that selects a sufficient and minimal set of tables from an enterprise data lake to answer a given analytical task. - Why it's important: Ranking-based retrieval (vector search, BM25, Pneuma) scores tables individually and cannot reason about multi-table combinations, join validity, data quality, or lineage — causing it to fail when tasks require integrating several sources from noisy, large-scale data lakes. - How it does it: The system stages metadata across three tiers — discrimination-oriented vector embeddings for initial candidate retrieval, LLM-generated statistical summaries attached at retrieval time, and on-demand tool calls (data_finder, joinability_check, column_profiler) — so the LLM agent consumes only the metadata fragments needed at each reasoning step. - The good: On KramaBench, the system achieves 83.16% average F1 vs. 50.77% for vector search and 45.12% for Pneuma, with the gap widest in the 1,498-table Astronomy domain (72.31% vs. 32.80%); downstream Text-to-SQL accuracy improves from 56.38% to 71.28%. - The missing: Inference cost and latency of the multi-step agentic loop are never reported, making it impossible to assess the efficiency trade-off against single-pass retrieval baselines. - Read it or not: Database engineers and agentic-systems researchers building data discovery pipelines should read it; the three-tier metadata staging design and the failure-case taxonomy are both concrete and actionable.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (78) strong — ablations across four variants with five seeds and standard deviations, KramaBench is external, baselines tuned to best K. Novelty (70) — three-tier metadata staging is principled integration rather than a new problem. Non-Obviousness (75) — the discrimination-oriented contrastive embedding insight and the empirical demonstration that staged access beats both full upfront and pure on-demand are non-obvious. Completeness (70) and Resilience (68) pulled down by missing cost/latency reporting (10 LLM calls per query unquantified), proprietary Gemini-3-Flash dependency with no open-weights ablation, synthetic BIRD circularity, and SQL-experiment confound (justification leak). Impact (75) — concrete reusable techniques, working ADK implementation, but no public artifact released. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*78 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*68 + 0.10*75 ≈ 73.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 68 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Enterprise data lakes contain thousands of heterogeneous tables. When an LLM agent must answer an analytical question (SQL query, ML prediction task), it must first identify the right subset of tables. The problem is harder than single-document retrieval: the agent must find combinations of tables that jointly cover all required entities and attributes, verify that tables can be joined correctly, reason about data quality and lineage, and do so without overwhelming the context window. Existing approaches — keyword-based retrieval, vector search, or hybrid systems like Pneuma — rank individual tables by topic relevance and cannot capture whether a retrieved set is jointly sufficient, joinable, or free of quality defects. Expanding the context window to include all metadata is computationally prohibitive and degrades reasoning quality due to attention dilution in long contexts. Key Idea: The central insight is that different types of metadata are optimal at different stages of reasoning: coarse vector retrieval needs discrimination-oriented embeddings that highlight inter-table differences rather than shared high-level descriptions; per-table reasoning needs attached statistical summaries; and join/value verification needs on-demand tool calls. By routing each metadata type to the stage where it is most informative, the agent avoids both context saturation and the latency of precomputing all pairwise metadata signals (e.g., all join paths). Methodology: The pipeline has four stages. (1) Query decomposition: a Gemini-3-Flash LLM agent decomposes the analytical task into named entities, measures, temporal scope, and granularity constraints, producing a structured search plan. (2) Discrimination-oriented semantic search: metadata embeddings are constructed via a two-stage process — tables are grouped by semantic relatedness, and an LLM generates group-relative descriptions that emphasize distinguishing features (e.g., specific temporal ranges for geomagnetic forecast tables with identical schemas). A state-aware deduplication mechanism suppresses repeated metadata from tables already surfaced, replacing them with recurrence indicators. (3) Reasoning over attached metadata: each retrieved table is accompanied by schema information plus LLM-generated statistical summaries (min/max, top-K values, null ratios, cardinality). (4) On-the-fly tool calls: three specialized tools are invoked when attached metadata is insufficient — column_profiler() for fine-grained column histograms, data_finder() for deterministic value-level presence verification, and joinability_check() for foreign-key overlap between specific candidate pairs. The agent iterates between search and tool calls until it declares a sufficient and minimal set. Critical design decisions: the partition between attached vs. on-the-fly metadata is explicitly tunable (and validated by ablation); the search engine itself uses the same high-performance vector engine (text-embedding-005 with semantic reranking) as the baselines, so improvements are attributable to the agentic reasoning layer, not embedding quality alone. Key Results: On KramaBench (6 real-world domains, up to 1,498 tables), the full system achieves 83.16% average F1 (84.44% recall, 86.63% precision) using an average of 10.10 reasoning steps per task, versus 50.77% for vector search and 45.12% for Pneuma. The gain is largest in the high-cardinality Astronomy domain: 72.31% vs. 32.80% (vector) and 27.70% (Pneuma). On a synthetically scaled BIRD data lake with injected partitions, duplicates, and quality degradation (up to 208 tables per database), the system achieves 85.5% average F1 vs. 30.0% for Top-10 vector retrieval; 99.0% of selected tables are noise-free vs. 64.4% for the baseline. In a downstream Text-to-SQL experiment, MR-selected tables yield 71.28% SQL execution accuracy vs. 56.38% for Top-10 vector results across five BIRD databases, with individual gains ranging from +8.62 to +21.47 points. Ablation confirms that attached metadata provides the largest single improvement (73.43% → 79.66% F1) while simultaneously reducing steps from 13.51 to 8.36; adding on-the-fly tools without attached metadata causes steps to nearly triple (24.14) due to trial-and-error search. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The orchestration of three staged metadata tiers within an agentic loop for data-source selection is the primary contribution; discrimination-oriented embedding construction (group-aware meta-prompting to generate contrastive table descriptions) is a secondary technical novelty. Individual components — LLM tool use, vector search, statistical table profiling — are known; the contribution lies in their principled integration and the empirical demonstration that staged metadata access is both more accurate and more efficient than providing all metadata upfront or on-demand alone. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations cover four system variants and are run over five seeds with standard deviations reported. Baselines include a strong vector-search engine (text-embedding-005 with semantic reranker) and Pneuma (a recent LLM-hybrid system), both evaluated at their best retrieval threshold K — this is a fair comparison protocol. KramaBench is an external benchmark not created by the authors, providing independent evaluation. The synthetic BIRD dataset is author-created, which introduces Goodhart's Law risk: the noise types injected (lineage suffixes, quality flags) are precisely those the system is designed to reason about, so strong performance on this benchmark may partly reflect design alignment rather than general robustness. No code, model weights, or data are stated as publicly released. The backbone model (Gemini-3-Flash) is a proprietary Google model referenced via an arXiv preprint, making exact reproduction impossible. Inference cost and latency are not reported, which is a significant omission for an agentic system that makes multiple LLM calls per task. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: Requires cloud API access to a large LLM (Gemini-3-Flash) and embedding model (text-embedding-005); not deployable on commodity on-premise hardware without equivalent API infrastructure. - Efficient: Average 10.10 LLM calls per task for the full system; the paper does not report wall-clock time or token cost, making it impossible to quantify the efficiency overhead relative to single-pass retrieval baselines. - Effective: The evidence is strong on both benchmarks — 32-point F1 gain over the best baseline on KramaBench, 99% noise-free table selection on synthetic BIRD, and a 14.9-point average gain in downstream SQL execution accuracy. - Scalable: The largest tested environment is 1,498 tables (Astronomy domain); enterprise data warehouses commonly contain tens of thousands of tables. Scalability beyond ~1,500 tables is claimed but not tested. The system exists as a working implementation using the Google ADK framework but is not released as a public artifact. Strengths: - The three-tier metadata staging design is principled and directly motivated by the context saturation problem; the ablation provides clean evidence that each tier contributes independently, with attached metadata providing the best accuracy-efficiency trade-off. - Discrimination-oriented embedding construction (group-aware meta-prompting to generate contrastive descriptions) is a concrete, reusable technique that yields +15 point Recall@5 gains in the Astronomy domain vs. schema-only embeddings at no inference-time cost. - Using KramaBench as the primary benchmark (external, multi-domain, real-world data lake with up to 1,498 tables) provides credible independent evidence; the 32-point margin over Pneuma is large and consistent across domains. - The failure-case taxonomy in Section 5.6 (5 failure modes: missing relation dependencies, granularity mismatch, multi-stage planning failure, redundant relation selection, incomplete partition retrieval) is analytically rigorous and provides a clear agenda for follow-on work. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly analyze 126 failure cases from KramaBench and attribute them to five concrete failure modes (Section 5.6). They acknowledge limitations in handling multi-stage dependent query planning, granularity inference, exhaustive partition retrieval, and redundant relation filtering. Future work directions include unstructured data support, web search integration, and metadata quality improvement. The authors do not acknowledge the cost/latency issue, the synthetic benchmark circularity risk, or the replication barrier posed by the proprietary backbone. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Inference cost not quantified: the agentic loop averages 10.10 LLM calls per query; comparing to baselines that make one call without reporting token budget or latency makes the efficiency trade-off opaque and is the most important missing analysis for practitioners. - Synthetic BIRD benchmark circularity: the authors designed the noise injection scheme and the system's quality-reasoning tools simultaneously; strong performance on this benchmark is weaker evidence than performance on an independently constructed noisy benchmark. - Proprietary backbone: all experiments use Gemini-3-Flash (and Gemini-3-Pro for SQL generation), which is unreleased; no ablation with open-weights models (e.g., Llama, Mistral) is provided, making it unknown whether the approach generalizes beyond Google's models. - No human upper-bound on KramaBench: 83.16% F1 is uncontextualized — it is unclear whether the remaining 17% error reflects inherent task ambiguity or system fixable failures. - Scale gap: the 1,498-table Astronomy domain is the largest tested; there is no evidence the approach holds at 10,000–100,000 table scale typical of large enterprise data warehouses, where the planning and deduplication logic may not scale. - Downstream SQL experiment confounds table selection with justification quality: the MR configuration also passes the natural-language justification to the SQL generator, which is not available in the vector search condition, potentially inflating the downstream accuracy improvement attributable to table selection alone. Bottom Line: This is a solid systems paper with a principled design and convincing empirical results on real-world benchmarks; database engineers building LLM-powered data discovery pipelines will find both the three-tier metadata staging architecture and the discrimination-oriented embedding construction directly applicable, but the absence of cost analysis and the reliance on unreleased proprietary models leave the most important deployment questions unanswered.
Citations 2604.20144 PDF
#23 — Innovative (72)

Bringing Value Models Back: Generative Critics for Value Modeling in LLM Reinforcement Learning

arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Generative Actor-Critic (GenAC), a method that replaces conventional discriminative (one-shot scalar) value critics in PPO-style LLM RL with a generative critic that performs chain-of-thought reasoning before predicting a value estimate, achieving stronger credit assignment and downstream RL performance. - Why it's important: As LLM post-training pushes toward longer-horizon, multi-step reasoning and agentic systems, accurate token-level credit assignment becomes the binding constraint on performance — and the community's current workaround (value-free GRPO/RLOO) sacrifices that precision by assigning uniform advantage across entire trajectories. - How it does it: The critic retains the autoregressive language modeling head and generates explicit CoT reasoning about partial-solution quality before emitting an integer score (0–10, normalized to [0,1]), exploiting the theoretical result that autoregressive intermediate computation expands expressive power beyond the TC^0 complexity class that limits one-shot scalar predictors. - The good: Generative critics achieve 52.5% lower approximation MSE than discriminative critics on the out-of-distribution AIME24 benchmark and maintain a widening accuracy margin over all baselines (GRPO, RLOO, VC-PPO) as training progresses beyond 120 steps on six math reasoning benchmarks. - The missing: RL experiments are conducted only at 8B parameter scale on math reasoning tasks; the claim that the benefits generalize to agentic settings and larger scales is entirely unsubstantiated by experiment. - Read it or not: Researchers working on LLM post-training with PPO-style methods, especially those targeting long-horizon reasoning or agentic applications, should read this — it provides both a principled theoretical diagnosis for why learned value models fail and a concrete, working remedy.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (72) pulled down by absence of seed variance on main RL Table 1, dependence on GPT-5 SFT oracle, and single 8B-scale evaluation; ablations and approximation-scaling experiments are otherwise solid. Novelty (75) — generative critic for value modeling (vs. reward modeling) plus a real complexity-class diagnosis. Non-Obviousness (75) — TC^0 vs. P framing is non-trivial. Completeness (70) and Resilience (68) limited by math-only, 8B-only evidence with broad-generalization claims. Impact (65) reflects no public release. Weighted ≈ 71.7 → 72.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 68 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: In LLM RL post-training, sparse outcome rewards are converted to dense, token-level advantage estimates via a learned value function, enabling more informative training signals than trajectory-level averaging. However, value-based PPO methods have fallen out of favor in practice because discriminative critics — models with a linear projection head mapping hidden states directly to a scalar — are unreliable and fail to improve consistently with model size. Practitioners have retreated to value-free methods like GRPO and RLOO, which avoid this problem by treating entire trajectories as contextual bandits. This simplification caps the performance ceiling for complex, multi-step tasks because it assigns uniform credit to all tokens regardless of where mistakes occur. The gap the paper addresses is: why do discriminative critics fail, and can an architectural fix make value models reliably useful? Key Idea: Discriminative critics fail not merely due to optimization difficulty but due to a fundamental expressiveness mismatch: computing value functions for sparse-reward language-generation MDPs can be P-complete, while log-precision Transformers with constant depth are confined to TC^0, which is widely believed to be strictly weaker than P under the assumption TC^0 ≠ P. Since one-shot scalar prediction is a single fixed-depth forward pass, it inherits this expressiveness ceiling regardless of model size. The key insight is that autoregressive chain-of-thought generation strictly expands transformer expressive power (per theoretical work on CoT), so a generative critic that reasons step-by-step before emitting a score can, in principle, compute value functions that one-shot predictors fundamentally cannot represent. This makes the architectural change theoretically grounded rather than heuristic. Methodology: GenAC frames language generation as a token/segment-level MDP. The generative critic retains the LM head and generates a CoT reasoning trace conditioned on the problem prompt and a partial actor response, concluding with an integer score 0–10 parsed into [0,1]. Responses are segmented into ~120-token chunks using delimiter heuristics; the critic predicts a value at each segment boundary, which is broadcast to token-level for GAE with λ=1. In-Context Conditioning (ICC) injects policy descriptors — model size and an exponential moving average of the actor's success rate — directly into the critic's prompt template, allowing policy calibration without requiring all policy information to be encoded in weights. The training pipeline has three stages: (1) SFT on GPT-5-synthesized reasoning traces to instill CoT format and basic value reasoning; (2) RL pretraining (actor frozen, critic trained via REINFORCE with reward R_v(s,z) = 1−(r−v̂)²) to ground the critic's reasoning in empirical returns; (3) Joint RL training of actor via PPO and critic via REINFORCE, alternating updates. Three critical design decisions: (a) scoring format (integer 0–10 rather than a free-form float) is justified as more natural for a language model to reason about; (b) ICC is justified both theoretically (value functions are policy-conditioned by definition) and empirically (even GPT-5 fails as a zero-shot value model without ICC); (c) RL pretraining before joint training is justified by the observation that SFT alone overfits to stylistic oracle patterns and is upper-bounded by oracle quality. Key Results: All RL experiments use Qwen3-8B-Base as the actor and evaluate on six mathematical reasoning benchmarks: AIME 2024, AMC 2023, MATH500, AIME 2025, OlympiadBench, and Minerva Math. GenAC achieves 51.90% average accuracy vs. 50.22% (GRPO), 49.93% (RLOO), and 50.39% (VC-PPO). The gap is larger on harder benchmarks: AIME24 30.00% vs. GRPO 26.46% and AMC23 74.53% vs. GRPO 69.69%. Critically, GenAC's performance margin over all baselines grows throughout the 200 training steps while VC-PPO's advantage over value-free methods diminishes — directly showing that discriminative critics saturate while generative critics do not. In controlled approximation experiments with critics 0.6B–14B parameters: discriminative critics achieve similar validation MSE (~0.065–0.075) across all model sizes with high seed variance, while generative critics improve consistently with scale (reaching ~0.05 at 8B) and show substantially lower variance. In relative ranking of candidate segments (Table 3), the generative critic achieves top-1 accuracy of 0.711 vs. 0.604 (discriminative) for 2 candidates, and 0.332 vs. 0.204 for 8 candidates. OOD generalization (Table 4) shows generative critics reduce MSE by 20.3% on in-distribution data, 39.3% on mild shift (Math500), 52.5% on moderate shift (AIME24), and 48.5% on high shift (GPQA). GenAC incurs ~2.1× the FLOPs of standard PPO, compared to 4.3× for VinePPO. All baselines are current and appropriate; GRPO and RLOO represent the dominant value-free paradigm and VC-PPO is the strongest prior value-based method. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The core novelty is applying generative architecture to value modeling (critics) — distinguished from prior work on generative reward models because value functions must track an evolving policy and are intrinsically harder than static reward modeling. The theoretical complexity-class argument for why discriminative critics fail is a genuine intellectual contribution. The ICC mechanism and the three-stage training pipeline are novel engineering contributions. What is not novel: the use of CoT in LLMs, representation complexity theory for Transformers, PPO itself, and the general idea of generative critics (which exists in reward modeling); the synthesis and the value-modeling-specific instantiation are the new contributions. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present for training pipeline stages and ICC (Table 2), cleanly isolating contributions. Scaling experiments for value approximation use 10 random seeds with mean and standard deviation reported. RL experiments report avg@16 scores (average over 16 sampled generations) across six benchmarks. No error bars are reported for the main RL training curves or final accuracy numbers in Table 1 — seed variance of the full RL runs is unknown. Code is built on the verl framework with vLLM and FSDP; no public repository for GenAC specifically is mentioned in the paper. Experiments are conducted on a single node of 8× Nvidia A100 80GB GPUs, which is reproducible for well-resourced academic labs. The paper evaluates on math benchmarks that are standard (MATH500, Minerva) and competitive (AIME24/25, OlympiadBench), avoiding evaluation on held-out proprietary datasets. One concern: the SFT data is synthesized by GPT-5, introducing a dependency on a closed commercial model; the ceiling of RL pretraining is partially bounded by GPT-5's quality as an oracle, which the paper acknowledges. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs on 8× A100 80GB GPUs using standard distributed training (FSDP); within reach for well-equipped research labs but not a single-GPU setup. - Efficient: At 2.1× PPO FLOPs, GenAC is substantially more efficient than VinePPO (4.3×), though still double the cost of standard PPO or value-free methods; the paper defers engineering optimizations to future work. - Effective: Evidence is solid for mathematical reasoning — GenAC consistently outperforms all baselines and the growing margin with training steps is direct evidence that the generative critic enables continued learning where discriminative critics saturate. - Scalable: Scaling is tested for value approximation only (0.6B–14B parameter critics) but not for the full RL pipeline; whether GenAC's advantage holds at actor scales of 70B+ is untested and only claimed to generalize broadly. The work delivers a training pipeline and architectural design; no public code or model weights are released as of the preprint date. Strengths: - The representation complexity argument provides a principled, non-trivial theoretical diagnosis for why discriminative critics fail to scale — not just empirical observation but a complexity-theoretic explanation grounded in prior formal results. - The empirical case for generative critics is multi-faceted and internally consistent: approximation error, seed robustness, ranking accuracy, OOD generalization, and downstream RL performance all align, making the story hard to dismiss as cherry-picking. - ICC is a simple but well-motivated mechanism that addresses a fundamental asymmetry between reward models (static) and value models (policy-conditioned); the ablation shows it consistently helps and the GPT-5 failure case makes the point sharply. - The growing performance gap between GenAC and baselines over training steps is a stronger argument than a single final-accuracy number — it demonstrates that the generative critic sustains learning signal rather than just starting with a better initialization. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge that (1) GenAC incurs ~2.1× the computational cost of PPO and defer cost reduction to future work; (2) experiments are conducted on mathematical reasoning tasks and they only claim broad generalizability without experimental support; (3) the SFT stage is bounded by GPT-5's oracle quality, which is itself imperfect. These are stated plainly, not minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The RL experiments are conducted only at 8B scale; the approximation scaling experiments (0.6B–14B) are static and do not involve RL training, so no claim about how the RL performance gap evolves at larger actor scales is backed by data. The single run without reported variance in Table 1 is a meaningful gap — it is unknown whether the ~1.7-point average improvement is statistically reliable or within seed variance. The SFT stage relies on GPT-5 for reasoning trace synthesis, creating a dependency on an external closed model that constrains reproducibility and introduces a confound (a larger closed-model oracle might yield even better SFT initialization). The paper does not evaluate robustness to the segment length hyperparameter (~120 tokens), which is a non-trivial design choice that affects both critic compute and credit assignment granularity. The critics are evaluated only on self-contained math problems with binary outcome rewards; the method's behavior on multi-turn, tool-using agents with non-binary or delayed rewards is entirely unknown. Bottom Line: This is a well-executed preprint that makes a credible case for rethinking value modeling in LLM RL post-training, combining a theoretically grounded diagnosis with a working solution — practitioners building PPO-style post-training pipelines should read it, with the caveat that the RL evidence is limited to 8B-scale math reasoning and the absence of seed-level variance in the main results leaves the statistical significance of the average accuracy gains unclear.
Recommended 2604.10701 PDF
#24 — Innovative (72)

Defragmenting Language Models: An Interpretability-based Approach for Vocabulary Expansion

arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces FragMend, an interpretability-based vocabulary expansion method that leverages "subword detokenization"—LLMs' internal progressive reconstruction of subwords across transformer layers—to select vocabulary items and initialize their embeddings, achieving up to 3× more token reduction than the prior interpretability baseline while preserving downstream performance. - Why it's important: Speakers of languages written in non-Latin scripts (e.g., Odia, Burmese) face a 5–10× tokenization premium over English speakers, directly inflating API costs and degrading model performance, and the standard BPE-based remedy forces a poor performance–efficiency tradeoff that interpretability-based methods can substantially improve. - How it does it: For each candidate subword affix extracted from a small unlabeled corpus, FragMend runs the Patchscopes activation-patching framework to detect whether the LLM internally reconstructs that subword; successfully detokenized subwords are added to the vocabulary with embeddings initialized via an orthogonal Procrustes transformation from the hidden state at the earliest detokenization layer. - The good: Interpretability-based item selection (vs. BPE) shifts the entire performance–efficiency Pareto frontier: BPE-expanded models can lose 60+ F1 points for languages like Odia at equivalent budget, while FragMend achieves 20–60% token reduction with single-digit performance drops on SIB200 and Belebele, using only 1000 training sequences and a single H200 GPU. - The missing: No evaluation on generative tasks (the method is only tested on topic classification and reading comprehension), and hypernetwork-based baselines (ZeTT, HYPEROFA) are explicitly deferred to future work—leaving the strongest competitors unaddressed. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone working on multilingual tokenization, vocabulary adaptation, or mechanistic interpretability; practitioners needing generation-quality guarantees should wait for generative task evaluation.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) is genuine on two fronts: empirically reframing item selection (not initialization) as the binding constraint in vocabulary expansion, and extending detokenization findings to subword affixes — a real NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING plus NEW METHOD.
