Non-Obviousness (60) reflects the surprising intra-Blackwell variance. Practical Impact (75) high — actionable decision tables and released benchmark repo. Resilience (65) and Completeness (75) good given honest limitation disclosure. Weighted: 0.20*55+0.25*70+0.15*60+0.15*75+0.15*65+0.10*75 ≈ 66.
empirical Novelty 55 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 75 Resilience 65 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: GPU kernel developers and ML infrastructure teams face a recurring dilemma: writing high-performance Tensor Core kernels requires hundreds to thousands of lines of architecture-specific CUDA C++ (e.g., FlashAttention-2, CUTLASS), and every new GPU generation—Hopper to Blackwell—demands substantial retuning. NVIDIA introduced CuTile in late 2025 as a Python-based tile-centric DSL intended to reduce this burden, claiming Tensor Core and TMA efficiency at dramatically reduced code volume. No independent, multi-GPU evaluation of CuTile existed at the time of submission; existing benchmarks cover Triton vs. CUTLASS but leave CuTile's performance portability across Blackwell variants (sm_100 vs. sm_120) entirely uncharacterized. Key Idea: The core insight is methodological rather than algorithmic: by fixing identical mathematical formulations across five abstractions and testing on two Blackwell micro-architectures simultaneously, the study isolates compiler maturity effects from hardware generation effects. The 5.6× attention performance gap between B200 (sm_100) and RTX PRO 6000 (sm_120) with identical CuTile kernel code is the central empirical finding, demonstrating that CuTile's tileiras compiler is datacenter-first and that the "Blackwell" label covers meaningfully different targets. Methodology: The experimental pipeline runs three kernel families: square and rectangular BF16/FP16 GEMM (M=N=K ∈ {4096, 8192, 12288, 16384} plus LLaMA-7B FFN shapes), causal fused multi-head attention (32 heads, d=128, seq ∈ {512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192}, batch=8), and end-to-end LLM inference on a 4-layer LLaMA-7B-like proxy (batch ∈ {1, 8, 32}). Timing uses CUDA event-based measurement, 10 warmup + 50 timed iterations, arithmetic mean reported; variance was below 1% for all non-SIMT implementations. TFLOP/s is computed from standard GEMM and attention FLOPs formulae with causal-masking halving. Correctness was validated against PyTorch/cuBLAS reference outputs. Three critical design decisions: (1) holding kernel code identical across GPUs for CuTile (valid to isolate compiler effects), (2) allowing Triton autotuning over 18 configurations (fair, but gives Triton an advantage over the non-autotuned CuTile kernel), (3) limiting attention to standard MHA rather than GQA/MQA (intentional to isolate compute-bound prefill behavior, but limits production relevance). Key Results: For BF16 square GEMM at N=8192: B200—cuBLAS 1,672 > Triton 1,033 > CuTile 876 > WMMA 200 TFLOP/s; RTX PRO 6000—cuBLAS 385 ≈ Triton 390 > CuTile 303 > WMMA 195 TFLOP/s; H100—cuBLAS 509 > Triton 385 > WMMA 133 TFLOP/s (CuTile unavailable). For fused attention at seq=4096: B200—CuTile 1,007 > Triton 526 > FlashAttention-2 401 > cuDNN SDPA 163 TFLOP/s; RTX PRO 6000—FlashAttention-2 335 > Triton 288 > cuDNN SDPA 133 > CuTile 179 TFLOP/s; H100—FlashAttention-2 262 > Triton 247 > cuDNN SDPA 134 TFLOP/s. The 5.6× CuTile attention gap between B200 and RTX PRO 6000 is the largest cross-architecture variance observed for any tested abstraction. Triton sustains 62–101% of cuBLAS across all three GPUs without architecture-specific code changes. End-to-end LLM decode performance is memory-bandwidth-bound and uniform across fused attention backends (4,594–4,647 tok/s on B200 at batch=32), confirming CuTile offers no decode advantage. Baselines are current and strong: FlashAttention-2 v2.8.3, Triton 3.3–3.4, CUDA 12.6–13.1. Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The primary contribution is empirical characterization of a recently released tool (CuTile v1.1.0) across two Blackwell variants that were not previously benchmarked together. The methodology is standard benchmarking; there is no new algorithm, system, or formalism. What is genuinely novel is the quantified, independent demonstration that CuTile's performance is strongly architecture-variant-dependent within a single GPU family, and the discovery of the 5.6× attention gap between sm_100 and sm_120—a finding with direct practical consequence that was not documented by NVIDIA. Rigor & Reproducibility: Variance below 1% is reported but no standard deviations or confidence intervals are provided; given 50-iteration means on deterministic hardware this is defensible. No ablations over CuTile kernel configurations are shown (e.g., varying CTA cluster size to demonstrate the claimed 4× sensitivity). The software version difference between H100 (PyTorch 2.7.1/CUDA 12.6) and Blackwell GPUs (PyTorch 2.8.0/CUDA 12.8) introduces a potential confounder the authors acknowledge but do not bound empirically. Triton is autotuned over 18 configurations while CuTile is not; the paper does not evaluate whether additional CuTile tuning could close the gap. Code is publicly available at an anonymous repository (https://anonymous.4open.science/r/CuTile_Benchmarkings-4F23/README.md). This is an arXiv preprint, not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (benchmarking of existing systems on physical hardware). - Practical: All benchmarks run on commercially available or accessible datacenter hardware (H100 NVL, RTX PRO 6000, B200); CuTile requires CUDA Toolkit 13.1 which was available at submission time. - Efficient: CuTile GEMM reaches 52–79% of cuBLAS throughput and requires 22 kernel lines vs. 123 for WMMA; for attention on B200, CuTile is 2.51× more compute-efficient than FlashAttention-2, but relies on 227 KB shared memory per block available only on B200/H100, not the 48 KB constraint of RTX PRO 6000. - Effective: For fused attention on B200, the evidence is strong and concrete (1,007 vs. 401 TFLOP/s measured); for GEMM, CuTile is a clear WMMA replacement but not a cuBLAS replacement, which the paper states honestly. - Scalable: Sequence lengths up to 8,192 and matrix sizes up to 16,384×16,384 are tested; multi-GPU scaling, larger batch sizes, and full 32-layer models are not evaluated; the end-to-end inference uses a 4-layer proxy that may not represent bottleneck distribution at full scale. The work delivers benchmark code as a software artifact; CuTile itself is NVIDIA's externally maintained library. Strengths: - The cross-architecture comparison within a single GPU family (B200 vs. RTX PRO 6000) is the paper's most valuable contribution: it establishes that compute-capability version matters more than marketing family name for CuTile adoption decisions. - The performance–productivity frontier visualization (Figure 1) and lines-of-code table (Table VII) give practitioners a direct, quantified cost-benefit signal that is absent from vendor documentation. - The paper identifies a concrete micro-architectural cause for the sm_100 vs. sm_120 gap (48 KB vs. 227 KB shared memory per block) and connects it to CuTile's tile-size and double-buffering requirements—a mechanistic hypothesis, not mere reporting. - The decision framework (Table VIII, Section X) is actionable and honest, explicitly stating the cases where CuTile is worse than alternatives. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly acknowledge: single-GPU-instance evaluation (1–3% manufacturing variation risk); absence of hardware counter profiling (no roofline decomposition); BF16/FP16 only (FP8/INT8 performance unknown); 4-layer proxy model for end-to-end inference; Blackwell-only CuTile (no Hopper/Ampere); software version differences between H100 and Blackwell test beds; and compiler immaturity (tileiras 1.1.0 may improve dramatically). This is a creditable disclosure posture—the authors confront scope boundaries rather than minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) Triton is autotuned over 18 configurations; CuTile is not subjected to equivalent hyperparameter search (CTA cluster size, tile shape). The paper claims a 4× sensitivity to CTA cluster configuration but does not show the sweep, making it unclear how much of the RTX PRO 6000 underperformance is compiler versus tuning. (2) The attention benchmark covers only standard MHA; GQA and MQA (dominant in current LLM deployment) have different memory-access patterns that could change the relative rankings. (3) No latency measurements under varying batch sizes for the kernel benchmarks—only throughput—which matters for latency-sensitive serving. (4) The end-to-end inference section tests CuTile's kernels individually but CuTile is not integrated into the inference stack, so the claimed "potential 2.5× prefill speedup" at system level is speculative and not measured. (5) No evaluation of multi-GPU or tensor-parallel configurations, which is the dominant regime for B200 deployment. Bottom Line: Practitioners deploying fused attention on B200 datacenter GPUs should evaluate this paper immediately—the 2.51× throughput advantage over FlashAttention-2 in 60 lines of Python is credible, independently measured, and large enough to matter; for all other hardware or standard GEMM, the paper correctly concludes that Triton or cuBLAS remain the better options.
Search Results 2604.23466 PDF
#59 — Innovative (66)

Computational Lesions in Multilingual Language Models Separate Shared and Language-specific Brain Alignment

arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a computational lesion framework applied to six multilingual LLMs that causally dissociates shared versus language-specific parameter subsets driving fMRI brain-model alignment across English, Chinese, and French native speakers. - Why it's important: Whether the brain's language network implements a common computational algorithm across languages or recruits language-specific mechanisms is foundational to multilingual neuroscience, and existing correlational methods cannot answer this causally. - How it does it: Gradient×weight importance scores derived from language-specific fine-tuning identify a compact shared "core" (top 1% of cross-language important parameters) and per-language subsets, which are then zeroed to create lesioned models whose voxel-wise fMRI encoding performance is compared against intact models. - The good: Core ablation produces a convergent triple collapse — representational geometry compresses (Silhouette Coefficient drops from 0.4277 to 0.0594 in Qwen2.5-7B), syntactic probing degrades across all three languages, and whole-brain encoding correlation falls 60.32% — all from removing just 1% of parameters, replicated across six models from three architectures. - The missing: Core ablation causes catastrophic perplexity inflation (Qwen2.5-7B: 6.75 → 3,792,672.75), making it impossible to distinguish selective removal of brain-relevant computation from generic representational collapse; dose-response or matched-performance controls are absent. - Read it or not: Researchers at the intersection of multilingual NLP and cognitive neuroscience should read it for its causal lesion logic, which advances beyond correlational layer-wise comparisons, though the confound between core lesion severity and general competence destruction remains the central unresolved problem.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Pulled down by Rigor (60) and Resilience (55): the headline core-lesion effect (60.32% drop) is confounded by catastrophic perplexity inflation (6.75→3.79M) with no matched-degradation control, and the 1% threshold lacks a dose-response curve. Novelty (78) and Non-Obviousness (78) are real — gradient-guided causal lesions across six multilingual LLMs to dissociate shared vs. language-specific brain alignment is a genuine new design with a clean typological-distance corroboration. Completeness (70) — authors honestly confront the confound. Impact (50) — niche cogsci tooling, code released but no deployable artifact. Weighted ≈ 66.
empirical Novelty 78 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 70 Resilience 55 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Neuroimaging has identified a fronto-temporo-parietal language network active across many languages, but shared anatomy does not imply shared computation — the same regions can support different operations over different grammatical structures. The question of whether multilingual comprehension relies on a common computational substrate or recruits language-specific mechanisms is unanswerable by standard neuroimaging alone. Prior brain-model alignment work has been largely correlational (comparing intact representations across model layers) and monolingual (English-centric), leaving open whether the model components that drive alignment are shared or selective. Native speakers of typologically distant languages such as Mandarin Chinese and French present particularly sharp test cases, since their grammatical and orthographic demands diverge substantially from the English-dominated training distributions of most models tested previously. Key Idea Causal necessity is testable by disruption rather than correlation: identify parameter subsets important for each language using gradient×weight importance scores accumulated during language-specific fine-tuning, zero the top 1% of the cross-language "core" versus per-language subsets, then measure whether fMRI predictivity fails globally (implicating shared computation) or selectively (implicating language-specific computation). This converts brain-model alignment from a descriptive encoding score into a failure-mode test tied to specific, distributed parameter circuits. Methodology Three-stage pipeline. (1) Parameter importance identification: six multilingual LLMs (LLaMA2-7B/13B, Qwen2.5-7B/14B, Mistral-8B/12B) are fine-tuned separately on English, Chinese, and French literary corpora (Jane Eyre / Pride and Prejudice; Dream of the Red Chamber / Romance of the Three Kingdoms; L'Étranger / Les Misérables) using autoregressive next-token prediction. Importance score IL(θi) = |θi| × Σt|∂LL/∂θi(t)| (weight magnitude × accumulated absolute gradient). Core importance sums scores across all three languages; language-specific importance is the per-language rank percentile minus the maximum of the other two languages' percentiles. Top 1% of core parameters and top 1% of each language-specific subset are zeroed (set to zero), uniformly per transformer layer and component. (2) Neural encoding: final-layer contextualized embeddings from intact and lesioned LLMs are averaged per TR (2s), shifted 4s for hemodynamic lag, and used to fit voxel-wise ridge regression models (9-fold cross-validation, one fold per acquisition run) predicting BOLD time series per participant. Performance = Pearson r between predicted and observed BOLD. (3) Statistical analysis: voxel-wise paired t-tests (Fisher-transformed r, intact vs. lesioned), FDR < 0.01. A Language Processing Index (LPI) normalizes per-language t-maps within each model and computes a contrast score (target language vs. mean of others), then averages across all six models for architecture-robust localization. Critical design decisions: uniform proportional ablation across all layers avoids concentrating damage at a single layer (well-justified relative to single-layer ablation); using final-layer embeddings only rather than a layer-optimal approach is not compared against alternatives; literary fine-tuning corpora differ in domain from the fMRI stimulus (The Little Prince), a mismatch not discussed. Key Results Intact multilingual LLMs reliably predict BOLD responses in the canonical language network across all three languages and all six architectures, with Pearson r up to ~0.12–0.14 in left temporal ROIs (FDR < 0.01). Increasing model size from ~7B to ~14B parameters does not yield systematic improvement in neural predictivity. Core ablation reduces whole-brain mean encoding correlation by 60.32% relative to intact models (reported as a mean across all models; within-model variance not stated). In Qwen2.5-7B, Silhouette Coefficient of language-separated embedding clusters drops from 0.4277 (intact) to 0.0594 (core-lesioned), and perplexity on WikiText-2 increases from 6.75 to 3,792,672.75. Syntactic probing (sentence length, tree depth, top constituents, coordination inversion) drops across all three languages after core ablation, with largest drops on structural tasks. Language-specific lesions produce a dissociation: cross-language embedding separation is preserved (UMAP clusters remain distinct), but voxel-wise encoding drops most for matched-language participants — a clear diagonal in the 3×3 (lesion language × participant language) t-map matrix. English-French specific ablations cross-impair each other more strongly than Chinese-specific ablations affect French/English participants, consistent with Indo-European typological proximity. A random-ablation control (same 1% budget, randomly selected parameters) is included and produces smaller effects, partially ruling out generic parameter-removal artifacts. All effects concentrate in canonical language-selective regions (anterior and posterior temporal, inferior frontal). Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. Applying gradient-guided computational lesions across multilingual LLMs to causally dissociate shared versus language-specific components of fMRI brain-model alignment is novel. The specific finding — that a compact, distributed core (~1% of parameters) accounts for the majority of cross-language brain-model alignment while language-specific subsets contribute smaller, language-matched and typologically graded effects — constitutes a new empirical result. Underlying components (voxel-wise ridge regression encoding, gradient-weight importance, the LPPC-fMRI dataset) are established; the novelty is the causal dissociation design and its application to a multilingual neural encoding framework. Rigor & Reproducibility Code is publicly available (github.com/yang-C23/Shared-Unique-Neural-Architecture-for-Language); fMRI data are available via OpenNeuro (ds003643). A random-ablation control is included. FDR correction (p < 0.01) is applied to all voxel-wise tests. Results replicate across six models from three families with different tokenizers and attention mechanisms — a genuine methodological strength. However, the headline 60.32% reduction figure is reported as a single mean with no confidence interval or model-level breakdown. The 1% ablation threshold is fixed and untested; no dose-response curve is provided. Syntactic probing datasets for French and Chinese are machine-translated from English SentEval without reported validation or back-translation checks; Chinese annotations (syntactic tree depth, coordination) depend on segmentation choices not documented. Fine-tuning corpora are literary texts in a different genre and era from the fMRI stimulus, introducing a domain mismatch that could bias which parameters are flagged as important. Effect sizes for the language-specific lesion dissociation (diagonal vs. off-diagonal contrast) are not reported quantitatively. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: The ablation and encoding pipeline runs on open-source model weights; full-parameter fine-tuning (required for importance estimation) used EuroHPC HPC resources and is not runnable on consumer hardware for 14B models. - Efficient: No compute or wall-time estimates are reported; the ablation itself is parameter zeroing and adds negligible overhead, but six rounds of full-parameter fine-tuning per model family is expensive. - Effective: The lesion framework demonstrates structured, anatomically specific reductions in fMRI predictivity, though the core lesion effect conflates specific information removal with general model collapse; evidence for causal specificity is strong for the language-specific lesions, weaker for the core. - Scalable: Only 7B–14B models are tested; the paper explicitly declines to extrapolate beyond this range and notes no systematic size effect within it; behavior at 70B+ is unknown. The paper delivers a reusable analysis pipeline and code; ablated model weights are not explicitly stated as released. Strengths - The causal lesion design — comparing intact versus lesioned models on the same stimulus within each language group — eliminates cross-stimulus confounds that plague cross-language fMRI comparisons, making the within-language impairment contrasts clean. - Convergence across six architecturally heterogeneous models (three families, two scales, different tokenizers, MHA vs. GQA) substantially reduces the risk that findings are artifacts of a specific design choice. - The "triple collapse" under core ablation — simultaneous failure of representational geometry, linguistic probing, and fMRI predictivity from the same 1% intervention — provides triangulating convergent evidence that is more compelling than any single measure alone. - The graded cross-language impairment pattern for language-specific lesions (English-French > Chinese cross-impairment) independently recapitulates a known typological distance gradient, providing external validity for the language-specific circuit claim without requiring additional experiments. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly and honestly confront: (1) core ablation causes extreme perplexity inflation, so reduced neural predictivity could partly reflect global representational corruption rather than selective removal of brain-relevant computation — they recommend dose-response and matched-performance controls as future work; (2) "core" and "language-specific" are operational constructs tied to importance under specific fine-tuning objectives, not guaranteed architecturally pure; (3) the single-narrative, three-language design entangles language differences with translation and prosody properties; (4) the linear final-layer encoding model may miss nonlinear correspondences or layer-specific dynamics; (5) scanner heterogeneity (GE for English/Chinese, Siemens for French) coincides with French showing weaker alignment in some ROIs. This level of self-criticism is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The 60.32% core lesion effect is uninterpretable without matched-degradation controls: ablating the same number of non-core parameters to achieve equivalent perplexity inflation would test whether the effect tracks structural specificity rather than competence loss. The current random-ablation control does not match perplexity, making it insufficient. - The 1% threshold is arbitrary; without a dose-response curve, it is unknown whether the effect is linear, threshold-gated, or whether 0.1% suffices — a question directly relevant to the "sparsity principle" conclusion. - Domain mismatch between importance estimation corpora (19th-century literary novels, classical Chinese texts) and the fMRI stimulus (The Little Prince, a 20th-century novella) is unacknowledged; parameters flagged as important may reflect genre- or register-specific processing, not language-general computation. - Machine-translated probing datasets for French and Chinese lack quality validation; Chinese syntactic probes are especially suspect given that tree depth and coordination are sensitive to segmentation conventions. - The study covers only three languages, two of which (English, French) are typologically close Indo-European languages; generalization of the "shared backbone with embedded specializations" conclusion to morphologically rich, agglutinative, or tonal languages beyond Mandarin is not established. - The language-specific lesion dissociation (diagonal vs. off-diagonal) is shown qualitatively in t-maps but the quantitative effect size of the matched vs. mismatched contrast is never reported, making it impossible to assess practical significance. Bottom Line A neuroscientist or computational linguist working on brain-language alignment will find the causal lesion logic and cross-architecture convergence genuinely valuable, but the catastrophic core-lesion confound — collapsing a model's language ability by factor ~560,000 in perplexity and then attributing reduced brain predictivity to specific information loss — is the paper's central unsolved problem, and the conclusion that a sparse core is causally necessary for alignment should be read as a strong hypothesis requiring the matched-degradation controls the authors themselves identify.

#60 — Innovative (66)

ClawEnvKit: Automatic Environment Generation for Claw-Like Agents

arxiv.org · Ion Stoica - new articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ClawEnvKit, an automated pipeline that generates verified evaluation and training environments for CLI-style LLM agent harnesses from natural language descriptions, and releases Auto-ClawEval, a 1,040-environment benchmark across 24 task categories. - Why it's important: Manual construction of agent benchmarks requires ~2 person-hours per task (208 hours for 104 tasks in Claw-Eval), produces static artifacts that saturate and leak into training data, and cannot scale alongside rapidly improving agent capabilities. - How it does it: A three-module LLM pipeline (Parser → Generator → Validator) decouples declarative task specification from deterministic verification by representing each environment as a (P, M, C) triple — natural language task spec, mock API interface, evaluation functional — so an LLM can generate valid environments without reasoning about full execution semantics. - The good: Auto-ClawEval-Mini (104 auto-generated tasks) scores higher than human-curated Claw-Eval on both Coherence (0.59 vs 0.51) and Clarity (3.54 vs 3.38) at 13,800× lower cost, with a manually audited 0% false-negative rate across 52 high-effort low-score cases. - The missing: The coherence advantage is partially self-defeating: the authors acknowledge it arises because the LLM judge can directly inspect the structured P↔M↔C alignment in generated tasks but cannot read Claw-Eval's rubrics embedded in code — a measurement artifact, not a genuine quality signal. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building agent evaluation infrastructure or studying harness engineering effects; less valuable if your interest is in novel LLM agent architectures or training algorithms, as the contribution is tooling and benchmarking rather than new learning methods.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Novelty (65) — declarative (P,M,C) formalism is a genuine architectural insight but auto-benchmark generation is an active area. Rigor (60) — no per-module ablations, no error bars, coherence comparison confounded by judge visibility (acknowledged). Non-Obviousness (60) — the asymmetry insight is moderate. Completeness (65) — limitations directly confronted. Resilience (60) — pseudonymous ecosystem naming complicates replication; M2.7/M2.5 rank reversal undiscussed. Practical Impact (80) — public code, released benchmark, $80 generation cost. Weighted: 0.15*65+0.15*60+0.10*60+0.15*65+0.15*60+0.30*80 = 67.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Evaluating and training CLI-style LLM agents (called "claw-like agents" throughout — a pseudonymous term for a class of open-source agent harnesses) requires constructing environments that specify what the agent must do, what tools it can call, and how its actions are graded. Prior benchmarks (Claw-Eval, ClawArena, SkillsBench) are human-authored, requiring hundreds of person-hours, and freeze into static artifacts vulnerable to benchmark contamination as agents absorb them through training. Training pipelines (OpenClaw-RL, MetaClaw) are similarly bounded by whatever tasks users happen to perform. The gap is that neither evaluation nor training can scale alongside agent capabilities when environment construction is a manual bottleneck. Key Idea: The core insight is that modern agent environments have an asymmetry: they are semantically infinite from the agent's perspective (unbounded NL context, tool outputs, conversation history) but implementationally finite (a small set of in-memory mock service databases). Exploiting this asymmetry, the paper proposes a declarative formalism E = (P, M, C) — task spec, interaction interface, evaluation functional — that separates what to verify from how to verify it. An LLM can generate a valid (P, M, C) triple without reasoning about state transitions; the deterministic Validator enforces correctness structurally. This makes automated generation tractable where prior approaches required per-task grader logic. Methodology: The pipeline has three modules. The Parser converts a natural language request into structured "intent atoms" (actions, objects, constraints) via a single LLM call. The Generator has three sub-workflows: task generation (LLM writes task.yaml with tools, fixtures, scoring components), service generation (designs new FastAPI mock servers when needed), and fixture generation (mounts files into containers). The Validator runs 12 structural checks (required fields, weight sums, check type validity, LLM judge weight cap at 55%, safety/scoring contradiction detection) and semantic coverage checks (every intent atom must appear in P, M, or C). Execution uses Docker containers with `--network none`, mock services injecting 25% random HTTP 429/500 errors to test robustness, and three harness integration tiers (native plugin, MCP server, SKILL.md+curl). The GradingEngine applies 15 check types (audit-based, output-based, file-based, plus LLM judge) weighted by a safety gate that zeroes the entire score on any forbidden action. Critical design decisions: (1) capping LLM judge weight at 55% to keep the majority of every score deterministic — well-justified to reduce grading variance; (2) deduplication via action rotation and passing last 10 task names to the LLM — reasonable but heuristic; (3) using server-side audit logs rather than agent-reported actions to prevent credit for described-but-not-performed actions — well-justified. Key Results: On quality: Auto-ClawEval-Mini achieves Coherence 0.59 vs. 0.51 for Claw-Eval and Clarity 3.54 vs. 3.38, with 100% validity, generated in 1.8 hours vs. 208 person-hours. On agent performance across 1,040 environments: completion scores range from 34% (MiniMax M2.5) to 76% (MiniMax M2.7 — oddly, the smaller model variant scores worse), with no model saturating the benchmark. GPT-5.4 achieves the highest mean score (58.8). Safety (≥83%) and robustness (100% across all models) are near-perfect, making completion the primary discriminating axis. Across 8 harnesses, all structured harnesses outperform the ReAct Agent Loop baseline (53.3%), with NemoClaw reaching 69.0 (+15.7 pp). Counter-intuitively, Tier 3 SKILL.md harnesses (NemoClaw 69.0, Hermes 66.9) outperform several Tier 2 MCP harnesses (ZeroClaw 57.1, PicoClaw 53.2), suggesting harness integration tier does not determine performance. Auto-ClawEval and Mini scores differ by <2% across all models and harnesses. The system produces 1,040 environments for $80 in API costs (claude-sonnet-4.6). A false-negative audit of 52 high-effort low-score trajectories finds 0 genuine grading errors. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The declarative (P, M, C) environment formalism and the three-module generation pipeline are genuinely novel for the claw-like agent domain; the broader idea of automated benchmark generation via LLM (c.f. SWE-smith, RandomWorld, Agent World Model, Endless Terminal) is an active area, and this paper's specific contribution is the application to multi-harness API-calling agents with structured verification. The cross-harness evaluation design revealing that harness engineering matters as much as model choice is an empirical finding with practical significance. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations on individual pipeline components (Parser, Validator, Generator sub-workflows) are absent — it is not possible to determine how much each module contributes to final quality. No error bars or significance tests are reported on coherence/clarity comparisons (small N for quality study: 104 tasks). The Pass³ aggregation (requiring success in 3 independent runs) is used for methodology description but not clearly applied as the primary reported metric in Tables 3–4. A notable methodological concern: the coherence metric is assessed by an LLM judge that can directly read ClawEnvKit's explicit P↔M↔C YAML structure but cannot inspect Claw-Eval's rubrics embedded in task-specific Python graders — the authors acknowledge this explicitly, which partially deflates the coherence advantage claim. Code and benchmark are publicly released at https://github.com/xirui-li/ClawEnvKit. This is a preprint; results have not been peer-reviewed. All experiments ran on a single Apple M-series Mac with Docker Desktop. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — a built tool and infrastructure evaluated by deployment. - Practical: Runs on a single Apple M-series Mac with Docker Desktop; no GPU required; estimated $20–50 per 1,040-task run using Haiku. - Efficient: Generates 1,040 environments in 18 hours at $80 API cost vs. 208 person-hours for 104 human-curated tasks; evaluation cost scales linearly with task count and model API pricing. - Effective: The evidence that generated environments are evaluation-grade is moderately strong: 0% false negatives in audited cases and comparable agent score distributions to Claw-Eval; but the coherence comparison is partially confounded by judge visibility into task format. - Scalable: Tested at 1,040 environments across 8 harnesses and 5 model families; the authors claim unlimited scalability but do not demonstrate what happens at 10,000+ environments or whether task diversity degrades at scale due to the 24-category constraint anchored to Claw-Eval's service library. The work delivers a software tool (ClawEnvKit) and a benchmark dataset (Auto-ClawEval), both released publicly. Strengths: - The declarative (P, M, C) formalism is well-motivated and cleanly separates environment specification from verification, enabling LLM generation without explicit state-space reasoning — a genuine architectural insight. - The server-side audit log grading design (rather than agent-reported actions) closes a subtle but important loophole in agent evaluation where models receive credit for hallucinated tool calls. - The LLM judge weight cap at 55% and the Pass³ aggregation are thoughtful engineering choices that reduce non-determinism in grading without eliminating qualitative assessment. - The cross-harness evaluation (8 frameworks, 4 model families) is more comprehensive than any prior benchmark in this space and yields the non-obvious finding that harness tier does not strictly determine performance. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly confront three limitations: (1) mock services cannot replicate production API messiness (auth flows, schema drift, subscription-tier rate limits); (2) coverage is limited to 24 categories lacking GUI automation, voice, multi-agent delegation, and domain-specific workflows; (3) tasks are capped at 20 tool-calling rounds, not designed for long-horizon multi-session workflows. The authors also acknowledge the coherence metric advantage is partly a measurement artifact of judge visibility into structured format. This directness is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The entire "claw-like agent" ecosystem (OpenClaw, NanoClaw, IronClaw, etc.) uses pseudonymous names for what appear to be real systems (OpenClaw maps to open-source Claude Code-style CLI agents; the paper explicitly includes Claude Code and Codex as Tier 2 MCP harnesses alongside these fictional names). This opacity makes it impossible to independently verify the harness results or replicate experiments without access to the named systems. - All 1,040 Auto-ClawEval environments are seeded from the same 104 Claw-Eval scenarios (10 variants per scenario), meaning task diversity is anchored to human-curated scenarios — the system is a diversity-amplifier, not a truly unconstrained generator. The benchmark's coverage is bounded by Claw-Eval's original 24 categories and 15 services. - No ablation shows what fraction of generated tasks fail validation and require regeneration, which would reveal how often the Generator produces invalid environments and the true cost of validation. - The MiniMax M2.7 (more capable) vs. M2.5 result is anomalous: M2.7 scores 49.4 on Auto-ClawEval but M2.5 scores 43.6, yet on Auto-ClawEval-Mini, M2.7 scores only 44.9 vs. M2.5's 51.4 — a rank reversal with no discussion. - Diversity within generated task categories is not quantified; 10 variants per scenario might produce semantically redundant tasks that over-represent narrow sub-patterns. Bottom Line: A practitioner building or evaluating CLI-style LLM agent harnesses gains concrete infrastructure and a ready-to-use benchmark from this work, but should weight the coherence/clarity claims conservatively given the measurement confound, and should note that the pseudonymous ecosystem naming complicates independent replication.

#61 — Innovative (66)

CyberCane: Neuro-Symbolic RAG for Privacy-Preserving Phishing Detection with Formal Ontology Reasoning

arxiv.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CyberCane, a deployed neuro-symbolic phishing detection system that combines deterministic symbolic rules with privacy-preserving RAG (retrieval-augmented generation) and an OWL ontology (PhishOnt) to classify emails without transmitting unredacted sensitive data to external LLMs. - Why it's important: Healthcare, finance, and other regulated environments cannot use cloud-based LLM detectors because HIPAA and similar regulations prohibit transmitting unredacted protected health information to third-party APIs, yet AI-generated phishing now evades rule-based systems entirely. - How it does it: Emails are first scored by deterministic symbolic rules (DNS/SPF/DMARC checks, URL patterns, urgency cues); borderline cases are escalated to a RAG pipeline that redacts PHI before embedding, retrieves top-8 neighbors from a phishing-only corpus via HNSW, and derives the final verdict from cosine similarity statistics rather than LLM generation, with GPT-4.1-mini producing tagged explanations citing symbolic and retrieval evidence. - The good: On DataPhish 2025 (12.3k emails, ~75% LLM-generated), Phase 2 RAG achieves F1=0.987 at 98.2% precision and 99.1% recall, closing a 78.6-point recall gap left by symbolic-only detection on AI-crafted attacks, with statistical significance confirmed by McNemar's χ²=181.0 (p<0.001). - The missing: The retrieval corpus (2,297 examples) is drawn from the same DataPhish 2025 training split used to evaluate Phase 2 recall—a same-distribution advantage that inflates the near-perfect 99.1% recall figure; no out-of-distribution or white-box adversarial evaluation tests whether this carries over to novel campaigns. - Read it or not: Security engineers designing privacy-compliant email triage systems for regulated industries will find the architecture and deployment economics useful; researchers seeking a generalizable phishing detection advance should approach cautiously given the same-distribution retrieval and the absence of real healthcare data.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Impact (75) is the strongest dimension — open-source FastAPI/PostgreSQL/Next.js stack with documented deployment, real per-email cost analysis, and a principled solution to a genuine HIPAA-compliance gap that blocks cloud LLM detectors. Completeness (75) is solid: ablations, bootstrap CIs, McNemar tests, and honest acknowledgment of synthetic-only healthcare validation and adversarial robustness gaps. Rigor (65) and Resilience (55) are pulled down by the same-distribution retrieval issue — the 99.1% recall headline collapses to 37.2% on Nazario/SpamAssassin where TF-IDF+redaction baseline reaches 97.4%, and the 542× ROI headline suppresses a -376× conservative bound. Novelty (55) and Non-Obviousness (55) are moderate — the components (RAG, OWL, HNSW, regex redaction) are well-known; the integration and the empirical finding that embeddings are author-model invariant are the genuine contributions. Weighted: 0.15*55+0.15*65+0.10*55+0.15*75+0.15*55+0.30*75 ≈ 66.
systems Novelty 55 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 75 Resilience 55 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Phishing is the dominant initial access vector in data breaches, yet organizations in privacy-critical domains (healthcare, finance, government) cannot deploy state-of-the-art LLM-based detectors because those systems transmit email content—potentially including protected health information—to external APIs, violating HIPAA and analogous regulations. Rule-based systems are precise but achieve <21% recall against AI-generated phishing that avoids keyword triggers and maintains legitimate DNS infrastructure. Deep learning classifiers generalize better but produce opaque decisions incompatible with the audit trails required by compliance frameworks. The problem is specifically the four-way tension among near-zero false positives (to prevent workflow disruption), verifiable explainability (for non-specialist IT staff), regulatory data minimization, and robustness against adversarially crafted LLM-generated content. Key Idea: The core insight is that the verdict-determining signal can be made reproducible and privacy-safe by deriving it from cosine similarity statistics (top-1 and mean-top-3 scores against a local phishing corpus) rather than from LLM generation, while using the LLM only to produce human-readable explanations conditioned on already-redacted text and pre-approved retrieved snippets. The OWL ontology (PhishOnt) then provides a formal provenance chain linking Phase 1 indicator detections to named attack classes, enabling audit-trail explanations that are grounded in deterministic inference rather than probabilistic LLM output. Methodology: The pipeline has two phases. Phase 1 applies a weighted rule engine (weights w ∈ {1,2,3}) across five indicator categories: DNS/MX/SPF/DMARC authentication, domain mismatch, URL obfuscation, urgency language, and credential requests. Risk scores map to three verdicts (benign s<2, needs_review 2≤s<5, phishing s≥5). Detected indicators are passed to PhishOnt, an OWL ontology with description-logic axioms; the reasoner computes multi-label attack type confidence as c=(m_universal + m_existential)/denominator and emits reasoning chains. Emails not immediately classified as phishing by Phase 1 are escalated to Phase 2: regex-based PHI redaction (email addresses, phone, SSN, credit card) precedes embedding via OpenAI text-embedding-3-small (1536-d), HNSW retrieval (k=8) against a phishing-only corpus of 2,297 examples in pgvector, and GPT-4.1-mini reasoning (temperature=0.2) conditioned on redacted email, Phase 1 indicators, ontology attack types, and retrieved snippets. The composite RAG score is srag=0.65·stop+0.35·savg; classification uses hard thresholds (top_sim≥0.40 for phishing; Phase 1 phishing verdict is a deterministic override). Critical design decisions: (1) verdict from similarity statistics, not LLM generation—justified for reproducibility; (2) phishing-only retrieval corpus—partially mitigated by conservative thresholds but creates structural positive bias the paper acknowledges; (3) deterministic Phase 1 override—justified to prevent false negatives on technical violations, though it constrains the neural phase from correcting Phase 1 errors. The system is implemented as FastAPI/PostgreSQL with a Next.js frontend and is open-source. Key Results: On DataPhish 2025 (test n=2,337; ~75% LLM-generated), Phase 2 RAG achieves 98.2% precision, 99.1% recall, F1=0.987; Phase 1 alone achieves 20.5% recall—a 78.6-point gap. F1 ranges 0.976–1.000 across 18 creator sources including GPT-4o, DeepSeek, Amazon Nova, and human-written emails. On Nazario/SpamAssassin (2006-era, test n=1,110), RAG achieves 99.5% precision and 37.2% recall at FPR=0.16%; the privacy-constrained TF-IDF logistic regression baseline achieves 98.8% precision and 97.4% recall at FPR=0.98%, meaning the baseline achieves 2.6× higher recall while CyberCane achieves 6× lower FPR. The ablation on Nazario/SpamAssassin is revealing: TF-IDF+redaction costs $0/email, produces no explanations, has no tunable operating points, and still misses far fewer phishing emails. On synthetic healthcare attacks (n=165), balanced mode achieves only 18.2% recall on GPT-generated attacks; aggressive mode recovers 87.3% at 3.5% FPR. The 542× ROI figure is an optimistic bound assuming zero undetected breaches; the honest conservative bound at 37.2% recall is −376× (negative, because missed-attack costs exceed mitigated risk). Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The technical components—RAG, OWL ontologies, HNSW retrieval, symbolic rule engines, regex PHI redaction—are individually well-established; the contribution is their specific integration with a privacy-first constraint (redact before embed), a phishing-only retrieval corpus, and similarity-statistic-based verdicts. PhishOnt is a new domain ontology but encodes known phishing indicator knowledge. The empirical finding that semantic embeddings are invariant to authoring model (human vs. 17 LLMs, F1 difference <0.002) is the most generalizable research result. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations (leave-one-out by rule group, retrieval corpus composition, redaction impact, threshold sensitivity) are present and informative. Bootstrap 95% CIs are reported (1,000 resamples for metrics, 10,000 for McNemar) confirming statistical stability. Code is open-source at https://github.com/sbhakim/Cybercane. The central methodological concern: the Phase 2 retrieval corpus (2,297 examples from DataPhish 2025 training split) is drawn from the same distribution as the DataPhish 2025 test set. Near-perfect recall (99.1%) on DataPhish 2025 is not surprising when the retrieval corpus contains semantically similar training examples from the same generation pipeline. The Nazario/SpamAssassin results—where the retrieval corpus is also built from DataPhish training data, a different distribution—show 37.2% recall, a far more honest performance signal. Healthcare validation relies entirely on synthetic data; the paper correctly labels this a limitation. The ROI analysis uses unverified assumptions ($50K/breach, 10% attack success rate) and the optimistic headline (542×) buries the negative conservative bound in the body text. A note in the appendix acknowledges the system's initial prototype was developed in an educational setting, which does not undermine the current work but warrants disclosure. Deployability (SYSTEMS profile): - Practical: Runs on commodity hardware with PostgreSQL and OpenAI API access; the paper reports $0.0017/email including human review labor, deployable by any organization with internet API access and standard server infrastructure. - Efficient: Phase 1 is zero-cost and near-instant; Phase 2 adds OpenAI API latency and cost ($1.71/day for 10,000 emails at 4.4% phishing rate), with 13.1% of emails escalated; the dominant cost is human review labor ($1,310/day), not compute. - Effective: Evidence for effectiveness is strong on DataPhish 2025 (same-distribution) and weaker on Nazario/SpamAssassin (37.2% recall) and synthetic healthcare attacks (18.2% recall in balanced mode); the system solves the stated problem only when operating point is tuned aggressively, at which point FPR rises to 3.5%. - Scalable: Tested at 10,000 emails/day in the cost-benefit scenario; HNSW retrieval scales sublinearly with corpus size by design; no empirical scaling tests beyond the evaluation corpora (~12k emails) are reported. The system delivers a working software artifact (open-source FastAPI + PostgreSQL + Next.js stack) with documented deployment guides. Strengths: - The privacy architecture (redact before embed, similarity-statistic verdicts, offline ontology inference) is a principled and deployable solution to the HIPAA-compliance gap that blocks cloud LLM detectors in regulated environments. - PhishOnt's 100% ONTOLOGY-tag groundedness (all 41 tagged bullets correctly cite formal reasoning chains) versus heuristic tags' 16–66% support rates demonstrates that formal symbolic reasoning produces more verifiable explanations than free-text LLM outputs—a meaningful finding for audit requirements. - The demonstration that detection F1 is statistically indistinguishable between human-written and LLM-generated phishing (0.985 vs. 0.987) under the same thresholds confirms the semantic embedding space generalizes across authoring models, which is a non-obvious and useful empirical result. - Operating point transparency (five tunable configurations from conservative to aggressive, all reported with precision, recall, F1, FPR, and economics) gives practitioners actionable deployment parameters rather than a single reported figure. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) no real-world healthcare deployment validation—only DataPhish 2025 and synthetic tests; (2) lack of publicly available domain-specific phishing corpora; (3) white-box adversarial robustness is unvalidated—an adaptive adversary could craft content simultaneously rule-free and semantically dissimilar to the corpus; (4) residual privacy risks from quasi-identifiers and embedding inversion attacks persist despite PHI redaction; (5) organizational controls (BAAs, privacy impact assessments, workforce training) are required beyond technical controls. These are confronted directly and with appropriate specificity, which is credibility-positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Same-distribution retrieval inflates the headline result. The DataPhish 2025 retrieval corpus and test set come from the same generation pipeline. The 99.1% recall figure is not a demonstration of generalization—it is a demonstration of within-distribution retrieval. The Nazario/SpamAssassin evaluation (different distribution) shows 37.2% recall, which should be the lead result for a generalization claim. - TF-IDF+redaction outperforms on recall at lower cost. The privacy-constrained TF-IDF logistic regression baseline achieves 97.4% recall vs. 37.2% for CyberCane on Nazario/SpamAssassin, at $0/email and with PHI exposure = 0%. The paper frames CyberCane's advantage as lower FPR and verifiable explanations, but the 60-point recall gap is not discussed with adequate prominence—in a healthcare setting with 37.2% recall, 62.8% of phishing emails are delivered. - The 542× ROI headline suppresses the conservative bound. The conservative ROI at the same operating point is −376× (Table 7); the paper reports this but only in the body. Using the conservative bound as the headline would more accurately represent deployment risk at the conservative threshold, which is the paper's recommended default. - No adversarial evaluation against the system itself. The paper tests robustness across LLM sources but not against an adversary who knows the retrieval corpus or the similarity thresholds and designs content to sit below them. The single false-positive case (one email in 1,110) raises no concern, but the 407 false negatives (82.2% of phishing missed) on Nazario/SpamAssassin demand more investigation. - Explanation groundedness for URL and CONTENT tags is near zero (0% and 16% support rates in Table 24 expanded analysis), yet these tags appear in 20% of explanation bullets each—meaning LLM-generated explanations for URL and content evidence are largely unverifiable by the ontology or retrieval system, directly contradicting the paper's explainability claim for a substantial fraction of outputs. Bottom Line: CyberCane is a useful systems paper with a deployable architecture for privacy-constrained phishing detection, but the headline recall figure (99.1%) is largely a same-distribution artifact, the conservative ROI is negative at the default operating point, and a practitioner who needs high recall without HIPAA constraints is better served by the TF-IDF+redaction baseline at zero cost and 97.4% recall.
Search Results 2604.23563 PDF
#62 — Innovative (66)

Multistakeholder Impacts of Profile Portability in a Recommender Ecosystem

arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper investigates, via simulation, how four data portability policies (algorithm-specific, cold start, user ownership, universal profile) affect consumer and provider utility across a two-recommender ecosystem (generic + niche) using three real-world datasets and three ranking algorithms. - Why it's important: Data portability regulations (GDPR Articles 17/20, DMA, CCPA) are actively reshaping how platforms must handle user data when users switch services, yet their downstream effects on recommendation quality for niche versus mainstream stakeholders are entirely unstudied. - How it does it: Extends the SMORES simulation framework with four profile management policies defined along two binary axes — exclusivity (does data follow the user?) and permanence (is data retained by the original platform?) — running 3 datasets × 3 algorithms × 4 portability conditions × 5 seeds. - The good: The counter-intuitive finding that exclusive conditions (algorithm-specific, cold-start), which GDPR portability rights are specifically designed to dislodge, actually yield the largest niche consumer utility gains (up to +192.2% over baseline), with a mechanistic explanation: non-exclusive conditions contaminate niche recommender training data with off-target generic user profiles. - The missing: The simulation uses a synthetic click-generation function derived from the same genre-preference dot product that defines consumer utility, creating a closed evaluation loop; no real user behavior is validated and no statistical significance tests are reported. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers at the intersection of recommender systems fairness and data governance policy; practitioners needing deployment guidance should treat the simulation results as directional hypotheses rather than empirically grounded conclusions.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (80) and Novelty (70) are the strengths — the 2×2 exclusivity×permanence portability design space and the counter-intuitive finding that exclusive conditions outperform user-empowering ones (with a coherent noise-contamination mechanism) is genuinely surprising and policy-relevant. Rigor (55) is held back by the closed evaluation loop (clicks generated from the same dot product defining utility), absence of significance tests with only 5 seeds, and no ablation of the portability mechanism. Completeness (70) — limitations confronted directly. Impact (60) — open-source SMORES extension is a real artifact. Composite ≈ 0.20·70 + 0.25·55 + 0.15·80 + 0.15·70 + 0.15·60 + 0.10·60 = 65.25 → 66.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 70 Resilience 60 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Niche consumers — users whose tastes diverge from the mainstream — are systematically underserved by generic, popularity-biased collaborative filtering recommenders. Algorithmic pluralism (middleware) proposes decoupling recommendation algorithms from platforms so users can select specialized algorithms, and prior SMORES simulation work shows this benefits niche consumers and providers. However, middleware introduces a regulatory complication: when users switch algorithms, what happens to their accumulated profile data? Multiple data portability regulations (GDPR Article 20 grants transfer rights; Article 17 grants deletion rights; DMA and CCPA add further requirements) are actively reshaping the answer, yet no prior work examines how these policies affect multistakeholder utility in a recommender ecosystem. The paper closes this gap. Key Idea Profile portability decomposes into two independent binary dimensions — exclusivity (does a user's profile travel with them when switching?) and permanence (does the original platform retain the profile after departure?) — yielding a 2×2 design space. The core insight is that non-exclusive conditions, which seem user-empowering, introduce noise into niche recommenders: curious but ultimately uninterested generic users carry their mainstream profiles into the niche recommender's training set, diluting its specialization. This mechanism explains why the intuitively user-friendly User Ownership condition often performs no better, and sometimes worse, than the restrictive Algorithm-Specific condition for niche stakeholders. Methodology The SMORES (Simulation Model for Recommender EcoSystems) framework, extended in this work, models three stakeholder types: consumers (selecting items from recommendation slates), providers (earning utility from clicks), and recommenders (trained on interaction histories). Two recommenders coexist: a generic one (R_G) serving all genres and a niche one (R_N) restricted to items in the designated niche genre. All consumers start on R_G; after a two-cycle warm-up, they switch if utility falls below threshold τ = 0.2 and a better alternative exists. Consumer utility is computed as the dot product of a genre-preference vector (p_j) and item feature vector (f_i), normalized, softmax-converted to selection probabilities, and exponentially smoothed with recency bias β = 2.0. Provider utility is click counts. Profile management after switching is governed by the active portability policy: cold start deletes the old profile; user ownership transfers it; universal shares it; algorithm-specific retains it silently. Three datasets are used: MovieLens 10M (k-core 30; Romance as niche genre, 17.32% of items), Goodreads (k-core 30; Fantasy-Paranormal, 32.75% of items), and Amazon Video Games (k-core 5; Platformer, 6.04% of items). Three ranking algorithms are evaluated: ALS, BPR, and ItemKNN, implemented via LensKit and Implicit libraries. Each scenario runs 10 cycles of 3 days with 5 recommendations/day; results are averaged over cycles 5–10 (steady state). Five seeds per scenario are used. Critical design decisions: (1) one niche genre per dataset — simplifies the ecosystem but makes genre share a confound rather than a controlled variable; (2) synthetic click generation from preference-feature dot products — ensures internal consistency but removes real behavioral noise; (3) threshold switching at τ = 0.2 — simple and acknowledged as a limitation. Key Results Niche consumer utility increases versus the generic-only baseline in 35 of 36 scenarios (changes ranging from -4.2% to +192.2%). Generic consumer utility changes are mostly negative but small (-26.4% to +3.9%). The largest niche consumer gains occur in the exclusive conditions: Algorithm-Specific and Cold Start, not the user-empowering User Ownership condition. ItemKNN with Algorithm-Specific on Amazon Video Games yields +192.2% for niche consumers; the same condition yields only -3.4% for generic consumers. On Goodreads, where the niche genre is 32.75% of items and niche/generic user preferences overlap substantially, gains are muted and sometimes negative for niche providers. Niche provider utility improves in most conditions (up to +356.9% for Universal/ItemKNN/MovieLens), but exhibits no consistent pattern across portability conditions; Goodreads niche providers lose utility in 10 of 12 scenarios, attributed to their already-favorable baseline. Generic providers lose utility in almost all scenarios due to niche consumer migration. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW FORMULATION. Framing profile portability as a 2×2 (exclusivity × permanence) design space mapped onto real data governance concepts (GDPR Articles 17 and 20) is a genuine conceptual contribution. The empirical finding that exclusive conditions outperform portability-friendly ones for niche utility — and the noise-contamination explanation — is non-obvious and contradicts regulatory intuitions. The simulation methodology adapts existing SMORES infrastructure; ranking algorithms (ALS, BPR, ItemKNN) are standard. Rigor & Reproducibility Five seeds per scenario provide basic variance coverage, but confidence intervals and significance tests are absent from all reported tables — results are presented as point estimates of mean utility. Code is open source (https://github.com/that-recsys-lab/smores) and datasets with configuration are DVC-tracked (https://github.com/that-recsys-lab/smores_umap_2026), supporting reproducibility. No ablations isolate the portability mechanism itself (e.g., partial portability, varying the fraction of users who port). Simulation validity is the key methodological concern: click generation uses the same genre-preference dot product that defines consumer utility, creating a closed evaluation loop where the recommender is assessed on a signal mechanically correlated with what it was trained on — a Goodhart's Law risk that may overstate recommendation effectiveness relative to real deployment. This is a preprint submitted to UMAP'26; peer-reviewed results are not yet available. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: SMORES is Python, open source, and runs on standard hardware with no special infrastructure requirements. - Efficient: Runtime and computational cost are not reported; training recommenders once per cycle on datasets of 7,000–48,000 consumers is tractable but scalability is not characterized. - Effective: In simulation, niche consumers and providers benefit from algorithmic pluralism in most portability conditions; whether this holds in real deployments with real user behavior is untested. - Scalable: Tested on datasets of 7,723–47,899 consumers after k-core filtering; no experiments at larger scale and no analysis of how results vary with ecosystem size or number of recommenders. The work delivers an extended open-source simulation framework and DVC-tracked experiment configurations; it stops at paper form for real-world deployment. Strengths - The 2×2 portability design space (exclusivity × permanence) is conceptually clean, policy-grounded (GDPR-anchored), and maps naturally to real regulatory instruments — a more precise formulation than prior data portability literature. - The noise-contamination explanation for why exclusive conditions outperform portability-friendly ones is mechanistically coherent and independently testable: non-exclusive profiles dilute niche recommender training data, an effect strongest in ItemKNN (which extrapolates directly from individual profiles) and weakest in Goodreads (where niche/generic preferences barely differ). - Cross-validated breadth: 3 datasets × 3 algorithms × 4 portability conditions × 5 seeds provides a reasonable combinatorial sweep for a simulation paper, and the dataset selection covers meaningfully different niche sparsities (6% to 33%). - Open-source simulation platform with DVC-tracked preprocessing enables direct replication and extension. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly note: only two recommenders (organic market emergence is deferred to future work); simple threshold switching model (multi-arm bandit acknowledged as alternative); platform utility not modeled; trajectory data omitted for space; sensitivity to niche genre selection is unexamined. The authors confront these limitations directly and honestly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Closed evaluation loop: both click generation and consumer utility use the same genre-preference dot product, so the simulation measures how well the recommender recovers a known genre signal rather than how well it serves heterogeneous real users. This circularity may systematically overestimate benefit. - No statistical significance testing: with only 5 seeds and results reported as raw mean utilities and percent changes, many reported differences (especially the small positive/negative swings for generic consumers) may not be distinguishable from noise. - Binary ecosystem: the two-recommender (one generic, one niche) setup is an extreme simplification of a realistic middleware market and may not generalize to settings with multiple competing niche recommenders. - Niche genre share is a confound, not a variable: Goodreads' niche genre at 32.75% is arguably not niche, yet it is treated symmetrically with Amazon's 6.04% Platformer genre. The paper attributes differences in results to this factor post-hoc but does not sweep it systematically. - Cold-start handling is homogeneous: both non-exclusive conditions introduce generic user profiles into the niche recommender equally, but real portability systems might allow users to curate which data they transfer — a nuance absent from the model. Bottom Line Researchers working on algorithmic pluralism, recommender fairness, or data governance policy will find the counter-intuitive portability findings and their mechanistic explanation worth engaging with, but should treat the conclusions as simulation-derived hypotheses requiring validation against real user behavior before informing regulatory design.
Search Results 2604.21750 PDF
#63 — Innovative (65)

EvoRAG: Making Knowledge Graph-based RAG Automatically Evolve through Feedback-driven Backpropagation

arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces EvoRAG, a self-evolving KG-RAG framework that continuously refines a knowledge graph by propagating response-level feedback back to individual triplets via a gradient-based backpropagation mechanism over a differentiable path-selection model. - Why it's important: Static KGs in existing KG-RAG systems irreversibly accumulate irrelevant, outdated, and low-utility triplets that degrade multi-hop reasoning—errors from these sources account for over 50% of failures on two standard benchmarks, and no prior mechanism corrects them at triplet granularity using task feedback. - How it does it: Per-triplet contribution scores are treated as learnable parameters; their gradients are computed analytically through a softmax path-priority function, and an LLM-as-judge scores each retrieved reasoning path's utility conditioned on query, response, and feedback score. - The good: EvoRAG achieves 7.34% average accuracy improvement over strong KG-RAG baselines across RGB, MultiHop, and HotpotQA benchmarks, converges in ~6 iterations, reduces prompt length 4.6× over baselines, and maintains gains on a 1.04M-triplet KG with 5,000 queries. - The missing: The training set is synthetically generated from the same KG subgraphs as the test queries, creating a potential data leakage concern the authors do not fully address; no statistical significance tests accompany gains as small as 3.33% on HotpotQA. - Read it or not: Researchers working on adaptive KG maintenance, feedback-driven retrieval, or closed-loop RAG systems should read this; practitioners with truly open-domain, non-concentrated query workloads should first verify whether the concentrated-query assumption holds in their setting.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (72) — formal gradient backprop over discrete KG retrieval via differentiable path-priority is a genuinely fresh framing. Rigor (55) — no significance tests on 3.33–6.08% gains over 600 instances, single seed implied, training data sampled from same KG subgraphs as test queries (leakage risk), and Qwen used for generation, judging, and feedback (circularity). Non-Obviousness (75) — the closed-form gradient derivation through softmax path priorities is non-trivial. Completeness (60) — concentrated-query assumption underpins the whole evaluation but isn't tested in disjoint-region settings. Resilience (55) — leakage and concentrated-query concerns weaken claims. Impact (75) — open-source, runs on commodity-ish hardware, scales to 1M triplets. Composite ≈ 65.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 60 Resilience 55 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation KG-RAG systems retrieve multi-hop reasoning paths from offline-constructed knowledge graphs to augment LLM generation. The KG is built once from a corpus and never updated during deployment. This creates three systematic error types measured empirically on RGB and MultiHop datasets: irrelevant facts retrieved via semantic similarity but contributing nothing to the answer (~17.9% of errors), failure to capture long-range dependencies beyond the pre-configured hop limit (~20.7%), and persistence of outdated or incorrect triplets (~11.9%). The paper observes that in real deployments, many users query overlapping KG subgraphs (hotspot regions around popular entities or events), so these per-query errors compound systematically. Existing KG refinement methods—embedding-based (TransE, RotatE) and LLM-based (LLM_sim)—require fine-grained triplet-level labels or operate offline without task-specific feedback, making them poorly suited to online KG-RAG adaptation. Key Idea The key insight is to treat each triplet's contribution score as a learnable scalar parameter and derive its gradient through a softmax-based path-priority function that explicitly depends on those scores. This enables backpropagation of response-level feedback to individual triplets—an analog to neural network gradient descent applied to a discrete, non-parametric knowledge store. The intermediate abstraction of reasoning paths (rather than direct triplet scoring) resolves the granularity mismatch between coarse response feedback and fine-grained triplet updates, because a triplet's contribution is context-dependent and best measured within the path it participates in. Methodology EvoRAG operates in four stages. (1) Feedback generation: an LLM (Qwen2.5-32B by default) evaluates each generated response and assigns a scalar satisfaction score 1–5, achieving 88–96% agreement with ground truth across datasets. (2) Path-level utility evaluation: given the query, retrieved paths, generated response, and feedback score, an LLM assigns each path a utility U ∈ [-1,1] decomposed into Supportiveness, Fidelity, and Conflict dimensions. A cross-validation guard suppresses Supportiveness updates unless Fidelity is high and Conflict is low, preventing spurious credit assignment. (3) Gradient backpropagation: path priority P(L_i) is defined as a softmax over the length-normalized log-average of per-triplet selection probabilities P(t) = (1-α)S_r(t) + αS_c(t), where S_r is semantic relevance (query-time) and S_c is the learnable contribution score (cross-query). Loss L = -log(E[U(L)]) is minimized by computing analytic gradients of L w.r.t. S_c(t) and the mixing parameter α, then applying gradient descent. The paper provides a convergence sketch: the loss is convex over path distributions and the gradient is Lipschitz-continuous through the softmax, yielding convergence to a stationary point under standard learning-rate conditions. (4) Relation-centric KG evolution: after each feedback iteration, triplets scoring above μ+σ trigger parallel BFS to identify high-utility multi-hop paths and create shortcut edges (relation fusion), reducing effective reasoning depth; triplets below μ-σ are progressively downweighted in retrieval probability (relation suppression, not hard deletion). Critical design decisions: using paths as intermediates (well-justified by context-dependence of triplet utility), relation-centric rather than entity-centric updates (avoids cascading effects from shared entities), and KV-cache reuse (forward and backward prompts share a common prefix, reducing backpropagation overhead to 23.90% of total runtime at batch size 20). Key Results Three datasets: RGB (300 single-hop queries, 54,544 entities, 74,394 triplets), MultiHop/MTH (816 multi-hop, 30,953 entities), HotpotQA/HPQ (600 multi-hop, 76,280 entities). Baselines: Microsoft GraphRAG (MRAG), LightRAG (LRAG), KRAG (from Lego-GraphRAG, 2025), and KRAG augmented with TransE, RotatE, CAGED, or LLM_sim. EvoRAG ACC: RGB 84.00% (vs. best baseline LRAG 76.00%, +8.00%); MTH 80.26% (vs. best CAGED+KRAG 76.72%, +3.54%); HPQ 48.16% (vs. best LRAG 44.83%, +3.33%). Average gains: 7.34% ACC, 9.84% EM, 7.29% F1 over KG-RAG baselines; 13.80% ACC over KGR-augmented methods. Notably, KGR baselines TransE and LLM_sim degrade below vanilla KRAG on MTH and HPQ, showing DL-based KGR methods are harmful in this setting without fine-grained triplet labels. At 20% injected feedback noise, accuracy drops only 2.43%. On a large-scale test (5,000 queries, 890,389 entities, 1,043,360 triplets), EvoRAG maintains 8.25% average ACC gain. Backpropagation accounts for only 15.35% of total runtime at scale. The cross-validation ablation shows 2.24% gain from the Fidelity/Conflict guards. Prompt length is reduced 4.6× over MRAG/LRAG/KRAG, yielding consistently lower time-to-first-token. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuine novelty is the formalization of gradient-based backpropagation over a discrete KG retrieval process—deriving closed-form gradients of an expected-utility loss with respect to per-triplet contribution scores. This is new in the KG-RAG literature. The individual components (LLM-as-judge, contribution scoring, hybrid retrieval, relation fusion) each have antecedents; the contribution is their principled integration under a unified differentiable objective. The convergence analysis and noise tolerance arguments add theoretical grounding absent from related adaptive RAG work. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover the feedback mechanism (four settings from LLM-only to full EvoRAG), cross-validation guards, hyperparameter sensitivity (N entities, M paths), feedback source (LLM vs. human vs. F1), noise injection (10%, 20% flipped feedback), alternative LLM backends (Llama-3.1-70B, GPT-4o-mini), and scalability. No error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported anywhere—a notable gap when gains on HPQ are 3.33%–6.08% with only 600 test instances. Code is released at https://github.com/iDC-NEU/EvoRAG. The manual annotation of "problematic triplets" (Section 6.3) does not report annotator count, guidelines, or inter-annotator agreement. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2604.15676v1, April 2026), not peer-reviewed. Feedback generation uses Qwen2.5-32B, the same model family used for response generation and path scoring, introducing a potential circularity where systematic Qwen biases compound rather than cancel. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: Runs on 2× NVIDIA RTX A6000 (48 GB each) plus 503 GB DRAM; commercially available but not commodity hardware. Path evaluation requires a 32B LLM call per feedback iteration, making purely CPU-based or API-budget-constrained deployments expensive. - Efficient: Backpropagation overhead is 23.90% of total runtime at batch size 20 via KV-cache prefix reuse; prompt length is reduced 4.6× over baselines, lowering inference latency. Gradient computation is O(2P + H²P + T), which is lightweight relative to LLM calls. - Effective: Consistent accuracy gains across all three datasets, two alternative LLM backends, three feedback sources, and a concentrated-query deployment simulation. The 83.01% suppression of manually annotated problematic triplets on RGB provides a mechanistic account of why gains occur. - Scalable: Tested at 5,000 queries on a KG with 1.04M triplets with maintained 8.25% ACC gain; backpropagation remains 15.35% of runtime at scale. No test at 10×–100× this KG size. The relation-fusion BFS is parallelized, but complexity at very large triplet counts is not characterized. The system delivers a working open-source software artifact. Strengths - The gradient-backpropagation framing is conceptually rigorous and yields closed-form, analytically tractable updates—not a heuristic scoring heuristic—with a sketch of convergence guarantees. - The three-dimensional path evaluation with Fidelity/Conflict cross-validation guards is a principled design that isolates reliable credit assignment signals; the 2.24% ablation gain confirms it does meaningful work. - Relation fusion directly addresses the long-range dependency failure mode by creating shortcut edges between high-utility multi-hop path endpoints, reducing effective retrieval depth without requiring manual hop-count tuning. - The framework transfers to chunk-level retrieval (MRAG, LRAG) with a 1.92%–2.83% gain, and generalizes across LLM backends and feedback sources, suggesting the mechanism is not brittle to specific implementation choices. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state that the evolution module covers relation refinement only and does not add or remove entities; entity-level factual correction requires external knowledge verification beyond feedback-based optimization, and they flag this as future work. They also acknowledge that feedback can be noisy and coarse-grained but argue cumulative aggregation mitigates this—a claim they partially validate empirically. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Training/test leakage risk: The training set is generated by sampling alternative reasoning paths from the same KG subgraphs used to answer test queries. The authors claim different paths are used, but EvoRAG's contribution scores are tuned on KG regions directly adjacent to test answers. This is a significant methodological concern that could inflate reported gains; the paper does not provide a held-out-subgraph evaluation. - No significance testing: Gains on HPQ are 3.33%–6.08% ACC with 600 test instances; without confidence intervals or paired significance tests, these margins may not be reliable, especially given the single random seed implied by the experimental setup. - Concentrated-query assumption: The entire evaluation simulates multi-user access to overlapping KG hotspots. The paper does not evaluate EvoRAG in a diverse open-domain setting where queries hit disjoint KG regions, where contribution scores would have far fewer cross-query updates to aggregate. - Feedback availability at training time: The framework requires ground-truth answers or expert annotations during the training phase to calibrate the LLM-based feedback. In deployments without labeled data or human raters, it is unclear how EvoRAG would bootstrap. - KG construction quality is uncontrolled: All KGs are built by GPT-4o-mini from chunked text; the initial error rate in extracted triplets is not characterized, and EvoRAG's gains may depend partially on the quality and error profile of GPT-4o-mini extractions rather than the evaluation datasets alone. Bottom Line EvoRAG delivers a principled and empirically validated mechanism for adaptive KG refinement in concentrated-query deployments, but the training/test subgraph overlap and absent significance tests mean the reported margins should be treated with moderate confidence until an independent evaluation confirms generalization to truly open-domain workloads.
Recommended 2604.15676 PDF
#64 — Innovative (65)

Claw-Eval-Live: A Live Agent Benchmark for Evolving Real-World Workflows (16 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 1 May 2026
