TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Claw-Eval-Live, a live benchmark for LLM workflow agents that derives its task distribution from public workflow demand signals (ClawHub Top-500 skill popularity) and grades agents on observable execution evidence rather than final-text plausibility. - Why it's important: Static agent benchmarks freeze task distributions at publication time and score only final responses, causing silent drift from real automation demand and masking agents that produce fluent output without actually executing required actions. - How it does it: A refreshable signal layer (updated quarterly from ClawHub popularity snapshots) is separated from a fixed time-stamped release snapshot; tasks are selected via MILP to maximize pilot-model discrimination while covering all 22 task families, and graded deterministically from execution traces, audit logs, and post-run workspace state. - The good: The result that no model clears 70% pass rate — with HR/management workflows near-zero and workspace repair near-ceiling — provides a concrete and actionable capability map that a static, author-curated task set would not have surfaced. - The missing: GPT-5.4 is simultaneously an evaluated model and the LLM judge for semantic grading dimensions — a direct conflict of interest the paper acknowledges but addresses only by limiting judge scope, with no cross-judge validation using a model absent from the leaderboard. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers and practitioners building or benchmarking workflow agents who need a quantified breakdown of where frontier models fail on real enterprise tasks; less necessary for those focused on code agents or single-turn evaluation.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Novelty (70) is solid — signal-driven, refreshable construction with MILP-based discrimination-aware selection is principled and uncommon. Rigor (55) is the main drag: no ablations on the construction pipeline, no inter-rater statistics, the GPT-5.4 judge conflict is acknowledged but unresolved with no neutral-judge validation, and the proprietary ClawHub signal is unverifiable. Non-Obviousness (65) reflects the MILP framing. Completeness (70) is reasonable — limitations confronted directly. Resilience (55) is weakened by the judge conflict, single-release evidence (which can't demonstrate the temporal property the paper claims), and ~61 effectively discriminative tasks. Impact (70) reflects a released benchmark artifact and rerunnable pipeline. Weighted: 0.15*70+0.15*55+0.10*65+0.15*70+0.15*55+0.30*70 ≈ 65.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 70 Resilience 55 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM agent benchmarks face two structural problems. First, static benchmarks freeze a task mixture at publication time; as enterprise tool stacks evolve and automation priorities shift, that mixture becomes stale while remaining reproducible — the benchmark measures yesterday's demand. Second, grading final responses cannot verify whether an agent actually called the required APIs, updated the correct records, or repaired the intended artifact; a polished output can conceal complete execution failure. Both problems compound in deployed business workflows, which combine cross-service state manipulation (CRM lookups, calendar writes, ticket updates) with local workspace repair (file edits, shell commands, test verification). Existing benchmarks address one surface or the other, and none derive their task distribution from external demand signals. Key Idea: The benchmark separates construction into two layers with different time scales: a refreshable signal layer that reads workflow popularity from ClawHub Top-500 skill downloads and translates it into a distributional prior over task families, and a fixed release layer that materializes that prior into executable tasks with fixtures, controlled services, and deterministic graders. The release is reproducible; the construction pipeline is rerunnable. This decoupling allows future releases to absorb demand shifts without invalidating prior evaluations. Methodology: The signal-to-task pipeline has five stages. Stage 1 collects a time-stamped ClawHub Top-500 snapshot, treating each skill as a public workflow demand signal. Stage 2 clusters related signals into workflow patterns by user goal, target artifact, and execution surface (33 patterns collapse to 6 signal families). Stage 3 converts normalized signal mass into family weights (w_f = sum of pattern masses in family / total mass) used as distributional priors for task seeding. Stage 4 expands weighted patterns into executable task candidates, each with a YAML task definition, RESTful mock services that load fixture data and record audit logs, and a task-specific grader.py inheriting from AbstractGrader. Candidates pass pilot screening only if they are runnable, reproducible, and produce score variation. Stage 5 selects the public release using a MILP over 157 screened candidates: maximize pairwise pilot-ordering preservation across K=5 pilot models, subject to N=105 tasks, at least one task per fine-grained family, and zero-discrimination exclusions. Grading uses a deterministic-first hybrid: tool dispatch logs verify data retrieval (15–20% of task score), ground-truth fixture comparison verifies data accuracy (40–60%), and service audit trails or post-run workspace state verify action completion (10–20%); GPT-5.4 judges semantic dimensions only when deterministic signals are insufficient. Workspace repair tasks use fully deterministic script-first verification. Pass threshold is τ=0.80; models are ranked by Pass Rate, ties broken by Overall Completion Score (mean score × 100). Key Results: The best model, Claude Opus 4.6, reaches 66.7% pass rate (70/105 tasks) with overall completion 83.6; GPT-5.4 follows at 63.8% and 81.7. No model clears 70%. The leaderboard spans 22.9 percentage points from top (Opus 4.6) to bottom (Doubao Seed 2.0, 43.8%). Task family structure is more informative than aggregate rank: Development/Terminal tasks (18 tasks) score 100% for Opus 4.6, GPT-5.4, and Sonnet 4.6; HR/People tasks (9 tasks) average only 6.8% across all 13 models with several at 0.0%; MGMT is all-fail under the public pass rule. The service-backed surface (87 tasks) sees a best pass rate of 59.8% (Opus 4.6), while workspace repair (18 tasks) sees all models above 72.2% with several near 100%. Under the public pass rule, 27 tasks are all-fail and 19 are all-pass, concentrating discrimination in a middle band. Among top-ranked models, GPT-5.4 consumes the fewest tokens (1.26M vs. 3.32M for Opus 4.6) at an estimated API cost of $6.27 vs. $31.61. Novelty Classification: NEW BENCHMARK / NEW SYSTEM. The core novelty is the signal-driven, refreshable benchmark construction pipeline — deriving task distributions from external popularity data rather than author judgment is a principled and uncommon design choice. The MILP-based discrimination-aware subset selection is a concrete methodological contribution that makes curation auditable. The combination of service-backed and workspace repair under one signal-calibrated release is a scope contribution. The grading methodology (deterministic-first, LLM-for-semantics-only) is not novel in principle but is applied carefully. The individual controlled services and mock fixtures are engineering execution rather than conceptual contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations are presented on the signal-to-task pipeline itself — neither the effect of different signal sources nor alternative MILP objective formulations are evaluated. No inter-rater agreement or reliability statistics are reported for the LLM judge component. The full 105×13 score matrix is released as raw_matrix_v3.csv, and the project page (claw-eval-live.github.io) is referenced, but the completeness of the public artifact release is not specified in the paper. The GPT-5.4 judge conflict is acknowledged but resolved only by scope restriction; no validation against human annotation or an independent judge model is presented. The ClawHub Top-500 signal source is proprietary and not independently verifiable, which limits external replication of the construction pipeline's validity claim. This is a preprint; results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check): SYSTEMS profile — the paper presents and evaluates a benchmark infrastructure. - Practical: The evaluation infrastructure runs against controlled mock services and sandboxed workspaces accessible via API, requiring no specialized hardware. - Efficient: Full evaluation of 13 models over 105 tasks costs $0.56–$31.61 per model at list prices and 79–241 minutes wall time; the MILP over 157 binary variables is tractable with off-the-shelf solvers. - Effective: The benchmark produces a 22.9 pp spread across models and reveals structured failure patterns by task family, demonstrating it measures meaningful capability differences beyond general language fluency. - Scalable: The quarterly refresh pipeline scales linearly with screened candidates; MILP tractability at larger candidate pool sizes is not analyzed. Task counts per family remain small (1–17), making family-level statistics noisy as the benchmark stands. The benchmark delivers a software artifact (YAML task definitions, grader.py files, mock service fixtures, score matrix CSV) and a rerunnable construction pipeline. Strengths: - Signal-driven task construction is a principled antidote to author-snapshot bias; anchoring the task distribution to an external popularity proxy makes the distributional prior externally inspectable and updateable. - MILP-based discrimination-aware subset selection converts subjective curation into an auditable optimization with explicit size, coverage, and ordering constraints — a methodological transparency step that most benchmark papers skip. - Deterministic-first hybrid grading reduces judge gaming surface area: the LLM judge cannot override hard evidence from service audit logs or workspace state, and its scope is explicitly bounded to semantic dimensions only. - The execution surface comparison (workspace repair near-ceiling vs. service-backed workflows below 60% for every model) is a concrete, actionable finding that reshapes where agent research effort should focus. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge GPT-5.4 judge bias, noting they mitigate it by limiting LLM judging to semantic dimensions and grounding every judge input in collected traces and rubrics. They note the 105-task release is modest in scale and position each release as a time-stamped snapshot rather than a permanent capability definition. ClawHub signals are explicitly described as proxies for workflow salience, not ground-truth deployment frequency, economic value, or task difficulty. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The live benchmark's core value proposition depends entirely on ClawHub Top-500 popularity data, which is proprietary, potentially gameable, and not independently verifiable. If skill downloads reflect developer curiosity or marketing rather than enterprise automation demand, the external grounding claim is weakened and the paper provides no validation of signal validity. - GPT-5.4 judge bias is not resolved: the paper presents no comparison against a neutral judge (e.g., a model absent from the leaderboard) or human annotation, so the magnitude of judge self-favoritism on semantic dimensions remains unmeasured. - 44 of 105 released tasks are non-discriminative under the public pass rule (27 all-fail, 19 all-pass), reducing the effective evaluation set to approximately 61 tasks — smaller than the headline count implies. - The paper presents only one release; the defining property of a live benchmark (tracking demand shifts across releases and demonstrating that rankings change) is claimed but not empirically demonstrated. - Several task families have extremely small counts (SEC: 1, R: 1, DOC: 1, C: 2), making family-level pass rates highly sensitive to single-task scoring artifacts and statistically unreliable. - The pass threshold τ=0.80 is not justified, ablated, or compared to alternatives; different thresholds would shift the all-fail/all-pass balance and potentially reorder the leaderboard. - No human baseline is reported for any task family, making it impossible to determine whether MGMT (all-fail) and HR (<7% average) represent genuine agent capability limits or flawed task design. Bottom Line: A practitioner evaluating workflow agents for enterprise deployment gains a useful structured failure map and a more honest grading methodology from this work, but the live/refreshable claim rests on a proprietary signal source that cannot be independently validated, the single-release paper cannot demonstrate the benchmark's defining temporal property, and the effective discrimination base of ~61 tasks is too narrow to support confident fine-grained conclusions.
Recommended 2604.28139 PDF
#65 — Innovative (65)

Small Model as Master Orchestrator: Learning Unified Agent-Tool Orchestration with Parallel Subtask Decomposition

arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ParaManager, a 4B-parameter LLM orchestrator trained via SFT and GRPO-based RL to decompose complex queries into parallel subtasks and delegate them to a unified pool of agents and external tools under a standardized protocol. - Why it's important: Existing multi-agent orchestration systems rely on serial execution and incompatible tool/agent interfaces, creating bottlenecks and brittle long-horizon trajectories; a small, learnable orchestrator that issues parallel verified calls at lower latency than serial alternatives would reduce both failure rate and wall-clock time. - How it does it: The Agent-as-Tool paradigm exposes every agent and tool through the same structured input/output/status schema, collapsing the heterogeneous action space to a single learnable interface; ParaManager then issues parallel batched calls, receives structured execution feedback, and self-repairs format/tool failures in subsequent rounds. - The good: The full model achieves mean@8 average accuracy of 70.48 across 8 benchmarks — 4.0 points above the best serial orchestration baseline (ToolOrchestra 66.21) and 3.6 points above the best single-model majority vote (GPT-OSS-20B 66.88) — while delivering lower latency (528s vs. 757s for ToolOrchestra) at comparable token count. - The missing: All benchmarks, including the nominally "out-of-domain" ones (GPQA, MMLU-pro, LiveCodeBench), use structured closed-form answers; there is no evaluation on open-ended agentic tasks (web navigation, multi-step tool use with real retrieval, code generation pipelines), leaving the core claim of general orchestration capability untested. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building tool-augmented or multi-agent systems who want a concrete recipe for training a compact orchestrator with RL; practitioners primarily interested in coding or open-ended agents should treat the results as math-domain evidence only.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (72) — Agent-as-Tool interface unification combined with masked GRPO for parallel scheduling is a genuinely novel system-level integration. Rigor (60) pulled down by no error bars across mean@8 (AIME has 24-25 problems, nontrivial variance), only 50 RL steps with no convergence curves on main metrics, preprint with no public release. Non-Obviousness (70) — interface normalization as prerequisite for end-to-end parallel-call learning is a non-obvious framing. Completeness (55) — no explicit limitations section, no length-normalization analysis on importance ratio variance. Resilience (55) — the 'general orchestration' claim is undermined because all 8 benchmarks are closed-form math/STEM. Impact (60) — trained 4B model but not released, requires multi-model serving infrastructure. Weighted: 0.20*72 + 0.25*60 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*60 ≈ 62. Bumped to 65 reflecting the budget-matched efficiency comparison and SFT-collapse evidence.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 55 Resilience 55 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Complex reasoning tasks benefit from combining multiple specialized agents and external tools, but current multi-agent systems (MAS) face two structural problems: (1) static workflows predetermined before execution cannot adapt to intermediate failures or partial results, and (2) heterogeneous tool/agent interface schemas force the orchestrator to handle incompatible formats, limiting both reliability and extensibility. In practice, tasks require stateful multi-round decisions driven by observed execution feedback, not pre-specified graphs. Existing learned orchestration (Puppeteer, ToolOrchestra) partially addresses (1) but still relies on serial scheduling and heterogeneous interfaces, making parallel cross-verification structurally difficult. Key Idea: The central insight is interface normalization as a prerequisite for learned parallel scheduling: if every callable unit — whether a code executor, a Wikipedia retriever, or a 30B reasoning agent — exposes the same (structured_input, structured_output, status_code) contract, then a single small LLM policy can learn to issue batched parallel calls across all of them, observe structured success/error feedback, and self-repair rather than fail silently. The combination of this normalized action space with masked GRPO (gradients applied only to orchestrator tokens, not tool outputs) and SFT initialization on failure-recovering trajectories is what allows stable RL training. Methodology: The system has four layers. (1) Tool-space unification (Agent-as-Tool): every tool Tk returns (value, status) where status ∈ {OK, PARSE_ERR, EXEC_ERR, TIMEOUT}; sub-agents are wrapped identically. (2) ParaManager policy: at each of up to 12 rounds, the orchestrator observes state st = (query, history, remaining_budget) and emits up to 4 parallel tool calls; structured feedback from invalid calls becomes explicit state signals for self-repair. (3) SFT initialization: 4,470 curated trajectories from Qwen3-30B-A3B running the framework; masked NLL loss covers only orchestrator tokens (not tool responses); ~fraction include recoverable error trajectories to teach self-repair. (4) RL with GRPO: reward R = R_task + R_fmt + R_div + R_eff. R_task is binary answer correctness. R_fmt is per-round format compliance. R_div combines a binary parallelism reward (avg parallel calls ≥ 1.25) and a tool-variety reward (≥3 unique tools). R_eff linearly penalizes token length and tool cost beyond soft targets (L_tar=12,288, C_tar=8 units). GRPO samples G=8 rollouts per instance, computes group-relative advantages, and clips with ε clipping; KL penalty is omitted following DAPO. Training runs 200 SFT steps and 50 RL steps on 12 H200 GPUs. Critical design decisions: (a) SFT-before-RL is well-justified — without it, the policy collapses to single-turn answering within ~20 RL steps as shown in Figure 3; (b) diversity reward is justified by ablation showing saturation to a dominant tool sequence without it; (c) masked GRPO is standard and well-motivated but the token-product importance ratio over a long multi-turn sequence may have high variance — not analyzed. Key Results: ParaManager-4B achieves mean@8 accuracy of 70.48% averaged across 8 benchmarks: AIME24 86.67%, AIME25 84.17%, AMC 95.18%, HMMT25 63.33%, GPQA 72.10%, MMLU-pro 81.43%, LCBv5 39.45%, LCBv6 41.51%. Baselines (best values): single-model GPT-OSS-20B majority vote 66.88%, static workflow EvoFlow 65.76%, serial orchestration ToolOrchestra 66.21%. Ablations isolate contributions: SFT-only 63.43 (−7.05 vs. full), mono-backbone 65.54 (−4.94), serial execution 66.48 (−4.00). In the unseen-model-pool generalization test, replacing the training pool with Gemini-2.5-flash + Qwen3-235B + GPT-OSS-120B, ParaManager achieves 86.52% average vs. best competing orchestration baseline ToolOrchestra 81.90%. Budget analysis shows ParaManager uses 55,063 tokens at 528.84s vs. the serial variant at 46,801 tokens but 660.32s — confirming that parallelism improves wall-clock efficiency despite modestly more tokens. Baselines share the same tool suite and model pool; comparison uses officially released weights for Puppeteer and ToolOrchestra. No error bars or significance tests are reported across the 8× mean@8 evaluation. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The Agent-as-Tool protocol normalization combined with parallel-call RL training for orchestration is genuinely novel as a system design. Individual components are prior work: GRPO (DeepSeekMath), masked trajectory optimization (standard), SFT-then-RL pipelines (common), tool-augmented reasoning (Search-R1, ToolOrchestra). The genuine novelty is the integration: treating heterogeneous agents under a unified schema enables end-to-end learning of parallel scheduling, which prior work did not achieve. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and informative — the SFT vs. no-SFT comparison (Figure 3), serial vs. parallel execution, mono vs. heterogeneous agent pool, and Ctar sensitivity (Table 6) each isolate a meaningful factor. The diversity reward ablation (Figure 6) is credible. No error bars or confidence intervals are reported anywhere despite mean@8 being an aggregate; for AIME24/25 (24 or 25 problems per set), variance in 8-sample mean is non-negligible. The RL training is extremely brief (50 steps, batch 16), raising questions about convergence stability. No code, model weights, or data are released as of this preprint. The Wikipedia-only retrieval corpus is a non-trivial constraint for the knowledge searcher, not disclosed as a limitation. Evaluation on math-dominated benchmarks (6 of 8 datasets are math/science competition or STEM) limits generalization claims. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: ParaManager itself (4B) runs on commodity hardware, but the full system requires simultaneous inference access to multiple 20–30B parameter models and depends on a sandboxed code executor and Wikipedia retrieval service, making self-hosted deployment non-trivial. - Efficient: Median trajectory consumes ~55,063 tokens across multiple parallel calls to 20–30B models, which is substantially more expensive per query than a single model call; the reported latency advantage (529s vs. 757s for ToolOrchestra) comes from parallelism, not reduced total compute. - Effective: Evidence is strong within the math/STEM benchmark domain, with consistent gains over both static and learned baselines; the out-of-domain benchmarks (GPQA, MMLU-pro, LiveCodeBench) all use short structured answers, not open-ended generation or multi-step agentic completion, so the claim of general orchestration is not fully substantiated. - Scalable: Generalization to unseen larger models (Gemini-2.5-flash, Qwen3-235B, GPT-OSS-120B) is tested on four math datasets only; no evaluation exists at higher task complexity, longer horizons, or tool pools larger than the 7-tool + 3-model setup used in training. The work delivers a trained model (Qwen3-4B-Instruct fine-tuned) but no public release is announced in this preprint. Strengths: - The interface unification idea (Agent-as-Tool) is clean and makes end-to-end learning of parallel scheduling tractable in a principled way; this is the kind of insight that generalizes beyond the specific models tested. - The SFT-before-RL design decision is empirically validated with a clear collapse curve (Figure 3), not just asserted; including recoverable error trajectories in SFT is a well-motivated and novel training signal. - The diversity reward design prevents tool collapse (Figure 6) while the joint efficiency regularization ensures it does not induce redundant tool stacking — an important and non-obvious interaction. - The budget-matched efficiency comparison (Table 2) directly addresses the confound that better performance could simply reflect more compute, and shows ParaManager achieves lower latency than the best serial baseline at similar token count. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The paper does not include an explicit limitations section. The conclusion acknowledges that results are on math and out-of-domain benchmarks but does not enumerate scope boundaries. The authors note that without SFT initialization RL collapses, and that diversity rewards prevent path collapse, implicitly acknowledging these as failure modes rather than solved problems. The Wikipedia-only retrieval restriction and the narrow tool vocabulary are disclosed in the experimental setup but not framed as limitations. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - All 8 evaluation benchmarks require short structured answers (numeric or multiple-choice); the claim that the learned scheduling policy "transfers beyond math" is undermined by the fact that GPQA, MMLU-pro, and LiveCodeBench are also multiple-choice or pass@k code tasks, not open-ended agentic problems. - No comparison against majority voting at an equivalent total compute budget (e.g., 8× calls to the best single model is one configuration; how many total model tokens does the orchestrated system consume vs. 8× GPT-OSS-20B?). - The RL training runs only 50 steps with batch size 16 — roughly 800 gradient steps total — which is unusually brief and the convergence curves are not shown for the main evaluation metrics, only for training-set accuracy and reward proxies. - The token-product importance ratio in masked GRPO over long multi-turn sequences (Eq. 20) can suffer from very high or low variance when trajectories differ substantially in length; this is not analyzed or addressed with length normalization. - The generalization test uses a single evaluation domain (math) with models that are all frontier-class instruction-tuned LLMs; there is no test with significantly weaker or differently-capable models, or with a tool pool that includes truly novel tool types not seen during training. Bottom Line: A practitioner building math-reasoning or STEM multi-agent pipelines will gain concrete, actionable insights from this paper — particularly the Agent-as-Tool protocol design, the SFT-on-failure-recovery strategy, and the diversity reward; researchers in general agentic AI should treat the results as promising but domain-constrained evidence pending evaluation on open-ended, long-horizon tasks.
Recommended 2604.17009 PDF
#66 — Innovative (65)

Synthetic Computers at Scale for Long-Horizon Productivity Simulation (8 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 1 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Synthetic Computers at Scale, a methodology for generating persona-grounded, artifact-rich computer environments with realistic file hierarchies and cross-file dependency graphs, and running month-long productivity simulations on them to produce experiential learning signals for AI agents. - Why it's important: Training long-horizon productivity agents requires realistic, user-specific work context (files, history, collaborator dynamics), which is impossible to collect at scale from real computers due to privacy constraints — without this, synthetic data degenerates into generic toy workflows. - How it does it: A persona is progressively elaborated into a user profile, filesystem policy, and artifact dependency graph; an LLM-based setup agent generates objectives and simulated collaborators; a work agent then executes 20 simulated workdays navigating the filesystem and coordinating with collaborators, producing both process trajectories and final deliverables. - The good: Occupation-specific skills extracted from 900 training simulations improve agent performance from 61.6% to 68.6% rubric score in-domain (83/100 test computers), and the same skills transfer out-of-domain to GDPVal (105 wins vs. 67 losses on 220 tasks, p=0.005 two-sided) despite GDPVal being structurally very different from the simulation setting. - The missing: No ablation isolates which components — dependency graph, collaborator simulation, weekly planning — actually drive the gains; the entire system is evaluated only as a monolith. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers building long-horizon agent training pipelines or synthetic data infrastructure; practitioners interested only in benchmark leaderboard results can skip it.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) high — persona-grounded filesystem synthesis with dependency graph and month-long simulation is a genuinely new framing. Non-Obviousness (75) elevated by the credible OOD transfer to GDPVal. Rigor (60) and Completeness (55) pulled down by total absence of component ablations and undisclosed compute cost. Resilience (60) limited by circular evaluation risk — same Claude family produces, evaluates, and judges. Impact (65) reflects the released dataset but no library/weights. Weighted: 0.20*75 + 0.25*60 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*65 = 65.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 55 Resilience 60 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Long-horizon AI agents operating over entire user computers — navigating filesystems, reading artifacts, coordinating with collaborators, and producing multi-week deliverables — need training data grounded in realistic user-specific environments. Real trajectories cannot be collected at scale because personal computers contain private enterprise documents, project state, and interaction history. Prior synthetic data approaches generate isolated tasks (e.g., GDPVal, OSWorld) rather than the full work context that conditions realistic productivity behavior: prior files, evolving project state, collaborator feedback, and cross-artifact dependencies. The gap is not missing tasks but missing environments. Key Idea: The core insight is "synthesize the context, not only the task." Rather than sampling a task and generating a prompt, the methodology first builds a user-specific computer — complete with a directed dependency graph of correlated artifacts reflecting realistic professional work history — and then conditions both objectives and agent behavior on that computer. The dependency graph (topologically ordered using Kahn's algorithm) ensures each file is generated with reference to its predecessors, so later artifacts genuinely build on earlier ones rather than being independent samples conditioned only on the persona. Methodology: The pipeline has two major stages. Computer creation: (1) A persona (brief professional description) is expanded into a detailed user profile covering identity, career stage, responsibilities, collaborators, tool preferences, and document habits. (2) A filesystem policy is derived, specifying drive layout, naming conventions, storage patterns, and organization style. (3) A filesystem plan enumerates files with paths, artifact types, timestamps, origins, and cross-file dependency edges. (4) Files are instantiated in dependency order — web-downloadable artifacts retrieved first, user-specific artifacts generated by an LLM agent equipped with Office-creation skills (MiniMax minimax-docx/xlsx, pptx-generator, minimax-pdf), each conditioned on its predecessor artifacts. Long-horizon simulation: A setup agent reads the user profile and computer state and infers month-long productivity objectives comprising 4–5 deliverable work packages with per-week milestones and expected output artifacts. The setup agent also generates simulated collaborators (managers, clients, peers, compliance officers) with roles, backgrounds, communication styles, and private reference files not initially visible to the work agent. A work agent then executes weekly planning cycles followed by 20 daily agent sessions, each session restoring context from the activity log and collaborator exchanges, producing files and sending messages. The simulation runtime uses Claude Code SDK; the work agent is Claude Sonnet 4.6, the setup agent Claude Opus 4.6. In-domain evaluation uses occupation-specific skills extracted from retrospective trajectory reports generated by an LLM analyzer, frequency-ranked across the 900 training simulations, and compiled into occupation-specific behavioral guides by a skill-creator agent. These skills are injected as context for the work agent on held-out test computers. Key Results: 1,000 synthetic computers were created and simulated. Pre-simulation computers averaged 112 files; post-simulation 197 files. Productivity artifacts (DOCX, XLSX, PDF, PPTX) account for 67.8% of all files. Each simulation averaged 2,272 agent turns and 8.59 hours wall-clock time with 5.5 simulated collaborators and ~31 communications per run. Rubric-based evaluation on 100 held-out computers: baseline score 61.6%, occupation-skill-augmented score 68.6% (+7.0 pp); skill-augmented wins 83/100 paired comparisons. Scaling trend: 10/100/500/900 training computers yield 50%/64%/75%/83% win rates, indicating the improvement monotonically increases with training coverage. Out-of-domain evaluation on GDPVal (220 tasks; avg 31 turns, 17 minutes, vs. the simulations' 2,272 turns and 8.59 hours): Sonnet-4.6 wins 105, loses 67 (p₂=0.005); Opus-4.6 wins 99, loses 71 (p₂=0.038); Haiku-4.5 wins 104, loses 80 (p₂=0.090, not significant two-sided). The retrospective analysis reveals that the dominant failure mode across all deliverables is cross-document numerical inconsistency, not failure to produce files. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The combination of persona-grounded filesystem synthesis, artifact dependency graph generation, and month-long multi-deliverable agent simulation with simulated collaborators is new. The finding that trajectory-derived occupation skills transfer out-of-domain to a structurally very different benchmark (GDPVal) is a non-obvious empirical result. However, the individual components — LLM-based persona expansion (building on Ge et al. 2024), skill extraction from trajectories, and rubric-based LLM judgment — are all combinations of existing ideas applied to a new setting. Rigor & Reproducibility: This is a preprint technical report, not peer-reviewed. No ablations isolate individual components (dependency graph, collaborator simulation, weekly planning structure, retrospective analysis quality). The rubric generation procedure is somewhat principled — five runs per computer are used to generate and merge draft rubrics, reducing idiosyncratic rubric bias — but the rubric judge (Claude Opus 4.6) is the same model family as the agents being evaluated, raising potential circular evaluation concerns. Error bars, confidence intervals, and significance tests are reported for the GDPVal out-of-domain comparison (sign tests) but not for the in-domain rubric scores. The 4.2% tool-error rate in agent turns (213 errors across 5,114 turns in the appendix example) is described qualitatively but not systematically analyzed across all 1,000 simulations. The dataset (100 synthetic computers, 500 retrospective reports) is publicly released on HuggingFace at huggingface.co/datasets/microsoft/synthetic-computers-at-scale; trained model weights and extracted skills are not released. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (a methodology and system evaluated by running agents on synthetic computers). - Practical: The pipeline runs on Claude Code SDK with commercially available models (Sonnet 4.6, Opus 4.6) and MiniMax open-source Office skills; no specialized hardware is required, but API access and sufficient quota are prerequisites. - Efficient: Each simulation averages 8.59 hours wall-clock time and 2,272 agent turns, making 1,000 simulations extremely expensive; the paper does not report total compute cost, token counts, or API spend, which is a significant omission for practitioners considering scaling. - Effective: The +7.0 pp in-domain improvement and statistically significant GDPVal gains are real signal; however, the absolute baseline score of 61.6% on LLM-judged rubrics leaves open how much of the gain reflects genuine productivity improvement versus rubric-gaming by the same model family. - Scalable: The paper claims the methodology can scale to billions of computers given sufficient compute, with personas available at that scale from prior work; however, only 1,000 computers are demonstrated, and the compute cost per simulation makes billion-scale deployment a distant extrapolation unsupported by current evidence. The work delivers a dataset artifact (100 synthetic computers, 500 reports) and a methodology description, but not a library, CLI, or trained model. Strengths: - The dependency graph over files is a genuine conceptual advance over treating synthetic artifacts as independent samples; it produces correlated, coherent work histories that better reflect real computer environments. - The collaborator simulation — including private reference materials that are only revealed through interaction, and intentional errors introduced by junior collaborators for the agent to catch — creates rich grounding challenges not present in existing benchmarks. - The out-of-domain transfer to GDPVal is the most credible result in the paper: GDPVal tasks are structurally very different (31 turns vs. 2,272 turns, no full computer context), yet the skills derived from computer-grounded simulations significantly improve performance, suggesting the extracted lessons generalize beyond the specific simulation setup. - The retrospective analysis appendix (Appendix A) provides unusually detailed, domain-specific diagnosis of agent failure modes — cross-document inconsistency, blank messages, end-of-context degradation — which are actionable and non-obvious. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly identify three future directions that implicitly acknowledge current limitations: (1) artifacts have uniform visual style across computers despite varying content (insufficient personalization of formatting); (2) filesystems are too clean and lack realistic noise (temporary downloads, abandoned files, clutter); (3) simulated collaborators are mostly reactive rather than having their own evolving state, deadlines, and proactive agendas. The authors also acknowledge that the web-downloadable file check is performed after planning, so some planned web downloads fall back to synthesis. These are treated as opportunities rather than deficiencies, but they represent real fidelity gaps relative to real computers. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No component ablation: it is unknown whether the dependency graph, the collaborator simulation, the weekly planning structure, or simply the quantity of synthetic data drives the in-domain improvement; a practitioner cannot know which components to implement. - Circularity in evaluation: the rubric judge (Opus 4.6), the work agent (Sonnet 4.6), and the skill-extraction model are all from the same Claude family; the GDPVal judge is also Claude Opus 4.6; this creates a risk that the extracted skills teach the agent to produce outputs that Opus judges as better without necessarily representing true productivity improvement. - Occupation imbalance: the 1,000 personas are heavily skewed toward Management & Executive (17.7%), Tech (13.7%), and Engineering (13.4%); occupations like Healthcare Support (2.2%), Production & Trades (2.0%), and Office & Admin (0.9%) are severely underrepresented, limiting the diversity claims. - No comparison to alternative synthetic data baselines: there is no comparison to simpler approaches such as task-conditioned single-turn generation, OSWorld-style sandbox simulation, or other agentic data generation pipelines. - Compute cost is entirely absent: 1,000 simulations × 8.59 hours of Claude Opus/Sonnet usage is a substantial and unreported expense; the scalability claim requires this information to evaluate. - The rubric merging procedure (5 runs per computer) relies on the setup agent's objectives and collaborator expectations as ground truth, but these are themselves LLM-generated and may share systematic biases. Bottom Line: This paper makes a credible case that synthesizing realistic work context — not just tasks — is the right framing for long-horizon agent training data, and the out-of-domain GDPVal transfer results are the strongest evidence for this claim; researchers building productivity agent training pipelines should read it, though practitioners will need to wait for ablations, compute cost disclosure, and component-level analysis before adopting the full methodology.
Recommended 2604.28181 PDF
#67 — Innovative (65)

Recursive Multi-Agent Systems (123 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 29 Apr 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces RecursiveMAS, a framework that extends recursive latent-space computation from single language models to multi-agent systems by connecting heterogeneous LLM agents through lightweight two-layer residual projection modules (RecursiveLink), enabling agents to exchange hidden-state embeddings rather than decoded text across multiple recursion rounds. - Why it's important: Text-based multi-agent systems pay large vocabulary-decoding costs at every inter-agent communication step and suffer gradient vanishing during recursive training, limiting both efficiency and the ability to optimize agents as a unified system. - How it does it: An inner RecursiveLink maps each agent's last-layer embeddings back into its own input embedding space for latent thought generation; an outer RecursiveLink bridges heterogeneous embedding spaces between agents; a two-stage inner-outer training loop uses cosine-similarity regression to warm-start latent thought generation and cross-entropy on the final decoded output to backpropagate shared credit through the entire recursive computation graph. - The good: At r=3 recursion rounds, RecursiveMAS achieves 88.0% on MATH500, 86.7% on AIME2025 (Pass@10), and 66.2% on GPQA-Diamond using models of 1B–10B parameters, with 13.12M trainable parameters (0.31% of total) and lower GPU memory ($15.29 GB) and cost ($4.27) than both LoRA and full fine-tuning baselines. - The missing: AIME2025/2026 results are reported as Pass@10 rather than Pass@1, making the headline 86.7% figures substantially more optimistic than single-attempt comparisons would yield; the authors do not report Pass@1 numbers, obscuring a direct apples-to-apples comparison against single-inference baselines. - Read it or not: Researchers working on multi-agent LLM systems, test-time compute scaling, or latent-space communication between models should read this; practitioners deploying API-based or closed-weight agents can skip it, as the method requires white-box access to all agents' hidden states.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) is the strength — extending recursive latent computation from single models to heterogeneous MAS with theoretical motivation is a genuine architectural contribution. Non-Obviousness (70) is high because the gradient-stability argument and residual-projection trick reveal real structure. Rigor (55) is meaningfully weakened: AIME headline numbers are Pass@10 mixed into Pass@1 tables, the gradient-stability theorem assumes W3=I but the deployed module trains W3, the inner-loop warmup is unablated, and the Recursive-TextMAS baseline is author-built. Completeness (55) is hurt by the unaddressed Expert-vs-Distillation gap and white-box requirement. Resilience (55) and Impact (60) are moderate. Weighted: 0.20*75 + 0.25*55 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*60 ≈ 61.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 55 Resilience 55 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multi-agent systems (MAS) for LLMs decompose complex tasks across specialized agents, but conventional text-mediated communication creates two compounding problems. First, each intermediate agent must decode its hidden states into tokens and the next agent must re-encode them, incurring O(m|V|d_h) vocabulary-projection cost per inter-agent message (where |V| is vocabulary size and m is generation length). Second, when agents are connected recursively to refine answers over multiple rounds, backpropagating through the text-decode/re-encode bottleneck produces near-zero gradients because the Jacobian of the softmax-embedding composition is bounded by the token distribution entropy, which approaches zero as the model becomes confident. Together these problems make recursive multi-agent training slow, memory-intensive, and prone to gradient vanishing, preventing the system from being co-optimized as a whole. Key Idea: The central trick is that agent hidden states live in continuous embedding spaces that can be projected directly between agents using a small residual network, bypassing the expensive decode-re-encode cycle. Treating each agent as a layer of a recursive language model, the paper chains agents into a closed loop where latent representations circulate for n rounds before the final agent decodes one textual output. This preserves gradient flow across the full loop (Jacobian norm near 1 rather than near ε) while replacing O(m|V|d_h) token-generation cost per step with O(md²_h) projection cost, where d_h << |V| in practice. Methodology: The RecursiveLink R is a two-layer residual MLP: inner variant R_in(h) = h + W2·σ(W1·h) maps last-layer embeddings back to the same agent's input embedding space; outer variant R_out(h) = W3·h + W2·σ(W1·h) adds a linear map W3 to handle dimension mismatch between heterogeneous agents. During a recursion round, each agent generates m latent steps auto-regressively using its inner link, passes the resulting latent thoughts through the outer link to the next agent, and the loop closes by feeding the last agent's outputs back to the first. Only in the final recursion round does the last agent decode text. Training proceeds in two stages: (1) inner-loop — each agent's inner RecursiveLink is trained in parallel by minimizing 1 - cosine_similarity(R_in(H), Emb(y)), aligning latent thoughts to ground-truth token embeddings without explicit decoding; (2) outer-loop — the full system is unrolled for n rounds and outer links are jointly optimized via cross-entropy on the final textual output, with gradients propagated recursively through the complete computation graph. All base LLM parameters are frozen; only RecursiveLink weights are updated. Four collaboration patterns are evaluated: Sequential (Planner-Critic-Solver), Mixture (domain specialists + summarizer), Distillation (Expert + Learner), and Deliberation (Reflector + Tool-Caller). Critical design decisions: (a) residual connections in RecursiveLink — well-justified as reducing the projection task to learning distributional shifts rather than full reconstruction; (b) cosine similarity for inner training — reasonable as a distribution-alignment loss, though the choice of ground-truth Emb(y) as target is not ablated against alternatives such as teacher-forcing from the frozen model; (c) freezing all LLM parameters — a practical constraint that limits expressivity but enables parameter efficiency. Key Results: Against the primary text-based recursive baseline (Recursive-TextMAS, same structural pattern but text communication), RecursiveMAS achieves average accuracy improvements of 8.1% at r=1, 19.6% at r=2, and 20.2% at r=3. Against the broader baselines at r=3 (Table 3): MATH500 88.0% vs. TextGrad 84.9% (best baseline); AIME2025 86.7% vs. 73.3%; AIME2026 86.7% vs. 76.7%; GPQA-Diamond 66.2% vs. 62.5%; LiveCodeBench 42.9% vs. 39.8%; MedQA 79.3% vs. 77.2%. Inference speedup over text-based recursion scales from 1.2× at r=1 to 2.4× at r=3; token reduction from 34.6% to 75.6%. Baselines include LoopLM (looped single model), TextGrad (text-gradient optimization), Mixture-of-Agents, and single-agent LoRA/full-SFT — these are a reasonable but not complete set; notably, no reasoning-specialized models such as DeepSeek-R1 or QwQ are compared at equivalent parameter counts. Evaluation scale is 1B–10B parameters across diverse Qwen/Llama/Gemma/Mistral families. A critical caution: AIME2025/2026 results are Pass@10, not Pass@1, making those headline numbers (86.7%) substantially higher than what single-inference comparisons would show. Mixture-style distillation results also show that RecursiveMAS (Learner+Expert, 83.3% on AIME2026) does not surpass the Expert alone (90.0%), a gap the paper does not discuss. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The framing of MAS as system-level recursive computation, the RecursiveLink architecture for cross-agent latent communication, and the inner-outer training paradigm with shared gradient credit are genuinely novel as a combined contribution. Individual components — residual adapters, latent-space communication, recursive depth scaling — appear in concurrent work (Du et al. 2025, Zou et al. 2025, Geiping et al. 2025), and the paper explicitly positions itself as the first to extend recursive scaling to the system level. The theoretical results (Proposition 3.1, Theorem 4.1) formalize known intuitions about vocabulary-decoding cost and softmax gradient collapse. Rigor & Reproducibility: Five independent runs are reported with standard deviations (±0.0041 accuracy, ±26s runtime, ±33 tokens) — acceptable but modest sample sizes. RecursiveLink architecture ablations (Table 4) are present and cleanly show the residual + 2-layer design is best. Latent thought length m is ablated (Table 9), showing saturation at m≈80. Training cost is directly compared to LoRA and full-SFT (Table 5). However, several gaps undermine rigor: (a) The gradient stability theorem (Theorem 4.1) is proved under the assumption W3=I for the outer RecursiveLink — the actual deployed outer link has W3≠I, so the theorem does not directly cover the claimed setting. (b) Recursive-TextMAS is an author-implemented baseline, raising possible implementation-gap concerns. (c) No ablation isolates the contribution of the two-stage curriculum (inner-loop warmup) from the latent communication mechanism — it is unclear whether the performance gain requires both components. (d) Pass@10 metric on AIME benchmarks is not flagged in the main tables, where results appear alongside Pass@1 metrics on other benchmarks. Code availability: a project page is referenced (https://recursivemas.github.io) but no code or model weights are explicitly confirmed as released in the preprint. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Runs on H100/A100 GPUs with sub-10B models; GPU memory per agent (15.29 GB) is within commercial hardware limits. However, the method requires simultaneous white-box access to all agents' hidden states, ruling out API-based or closed-model deployments. - Efficient: RecursiveLink adds only 13.12M parameters (0.31% overhead) and reduces token generation by 34–76% compared to text-based recursive MAS; absolute runtime at r=3 remains high (2000–10000s per benchmark run), partly due to the recursive multi-agent overhead. - Effective: Consistent, statistically stable improvements across 9 benchmarks and 4 collaboration patterns provide reasonable evidence of task-general benefit, though gains on AIME benchmarks are inflated by the Pass@10 metric. - Scalable: Only tested up to r=3 recursion rounds and models up to 10B; behavior at deeper recursion, larger models (70B+), or more than 4 agents is untested and unknown. The paper shows a scaling law plot (Figure 1) but only across r=1–4 for training and inference, not across model scale. The paper delivers a software framework (code claimed at project page) and trained RecursiveLink adapter weights, stopping short of a standalone library. Strengths: - The core architectural insight — replacing decode/re-encode with direct residual projection — simultaneously addresses both efficiency (complexity reduction) and trainability (gradient stability), and both claims are supported by theory and measurement. - The framework is genuinely structure-agnostic: four qualitatively different collaboration patterns (sequential, mixture, distillation, deliberation) all benefit, demonstrating non-trivial generality beyond a single task configuration. - Cost analysis (Table 5) showing RecursiveMAS outperforms both LoRA and full-SFT on accuracy while using fewer parameters, less GPU memory, and lower estimated cost is a strong practical result if it replicates. - The semantic distribution visualization (Figure 7) provides interpretable evidence that latent representations converge toward ground-truth distributions across recursion rounds, corroborating the accuracy improvements mechanistically. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note in passing that all LLM parameters are frozen and only RecursiveLink weights are updated, implicitly limiting agent-level learning. They do not explicitly call out the white-box model requirement, the Pass@10 metric inflation on AIME, the W3=I assumption in the gradient proof, or the Expert underperformance in distillation style. The paper confronts limitations minimally rather than directly. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) AIME2025/2026 Pass@10 results are mixed into main result tables alongside Pass@1 metrics without prominent disclosure — the most impressive headline numbers are measured under a more lenient protocol. (2) The outer RecursiveLink proof assumes W3=I (identity), but the actual deployed module trains W3 freely; the theoretical guarantee does not cover the real system. (3) No ablation separates the inner-loop warmup stage from the outer-loop optimization — a version that skips inner-loop training is absent, making it impossible to determine how much of the gain comes from the training curriculum versus the latent communication architecture. (4) The Recursive-TextMAS baseline is author-constructed and may not represent an optimally-tuned text-based recursive MAS. (5) Distillation-style RecursiveMAS (83.3%) underperforms the Expert alone (90.0%) on AIME2026 — the paper presents this as a speed-accuracy trade-off but does not explain the accuracy gap. (6) All agents require simultaneously loaded GPU hidden states, which substantially constrains memory-efficient deployment compared to the paper's per-agent memory figures. (7) No evaluation on long-horizon agentic tasks (multi-step tool use, code execution pipelines) where the sequential latency advantages would be most relevant. Bottom Line: RecursiveMAS delivers a technically coherent and broadly validated framework for latent-space recursive collaboration across heterogeneous agents, with genuine efficiency and accuracy gains, but practitioners should account for the Pass@10 metric inflation on AIME benchmarks, the white-box model requirement, and the unablated training curriculum before adopting it.
Recommended 2604.25917 PDF
#68 — Innovative (64)

Turning the TIDE: Cross-Architecture Distillation for Diffusion Large Language Models (36 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 30 Apr 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TIDE, the first cross-architecture knowledge distillation framework for diffusion LLMs, enabling transfer from heterogeneous large teachers (8B dense, 16B MoE) into a 0.6B student with a different architecture, attention mechanism, and tokenizer. - Why it's important: Competitive dLLMs require 8–16B+ parameters, blocking commodity deployment, and no prior method handles distillation across architectural boundaries — only step compression within the same model family. - How it does it: Three modular components jointly address temporal, spatial, and vocabulary barriers: TIDAL (dual-axis λ schedule over diffusion timestep and training progress), COMPDEMO (complementary mask splitting to give the teacher richer context at high noise), and Reverse CALM (inverted chunk-level BCE that yields bounded gradients under cross-tokenizer misalignment). - The good: The distilled 0.6B student reaches HumanEval 48.78 vs. 32.30 for a same-size AR baseline, with 22× memory reduction and 5× inference speedup over the LLaDA2 teacher, demonstrating concrete deployment value. - The missing: Evaluation is confined to a single 0.6B student architecture at 512 tokens; no cross-tokenizer component-level ablation exists, and the distilled model still substantially lags a same-size AR model on reasoning-heavy benchmarks (MATH: 13.20 vs. 32.40, GSM8K: 52.24 vs. 59.60). - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on diffusion LM efficiency, cross-architecture transfer, or deployment of parallel-decoding models; less useful for those focused on reasoning-capable small LLMs, where same-size AR models remain substantially stronger.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) is strong — first cross-architecture dLLM distillation framework, with Reverse CALM's gradient-bounding inversion and COMPDEMO's complementary mask splitting being non-obvious, dLLM-specific contributions. Rigor (60) is mixed: principled derivations and a thorough self-aware limitations section, but ablations only on the shared-tokenizer pipeline (not the best-performing cross-tokenizer one), 3 vs. 10 epoch ablation discrepancy, n=50 benchmark sampling, and possible test-set hyperparameter selection. Non-Obviousness (75) high — the Bernoulli-KL equivalence and dual-end noise filtering are genuinely insightful. Completeness (65) — distilled model still trails same-size AR on reasoning, no separation of architecture vs. distillation causes. Resilience (55) — single 0.6B scale, single architecture, weak reasoning results undermine the deployment story. Impact (70) reflects released weights/code and concrete 22× memory reduction. Composite: 0.20*75 + 0.25*60 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*70 = 65.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 65 Resilience 55 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Competitive diffusion language models (LLaDA 8B, WeDLM 8B, LLaDA2 16B–100B) require billions of parameters, making edge and commodity deployment impractical. Knowledge distillation is well-established for AR LMs but existing dLLM distillation methods (CDLM, DDD, LSD, SDTT) exclusively perform step compression — teacher and student share the same architecture and tokenizer, and only inference steps are reduced. When the teacher and student differ in architecture, attention type, and tokenizer, three fundamental barriers arise that prior work does not address: (1) teacher signal reliability varies with the diffusion noise level (the teacher is effectively guessing at high masking ratios), (2) heavy masking deprives the teacher of context, degrading the distillation signal, and (3) vocabulary incompatibility makes token-level KL divergence mathematically undefined. Key Idea The central insight is that dLLM distillation requires two things absent from AR distillation: a timestep-aware interpolation weight that suppresses teacher influence when the teacher is masking-unreliable, and a reversed-direction chunk BCE loss that eliminates gradient explosion under imperfect cross-tokenizer chunk alignment by making the gradient coefficient depend only on the fixed teacher (bounded) rather than on the student's evolving probabilities (potentially zero, causing divergence in the forward direction). Methodology TIDE trains a Qwen3-0.6B-BD3LM student (block diffusion with staircase attention) against frozen teachers on a four-dataset SFT mixture (Tulu-3, SmolTalk, OpenCoder-SFT), 10 epochs, 512-token sequences, bfloat16. Three components: (1) TIDAL: The distillation weight λ_t = λ_train × (1−t) combines a cosine schedule over normalized training progress p (λ_train from 0.1 to 0.9) with a linear factor (1−t) over diffusion timestep t. At t≈1 (heavy masking), λ_t≈0 and the CE loss dominates; at t≈0 (near-clean), the student fully mirrors the teacher. The TIDAL loss is D_KL(r_t ‖ softmax(s/T)) × T², where r_t is the softmax of the interpolated logits. An optional midrange Gaussian weight w(t) emphasizes t≈0.5. Design decision: detaching r_t from the computation graph prevents gradient flow through the mixture target — well-justified for stability. (2) COMPDEMO: The masked position set M is randomly partitioned into complementary halves M_A, M_B (ratio ρ=0.5). Two frozen-teacher forward passes: pass 1 reveals M_A as context and obtains logits at M_B; pass 2 is symmetric. Merged logits cover all masked positions with enriched context. This doubles teacher inference passes, adding ~50% training time (no gradients through the frozen teacher). The mechanism exploits the bidirectional attention of dLLMs and has no AR analog. (3) Reverse CALM: For cross-tokenizer settings, tokenkit performs byte-level chunk alignment, identifying minimal text spans containing complete tokens from both vocabularies. Per-token log-probabilities are aggregated into chunk-level scalars p_s^c, p_t^c via matrix multiplication (avoiding full softmax materialization). Forward BCE gradient ratio p_t^c/p_s^c diverges when p_s^c→0. Reverse CALM inverts the loss to L = −[p_s^c log p_t^c + (1−p_s^c)log(1−p_t^c)], whose gradient coefficient log(p_t^c/(1−p_t^c)) depends only on the fixed teacher. Chunks with p_t^c≈0.5 zero the gradient (teacher-side noise filtering); low p_s^c suppresses updates via small ∂p_s^c/∂θ (student-side filtering). The authors prove Reverse CALM is equivalent to Bernoulli KL(p_s^c‖p_t^c) — principled mode-seeking in scalar space. TIDAL and Reverse CALM are explicitly shown to be incompatible (TIDAL's late-stage suppression destroys Reverse CALM's self-selection). Two evaluated pipelines: (A) cross-tokenizer: LLaDA2.0-mini (16B MoE) → student, using Reverse CALM; (B) shared-tokenizer: WeDLM-8B-Instruct (8B dense) → student, using TIDAL + COMPDEMO. Key Results Across eight benchmarks (GSM8K, MATH, BBH, MMLU-Pro, HellaSwag, MMLU, HumanEval, MBPP): - Best TIDE configuration (cross-tokenizer TIDE-Cross): 34.20 average vs. non-distilled BD3LM baseline 32.67 (+1.53); shared-tokenizer TIDE-Shared: 33.55. - Both remain well below AR Qwen3-0.6B-Base at 40.91 average. - Code tasks: HumanEval 48.78 (TIDE-Shared, shared-tokenizer), 49.39 (TIDE-Shared swapped into cross-tokenizer), vs. AR baseline 32.30 (+16.48); MBPP 38.60 vs. 36.60. - Reasoning: GSM8K 52.24 vs. AR 59.60; MATH 13.20 vs. AR 32.40 — distilled model substantially weaker. - Ablation (shared-tokenizer, 3 epochs): removing timestep axis costs −0.26 avg (−3.05 HumanEval); removing COMPDEMO costs −0.17 avg; full method 33.14 vs. baseline 33.06. - Memory: 1.4 GB (student) vs. 31.3 GB (LLaDA2) = 22× reduction; 5.2× inference latency speedup over LLaDA2 (6.25 s vs. 32.55 s for 256 tokens on H100). - Distillation adds only 2.6% throughput overhead vs. undistilled BD3LM (41.0 vs. 42.1 tokens/s). - Dark knowledge transfer: TIDE-Shared reduces KL divergence to WeDLM teacher by 46% on GSM8K (6.69 vs. 12.44). Baselines are current (LLaDA2, WeDLM, BD3LM, Qwen3-0.6B from 2025–2026 preprints) but all are preprints, not peer-reviewed. Scale tested: 0.6B student only. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The work introduces the first cross-architecture distillation framework for dLLMs. Reverse CALM's inversion of chunk-level BCE for bounded cross-tokenizer gradients is non-obvious and principled; TIDAL's second (timestep) axis is a genuine extension over TAID's single-axis AR interpolation; COMPDEMO's complementary mask splitting has no AR analog. The combination — rather than any single component — constitutes the novel contribution. ALM (Minixhofer et al. 2025) is adapted from AR, not invented here. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations exist for the shared-tokenizer pipeline only; the cross-tokenizer pipeline (which achieves the best average score) has no component-level ablation — the contribution of Reverse CALM alone vs. baseline CALM is not isolated. Ablation experiments use 3 training epochs while main results use 10, creating a potential discrepancy in relative rankings. Best inference numbers are reported across 5 independent runs (controlled setting); evaluation setting uses only 50 randomly sampled examples per benchmark, leaving HumanEval variance (164 total problems) unclear. The student baseline and AR comparison are taken from Zhou et al. (2026), a concurrent preprint — results depend on their training recipe. Code and model weights are stated as publicly available on GitHub and HuggingFace. This is a preprint under review; all results are self-reported. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: The distilled 0.6B student runs in 1.4 GB peak memory on a single H100; the authors demonstrate deployment on commodity hardware as an explicit goal. Training requires two H100-equivalent GPUs for teacher inference. - Efficient: COMPDEMO adds ~50% training time; Reverse CALM adds negligible overhead at inference. Student throughput is 41.0 tokens/s vs. 51.3 for same-size AR (80%), an acceptable trade-off for the 22× memory reduction over the teacher. - Effective: Distillation improves over non-distilled BD3LM by +1.53 average and delivers large code gains (+16.48 HumanEval), but the student lags the same-size AR model by −6.7 average, particularly on MATH (−19.2 points) and GSM8K (−7.4 points) — the stated deployment advantage is real but task-dependent. - Scalable: Tested only at 0.6B; the authors explicitly acknowledge no 1.3B/3B experiments and list this as primary future work. Scaling behavior is claimed but not measured. Delivers trained model weights on HuggingFace and training code on GitHub. Strengths - Correctly decomposes the cross-architecture dLLM distillation problem into three orthogonal barriers (temporal reliability, context scarcity, vocabulary mismatch) and provides a dedicated, principled solution for each rather than a single monolithic loss. - Reverse CALM has a rigorous information-theoretic grounding (Bernoulli KL equivalence, bounded gradient proof in Appendix C) that goes well beyond ad-hoc engineering — the dual-end noise filtering property is a direct consequence of the math, not a tuned heuristic. - COMPDEMO exploits a structural property unique to masked diffusion (bidirectional context, complementary mask partitioning) with no AR analog — the design is creative and tightly motivated. - The authors correctly diagnose and acknowledge that TIDAL and Reverse CALM are incompatible (one is mode-covering, the other mode-seeking), explain the mechanism, and do not attempt to combine them — this level of methodological self-awareness is uncommon. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly confront: student scale limited to 0.6B (no 1.3B/3B experiments); only block diffusion with staircase attention validated (continuous-state and encoder-style dLLMs untested); 512-token context window only; cross-tokenizer and shared-tokenizer pipelines processed independently with no unified multi-teacher objective; COMPDEMO adds ~50% training time; TIDAL and Reverse CALM are incompatible, leaving their combined benefits unrealized. This is an unusually thorough and honest limitations section. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The cross-tokenizer pipeline achieves the highest reported average (34.20) but has no component-level ablation — it is unclear how much of the gain over baseline CALM comes from Reverse CALM specifically vs. the chunk-alignment infrastructure alone. - Ablations use 3 epochs vs. 10 for main results; whether TIDAL's advantage over the timestep-only baseline holds at full training is unverified. - The distilled model substantially underperforms same-size AR on reasoning benchmarks (MATH: 13.20 vs. 32.40). The paper attributes this to the dLLM architecture rather than the distillation, but provides no experiment separating the two causes — the non-distilled BD3LM also scores 13.08 on MATH, so distillation barely moves the needle. - No wall-clock training cost is reported — a 50% teacher-forward-pass overhead is non-trivial at scale and absent from the efficiency analysis. - The "best configuration" in Table 1 is selected post-hoc after sweeping all strategy combinations; it is not stated whether hyperparameters (λ_init, λ_max, temperature T, CALM weight) were selected on a held-out validation set or on the test benchmarks themselves. - Evaluation setting uses 50 randomly sampled examples per benchmark — HumanEval (164 total) and MBPP (374 total) variance at n=50 can exceed reported differences between configurations. Bottom Line TIDE is a well-reasoned, practically motivated paper that solves a real gap in dLLM distillation; practitioners building deployable diffusion LMs will find the Reverse CALM objective and dual-axis scheduling directly applicable, but the persistent reasoning performance gap relative to same-size AR models means cross-architecture distillation alone does not yet make dLLMs a drop-in replacement for most NLP workloads.
Recommended 2604.26951 PDF
#69 — Innovative (64)

FLARE: Finetuning ReLU With FIRE for Efficient Long-Context Inference

past.date-conference.com · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces FLARE, a fine-tuning recipe and fused algorithm that converts pre-trained Softmax-attention Transformers to use ReLU attention + FIRE positional encoding, plus a custom CUDA kernel and hardware implementation for edge deployment. - Why it's important: Softmax attention is costly on edge hardware because it requires exponentiation, two-pass online normalization, and data-dependent sequential reduction steps that prevent pipelining and scale poorly with context length; eliminating it directly enables deployment on resource-constrained IoT and mobile devices. - How it does it: The paper shows that fine-tuning FIRE first (replacing RoPE), then fine-tuning ReLU (replacing Softmax) in two sequential 10k-iteration stages outperforms all other integration orderings and preserves length generalization; the FLARE algorithm further skips the FIRE bias addition whenever the sign condition implies the ReLU output is zero, exploiting 98.9% sparsity in the attention matrix. - The good: On Qwen2 1.5B, FLARE achieves validation loss 3.376 vs Softmax 3.378 — effectively parity — while delivering 34.7 vs 26.2 inference tokens/sec and a 6× frequency / 57× area / 600× energy reduction over Softmax in a 16nm hardware synthesis. - The missing: Evaluation covers only two benchmarks (validation perplexity and HellaSwag accuracy) on models up to 1.5B parameters; there is no quality comparison at 7B+ scale, no comparison against other hardware-efficient attention alternatives (linear attention, SSMs), and the hardware figures compare isolated function blocks rather than full accelerator systems. - Read it or not: Worth reading for hardware-ML co-design practitioners targeting edge deployment of sub-2B Transformer models, as the fine-tuning ordering insight and PPA analysis are actionable; practitioners deploying production-scale LLMs (7B+) should wait for scaled validation.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Practical Impact (75) anchors the score — full 16nm hardware synthesis with PPA, released CUDA kernel + nanoGPT codebase, end-to-end deployable for edge. Novelty (60) moderate: the FIRE-then-ReLU ordering finding is non-obvious but the FLARE algebraic skip is a straightforward optimization. Rigor (55) limited by single seed, single downstream task (HellaSwag), sub-2B-only evaluation, and the area/energy comparison being module-isolated rather than system-level. Non-Obviousness (65) for the ordering recipe. Completeness (50) — no comparison to linear attention, SSMs, or GQA, the relevant edge-deployment competition. Resilience (55) — the 57×/600× hardware claims will not survive scrutiny at full-system level. Weighted: 0.15*60 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*65 + 0.15*50 + 0.15*55 + 0.30*75 = 61. Adjust slightly upward for released artifact → 64.
systems Novelty 60 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 50 Resilience 55 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deploying LLMs on edge devices (phones, IoT) is bottlenecked by Softmax attention, which requires exponentiation approximations in silicon, two-pass sequential normalization loops (preventing pipeline parallelism), and hardware area that scales with maximum context length. Prior work training ReLU attention from scratch consistently underperforms Softmax-pretrained models. A gap exists in understanding whether post-hoc fine-tuning can bridge this quality gap while enabling ReLU's hardware simplicity. Separately, RoPE positional encoding requires pairwise rotation operations that are suboptimal for hardware acceleration, and FIRE (an additive relative positional encoding with strong length generalization) has not been combined with ReLU attention in a principled way. Key Idea: The central insight is that fine-tuning order determines whether a model acquires length generalization capability: adapting to FIRE first (letting the model learn additive positional biases with Softmax still in place) and then switching to ReLU (which exploits the resulting sparsity pattern) succeeds where simultaneous or reversed-order fine-tuning fails at length generalization. The secondary insight is that ReLU attention produces ~98.9% zeros in the causal attention probability matrix, so the FIRE bias addition can be skipped with a simple comparison, converting additions to comparisons for nearly all elements. Methodology: The pipeline has three components. (1) Fine-tuning recipe: Starting from GPT-2 124M (pretrained Softmax + APE), the model is first fine-tuned for 10k iterations with FIRE replacing absolute positional embeddings while retaining Softmax, then fine-tuned for another 10k iterations with ReLU replacing Softmax. Three alternative orderings (simultaneous, ReLU-first, FIRE-first) are compared on OpenWebText with a maximum context length of 1024. Experiments are also run on Qwen2 0.5B and 1.5B for 10k iterations. (2) FLARE algorithm: For each attention score element sij = max(aij + fij, 0), the algorithm checks if fij ≤ −aij before performing the addition; if so, output is 0 and the addition is skipped. This is implemented as a custom CUDA kernel with FlashAttention-style tiling in shared memory (Flash-FLARE-Attention). The kernel is modular and compatible with FlashAttention2. (3) Hardware PPA: ReLU and online-Softmax are synthesized using the 16nm node with Fusion Compiler + automatic place-and-route at 0.8V TT corner, 85°C, with identical I/O delay constraints and BF16 I/O. ReLU bus width is artificially widened to 8 to make the module non-trivial for the compiler. SRAM buffer is excluded from Softmax PPA on fairness grounds. Critical design decisions: (a) sequential FIRE-then-ReLU ordering is justified empirically but the mechanism is not formally explained; (b) SRAM exclusion from Softmax PPA is a reasonable choice but significantly magnifies the stated area ratio; (c) the 98.9% sparsity figure is measured on the final checkpoint — it is not clear whether this holds throughout fine-tuning or at different scales. Key Results: On GPT-2 124M (OpenWebText, 1024 context): Softmax→ReLU fine-tuning reaches lower validation loss than 30k-iteration ReLU-from-scratch training, and takes 12 hours vs 17 hours on A100. FIRE-then-ReLU is the only recipe achieving length generalization to 2048 and 4096 tokens (all other orderings degrade sharply at extended lengths). On Qwen2 0.5B (10k fine-tuning): Softmax 3.493 / FLARE 3.710 val loss, HellaSwag 0.313 / 0.324, inference 30.7 / 42.7 tok/s. On Qwen2 1.5B: Softmax 3.378 / FLARE 3.376 val loss, HellaSwag 0.334 / 0.335, inference 26.2 / 34.7 tok/s. Flash-FLARE-Attention achieves 3.8× average speedup over FlashAttention for context lengths 512–4096. Hardware PPA (16nm): ReLU 3226 MHz / 136 µm² / 80.7 µW / 0.003 nJ vs Softmax 515 MHz / 7838 µm² / 509 µW / 2.056 nJ — yielding 6× frequency, 57× area reduction, 6.3× power reduction, 642× energy reduction. A notable finding is that the quality gap between FLARE and Softmax largely disappears at 1.5B scale, suggesting ReLU fine-tuning becomes easier as models grow. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION. The genuinely novel contribution is the ordered fine-tuning recipe establishing that FIRE-then-ReLU sequential adaptation is necessary for length generalization — this is a non-obvious empirical finding with practical consequence. The FLARE algebraic reordering (skip-addition under sparsity) is a useful but straightforward engineering optimization once the 98.9% sparsity property is observed. The hardware PPA analysis is execution-of-known-methodology applied to a new design point rather than a new hardware technique. Rigor & Reproducibility: The paper performs controlled ablations over fine-tuning orderings and measures both validation loss and length generalization, which is the right evaluation structure. However, no error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported for any experiment. All experiments use a single A100 GPU run. The HellaSwag evaluation uses only one downstream task, which is a narrow proxy for general language modeling quality. Baselines at GPT-2 scale include NoPE and RoPE (reasonable), but on Qwen2, the comparison is only between four configurations of the same model — no external comparison to other edge-efficient attention architectures (e.g., GQA, linear attention, Mamba). Code and hardware designs are publicly released at https://github.com/ReaLLMASIC/nanoGPT. The hardware comparison excludes SRAM from Softmax, which while justified, should be noted when interpreting the 57× area claim — the full-system area ratio would be substantially smaller. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS (a built tool evaluated by implementation and deployment metrics). - Practical: Fine-tuning runs on a single A100 80GB GPU; the CUDA kernel targets standard NVIDIA GPUs; hardware designs target 16nm ASIC — all commercially available. - Efficient: Flash-FLARE-Attention is 3.8× faster than FlashAttention at context lengths 512–4096; hardware uses 0.003 nJ per 1024-element vector vs 2.056 nJ for Softmax, at the cost of 10k–20k iterations of fine-tuning compute. - Effective: Evidence shows FLARE reaches Softmax parity on validation loss and HellaSwag at 1.5B scale, and the length generalization to 4× training context is demonstrated; however, the 0.5B model has a meaningful quality gap (3.716 vs 3.493 val loss), and there is only one downstream task. - Scalable: Tested only up to 1.5B parameters and 4096 context length; modern production LLMs run at 7B–70B with 32K–128K contexts — extrapolation to those regimes is claimed but not validated. The hardware synthesis covers one module in isolation, not a full accelerator pipeline. The work delivers a software artifact (CUDA kernel + PyTorch codebase) and hardware designs (synthesized RTL at 16nm), making it more than paper form. Strengths: - The empirical discovery that fine-tuning order (FIRE-then-ReLU, not simultaneous or reversed) determines whether length generalization is acquired is non-obvious and actionable — it is the kind of practical recipe a practitioner cannot easily derive from first principles. - Closing the quality gap to near-parity at 1.5B scale (val loss delta < 0.002) while achieving 32% inference throughput improvement is a strong concrete result. - The 98.9% sparsity observation is a useful empirical characterization of ReLU attention behavior that justifies the FLARE algebraic optimization and connects to prior theoretical work on null attention. - Full hardware synthesis with PPA analysis at 16nm, including APR, provides credible and reproducible efficiency numbers rather than FLOP estimates.
