Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors do not explicitly list limitations in a dedicated section. The paper implicitly acknowledges that the largest model tested is 1.5B parameters and the longest context is 4096 tokens. The abstract notes the work is oriented toward edge devices, implicitly scoping out cloud-scale LLMs. The authors do not discuss the quality penalty at 0.5B scale, the reliance on a single downstream task, or the gap between isolated hardware module comparison and full-system PPA. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The 57× area and 600× energy reductions compare an isolated ReLU function block to an isolated Softmax block; in a complete attention accelerator, Softmax is a small fraction of the total silicon area (dominated by matrix multiply units), so the system-level reduction is far smaller and unstated. - No comparison to alternative hardware-efficient attention approaches: linear attention variants, state-space models (Mamba, S4), or grouped-query attention — the relevant competition for edge deployment. - Only one downstream benchmark (HellaSwag) is used; perplexity and one classification task do not establish that fine-tuned models preserve general capability, particularly for instruction-following or generation quality. - Fine-tuning of only 10k iterations on Qwen2 raises convergence questions — the models may not have fully adapted, potentially understating FLARE quality at higher iteration counts. - The 98.9% sparsity figure is measured on one checkpoint of one model; whether this holds at scale (7B+, long context) is not examined. - The FIRE-then-ReLU ordering superiority for length generalization lacks a mechanistic explanation — the paper identifies the phenomenon empirically but does not explain why reversing the order fails. Bottom Line: FLARE offers a concrete, reproducible recipe for converting small pre-trained Transformers into hardware-efficient edge-deployable models with competitive quality at 1.5B scale, but the evidence base is too narrow (sub-2B models, single downstream task, module-level hardware comparison) to know whether the approach holds at production-relevant scales.
Search Results
#70 — Innovative (64)

SparKV: Overhead-Aware KV Cache Loading for Efficient On-Device LLM Inference

arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SparKV, a cloud-edge hybrid framework that routes each KV cache chunk to either wireless streaming or local sparse computation based on per-chunk overhead estimates and Transformer dependency structure, overlapping the two paths to minimize Time-to-First-Token (TTFT) for on-device LLM inference with reusable contexts. - Why it's important: On-device LLMs processing private long-context data (15K–40K tokens) face prefill latencies exceeding 15 seconds, which makes interactive agent applications impractical on smartphones and edge AI platforms. - How it does it: A potential-aware greedy scheduler approximates a MILP formulation to assign each (token-chunk, layer, head) triple to stream or compute, guided by a lightweight MLP that predicts sparse-attention latency from sparsity features, with a sliding-window runtime controller that migrates chunks between paths when wireless throughput or GPU load fluctuates. - The good: Across nine public benchmarks, five models, and two hardware platforms, SparKV reduces TTFT by 1.3×–5.1× over the best prior baseline while preserving F1/accuracy within noise, with energy reductions of 1.5×–3.3× per request. - The missing: No code or artifact is released, the cloud-side KV pre-computation and storage cost is excluded from the reported TTFT and energy numbers, and the cumulative scheduling overhead (2.6 ms × hundreds of chunks) is not aggregated into a single end-to-end breakdown. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building edge LLM serving systems or studying compute-communication overlap under wireless variability; systems practitioners who need GPU-side NPU support or open-source code should wait for a follow-up release.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (55) is the dominant drag at 30% weight — no code release stated, NPU support deferred despite being the primary smartphone target, and scheduling overhead (10–25s) plus cloud pre-computation cost are excluded from headline TTFT. Resilience (60) is weak because the honest comparison against Strong Hybrid is only 1.3×, with no variance reporting. Novelty (70) and Non-Obviousness (75) are reasonable — the potential-aware greedy and MLP latency predictor are clean contributions — but the underlying primitives are all from prior work. Rigor (70) reflects good ablations and multi-platform coverage offset by absent error bars and a non-reproducible Wi-Fi testbed.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation On-device LLMs (0.5–7B parameters, 4-bit quantized) increasingly support context-reuse workloads — multi-turn chat, private document QA, agentic UI traces, retrieval-augmented generation — where the same context is prepended to every query. The resulting prefill phase dominates TTFT: on a Redmi K80 Pro, a 15K-token context takes over 15 seconds; on Jetson Orin with sparse attention, more than 5 seconds. Two prior directions address complementary bottlenecks — streaming a compressed KV cache from the cloud (I/O-bound) and accelerating local computation with sparse attention (compute-bound) — but neither adapts to the heterogeneity of individual KV chunks or to volatile wireless and edge-GPU conditions. A naïve overlap of the two paths yields only 1.4×–1.8× speedup under wireless conditions, well below the 2.2× achievable on stable wired interconnects, because fixed positional scheduling ignores per-chunk cost variation (up to 17.7× in computation latency, substantial variation in compressed KV size). Key Idea The core insight is that the optimal path for each KV chunk (stream or compute) depends on that chunk's individual streaming cost (compressed size ÷ bandwidth) and computation cost (sparse-attention latency predicted by a lightweight MLP), not on its positional order. Formalizing this as a MILP over Transformer dependency constraints, then approximating it with a potential-aware greedy heuristic that also accounts for which future chunks a scheduling decision unlocks, lets SparKV exploit overlap more precisely than any positionally-ordered scheme. Methodology SparKV partitions the KV cache into 1024-token chunks indexed by (token-block t, layer l, attention head h). Three components work together. (1) KV Chunk Scheduler: formulates chunk assignment as a MILP minimizing total makespan — the sum over stages of max(streaming time, computation time) — subject to Transformer dependency constraints (a chunk at layer l > 1 requires the previous layer to be locally computed before it can be computed, and requires all earlier token chunks at layer l to have been resolved). Since exact MILP solving via Gurobi takes 114–705 seconds for 10–20K-token contexts, SparKV uses a potential-aware greedy heuristic that scores each chunk by its own cost plus the sum of reciprocal costs of newly unlocked chunks, reducing scheduling runtime to 10–25 seconds with TTFT within 4–6% of optimal. (2) Computation Latency Predictor: a two-hidden-layer MLP (48 × 24 neurons) maps a three-feature vector ⟨token-block index, number of active attention blocks, GPU utilization⟩ to sparse-attention latency; it is trained offline in 17.6 seconds on 6,000 samples and reduces latency estimation error by 4.8×–5.6× over Roofline at 2.6 ms per-chunk inference overhead. (3) Runtime Controller: sliding-window monitoring of wireless throughput and GPU utilization detects when either path falls behind; it speculatively prefetches from the next stage or migrates tail-of-queue chunks between paths, with a per-stage migration cap to prevent oscillation. KV compression uses layer-wise non-uniform 5-bit quantization plus Huffman coding; on Jetson's UMA, no explicit PCIe copy is needed. Key Results Evaluated on Qwen3-4B, Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen3-14B (LLMs) and Qwen2.5-VL-7B, InternVL2-8B (VLMs) on an RTX 5080 laptop and Jetson AGX 64GB, across nine LongBench and VideoMME datasets (10K–49K mean context lengths). Against Local Prefill (SpargeAttention), SparKV reduces TTFT by 2.9×–5.1× on the laptop and up to 3.8× on Jetson. Against CacheGen (streaming-only), TTFT improves by 1.8×–2.2× on the laptop and 1.9× on Jetson. Against Strong Hybrid (the augmented [25] baseline with the same SpargeAttention kernel and Huffman compression), SparKV improves TTFT by ~1.3× on both platforms. VLMs show larger gains (1.3×–1.4× over Strong Hybrid) due to stronger chunk heterogeneity in visual tokens. Under five competing Wi-Fi devices (congested), SparKV still beats Strong Hybrid and CacheGen by 1.4× and 1.6×. Under four concurrent LLM requests, TTFT degrades by only 0.15 s for SparKV versus sharp degradation for baselines; energy per request stays below 173 J (1.5×–3.3× savings). TTFT scales near-linearly with context length from 10K to 38K tokens, whereas Local Prefill and Strong Hybrid grow super-linearly. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The dependency-aware MILP formulation for hybrid KV chunk scheduling, the potential-aware greedy approximation, and the MLP-based sparse-attention latency predictor are genuinely novel contributions. The integration of runtime wireless and compute adaptation into a unified scheduling loop is new. The underlying primitives — sparse attention, KV quantization, Huffman coding, hybrid streaming — are from prior work; SparKV's contribution is the orchestration layer that makes their combination overhead-aware and dependency-correct. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present for the greedy heuristic vs. exact MILP, the MLP predictor vs. Roofline, and the runtime controller under interference. Multiple hardware platforms, models, and datasets support generalization claims. Error bars are absent from TTFT CDFs, and it is unclear whether any variance across repeated runs is reported beyond the energy measurement (averaged over ten trials for NIC power). The greedy-vs-MILP comparison is limited to LongChat; the gap on other datasets is not verified. No code, model, or dataset artifact is released (the paper states no URL). The Wi-Fi testbed is a production campus network, which is realistic but not reproducible by third parties. The scheduling runtime (10–25 seconds per context) is non-trivial for first-request latency and is reported separately rather than folded into TTFT, which is a methodological gap — if the schedule must be computed per request, this overhead directly adds to TTFT for the first query on a new context. Deployability (SYSTEMS profile) - Practical: SparKV runs on commodity hardware (RTX 5080 laptop, Jetson AGX, Jetson Orin, Redmi K80 Pro) with 4-bit quantized models and standard Wi-Fi infrastructure; the MLP predictor trains in under 20 seconds per device. - Efficient: Per-request energy savings of 1.5×–3.3× are demonstrated; the scheduler itself adds 10–25 seconds of offline overhead per unique context, which is amortized across repeated reuse but is unacceptable for single-use contexts. - Effective: TTFT reduction of 1.3×–5.1× with task quality (F1, Rouge-L, accuracy) within noise of full local prefill is demonstrated across diverse benchmarks and modalities. - Scalable: Near-linear TTFT scaling is shown up to 38K tokens; behavior beyond 40K tokens and with very large models (>14B parameters, which require >16 GB) is not tested; NPU deployment is left as future work despite NPU being a primary target platform for smartphones. The system delivers a working prototype integrated with HuggingFace Transformers and SpargeAttention; no public code release is stated. Strengths - The MILP-to-greedy reduction is well-motivated and the heuristic is analytically clean: the potential term correctly accounts for unlocking future computation, avoiding the myopic failure mode of latency-only greedy policies. - The empirical motivation section (Section III) is unusually rigorous for a systems paper: it measures 17.7× chunk-level compute variation and documents why naive overlap fails under wireless conditions before proposing SparKV, rather than asserting the problem exists. - Multimodal coverage (VLMs with visual tokens) is a meaningful generalization: visual tokens induce stronger chunk heterogeneity, and the larger margins in VLM experiments validate the core hypothesis. - Energy measurement methodology (Xiaomi smart plug + NPU active-power isolation) is explicitly described and not merely estimated, providing an honest accounting of per-request cost. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge two scope boundaries: (1) SparKV handles only a single reusable context, and multi-document RAG requiring concurrent context blending (à la CacheBlend) is future work; (2) the current implementation is GPU-centric — though the motivation study includes a mobile NPU, full NPU implementation is explicitly left to future work. These are candid and non-minimized admissions. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The cloud-side KV pre-computation and persistent storage cost is excluded from all TTFT and energy figures. The cloud must encode, quantize, and store KV caches before any request arrives; for new or infrequently reused contexts, this amortization may not hold, and the end-to-end privacy boundary is weakened (streaming half the chunks to the edge implies the cloud has already seen the full context). - The scheduling overhead (10.7–25.4 seconds for 10–20K tokens) is benchmarked separately and compared favorably to Gurobi, but it is not included in reported TTFT. For single-use or low-reuse contexts, this completely eliminates the TTFT benefit. - The 1.3× improvement over Strong Hybrid — the most honest comparison since both use identical primitives — is modest and may not justify deployment complexity in real-world pipelines with stable wireless. - Variance and statistical significance are not reported; all TTFT comparisons are presented as CDFs without confidence intervals or repeated-trial statistics, making it impossible to assess whether the 1.3× margin over Strong Hybrid is reliable across runs. - No baseline from the MobiSys/OSDI community (e.g., InfiniGen, IMPRESS) is implemented head-to-head; CacheGen is the only streaming baseline run experimentally. Bottom Line SparKV delivers a practically motivated, algorithmically clean system that outperforms the closest prior hybrid baseline by ~1.3× and naive approaches by up to 5×, but the 1.3× margin over the strengthened hybrid is the number that matters most in practice, and the absent code release, unaccounted scheduling latency, and omitted cloud pre-computation cost leave the end-to-end case for deployment weaker than the paper's framing suggests.
Search Results 2604.21231 PDF
#71 — Innovative (64)

Co-Director: Agentic Generative Video Storytelling (11 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 29 Apr 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Co-Director, a hierarchical multi-agent framework that applies Multi-Armed Bandit (MAB) optimization over a factored creative action space to steer an agentic video generation pipeline toward globally coherent short-form video narratives. - Why it's important: Current agentic video pipelines rely on linear, waterfall-style prompt chains that propagate early errors downstream and lack any mechanism for principled creative exploration — producing identity drift, semantic inconsistency, and local optima. - How it does it: A UCB1 bandit selects combinations of creative strategy, narrative mode, and aesthetic archetype; the orchestrator injects the chosen configuration top-down into every sub-agent's system prompt; a factored reward signal (one scalar per axis) updates all arm estimates from a single pipeline execution, achieving sample efficiency over the 36-combination space. - The good: Co-Director scores 81.4 average on GenAd-Bench vs. 65.3 for the strongest prior agentic baseline (MovieAgent), a gap validated by a 50-scenario human study (MOS 3.96 vs. 3.71 for the best monolithic model, Veo 3.1). - The missing: Gemini 3 Pro is simultaneously the pipeline LLM backbone, the GenAd-Bench construction tool, and the MLLM-as-judge for both internal optimization and final evaluation — a circular evaluation loop the paper does not acknowledge. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building agentic creative pipelines or studying test-time search in non-differentiable generative systems; the factored MAB reward formulation is the transferable idea, but results should be treated as internal Google benchmarking, not independently reproducible findings.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (70) — factored-reward MAB over a non-differentiable agentic creative pipeline is a transferable idea, plus a new benchmark. Rigor (50) is the main drag: Gemini 3 Pro acts as pipeline backbone, benchmark constructor, MAB reward, AND final judge — a circular evaluation loop the paper never acknowledges, only partially mitigated by the human study. Non-Obviousness (70), Completeness (65), Resilience (55) — solid ablation table and human study, but proprietary stack, hand-picked 3×3×4 action space untested outside ads, 12-second/4-shot ceiling, and no failure-rate reporting. Impact (60) — open-source code promised but reproducibility gated by five Google APIs. Weighted ≈ 61.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 65 Resilience 55 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation State-of-the-art video diffusion models (Veo, Kling, Wan) generate high-fidelity individual clips but cannot autonomously transform an abstract brief — a product tagline and reference images — into a coherent multi-shot narrative. Existing agentic pipelines chain LLM modules (script → storyboard → keyframes → video) in a linear order with hand-crafted prompt templates. This architecture suffers from two compounding problems: (1) early-stage errors (LLM hallucinating a product category, generating mismatched character appearance) propagate and amplify downstream because no global credit-assignment mechanism exists, and (2) static prompt templates lock the system into a fixed creative direction with no mechanism to explore alternative narrative strategies that might yield higher quality — precisely the serendipitous exploration human directors rely on. Existing video benchmarks (MovieBench, ViStoryBench) evaluate isolated generative sub-tasks, not full end-to-end storytelling from abstract concept to final video. Key Idea The core insight is to treat the entire non-differentiable agentic pipeline as a black box and optimize a factored creative configuration space globally using a Multi-Armed Bandit. The arms are tuples θ = (θ_cs, θ_nm, θ_aa) drawn from three orthogonal axes (3 creative strategies × 3 narrative modes × 4 aesthetic archetypes = 36 combinations), but because the reward is factored into three independent dimension-specific scalars, a single full pipeline execution simultaneously updates all three axes — reducing the effective search space from 36 combinations to 10 independent parameters. Top-down propagation of the selected configuration into every sub-agent's system prompt prevents the semantic drift caused by independent per-agent prompt interpretation. Methodology The pipeline instantiates four agent layers: Orchestrator (Φ_orch), Pre-Production (Φ_pre), Production (Φ_prod), and Post-Production (Φ_post). Φ_orch manages global state: it parses the user prompt into structured constraints (brand, product, demographics), annotates reference visuals with semantic roles, and drives a T-iteration MAB optimization loop. At each iteration t, UCB1 samples θ^(t) and propagates the global conditioning context Θ^(t) = (p̂_user, Î_ref, θ^(t)) downstream. Φ_pre transforms Θ into a storyboard through four sequential modules: Creative Brief (cultural/contextual enrichment of the prompt), Storyline L (scene-by-scene script with plot, character actions, pacing), Visual Assets V (character and prop reference images generated only for entities absent from provided references), and Storyboard S (semantic descriptors, camera kinematics, temporal constraints, entity presence flags, and global audio directives per scene). All four modules are conditioned on θ. Φ_prod contains three sub-agents: Keyframe Agent (Φ_frame) generates first-frame images to condition downstream video synthesis; Video Agent (Φ_video) applies video diffusion conditioned on keyframes, scene descriptors, and θ; Audio Agent (Φ_audio) synthesizes global voiceover and background music using text-to-speech and text-to-audio models. Local optimization is applied at two stages: (1) Storyline refinement — an LLM scores L on five criteria (hook quality, narrative cohesion, product integration, engagement, prompt adherence; 0–20 each) and regenerates if below threshold τ_L; (2) Keyframe refinement — an MLLM evaluates the full keyframe sequence jointly, identifies inconsistent frames by index, and selectively regenerates only flagged frames while preserving accepted ones. Global optimization uses UCB1 with an LLM warm-start: before the first execution, an LLM initializes arm expected values using domain knowledge about the product category and target demographics, biasing early exploration toward theoretically sound configurations. The factored reward R^(t) = (r_cs, r_nm, r_aa) ∈ [0,100]³ is produced by an MLLM evaluating the final video against both an aggregate quality score (used for video selection across T iterations) and dimension-specific strategic efficacy scores (used for MAB arm updates). Critical design decisions: (a) factored vs. scalar reward — the paper ablates this, showing a 5.0 VAF-point loss with scalar reward; (b) warm start vs. cold start — cold start increases trajectory variance during early exploration; (c) joint keyframe evaluation vs. per-frame — joint evaluation is required to catch cross-frame identity drift. Implementation: Gemini 3 Pro (LLM/MLLM), Nano Banana Pro (image generation), Veo 3.1 (video generation), Gemini 2.5 Pro TTS (speech), Lyria 2 (music). T=4, local refinement capped at 3 retries. Output: 4-shot, 12-second videos. Key Results On GenAd-Bench (400 scenarios, validated split of 200): Co-Director achieves 81.4 average (VAF 82.1, DA 91.4, MA 82.0, VQ 70.2) vs. MovieAgent 65.3, AniMaker 62.2, Veo 3.1 63.6, Wan 2.6 65.0. The base agentic pipeline (T=1, no optimization) scores 68.5; random search at T=4 scores 75.7; Co-Director at T=4 scores 81.4 — demonstrating that MAB + warm start provides a 5.7-point gain over random search at equal compute budget. Human MOS (50-scenario subset, 5 raters each): Co-Director 3.96 overall vs. Veo 3.1 3.71, MovieAgent 3.22, AniMaker 3.07. MLLM-human Pearson correlation: 0.601 (VAF), 0.647 (DA), 0.570 (MA), 0.345 (VQ) — the VQ gap is attributed to frame-level vs. real-time evaluation differences. On ViStoryBench-Lite (ablated pipeline, keyframes only): Co-Director achieves best Style consistency (Cross: 0.499, Self: 0.743) and Character consistency (Cross: 0.499) but lower Inception Score (10.900) and OCCM (66.215) than some baselines — the authors attribute this to diversity metrics penalizing their consistency objective. Ablation baseline with all optimization removed: 67.2 average, vs. 81.4 full system (+21% relative improvement). Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW METHOD. The genuinely novel contribution is formalizing generative video storytelling as a MAB problem with a factored creative action space grounded in marketing and media theory (Laskey et al. 1989, Escalas 2004, Zettl 1973), and the insight that factored rewards allow sample-efficient exploration without per-dimension pipelines. GenAd-Bench's fictional-entity design to prevent memorization is a meaningful benchmark contribution. The hierarchical multi-agent architecture, MLLM-as-judge, and self-refinement loops are standard applications of known ideas (UCB1, TextGrad/feedback descent) applied to a new setting. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: Table 4 tests 8 conditions covering storyline refinement, keyframe refinement, MAB logic (warm start vs. cold start, factored vs. scalar reward, vs. none), and their combinations — each component is shown to contribute. The human study methodology is above-average for this area: Krippendorff's Alpha, Cohen's Kappa, Pearson and Spearman correlations, and MAE are all reported for both human-human and MLLM-human agreement across all four dimensions. The 200-scenario validation set is appropriately held out from the hillclimbing set. The paper claims code and GenAd-Bench will be open-sourced (project page listed); no specific repository URL is given. Critical methodological concern: Gemini 3 Pro is the LLM backbone for Co-Director's pipeline AND the GenAd-Bench construction tool AND the MLLM-as-judge for both the internal MAB reward and the final benchmark evaluation. This conflates generator and judge within a single model family, creating a systematic bias toward Gemini-style outputs that the paper does not acknowledge. The human validation partially mitigates this for narrative dimensions (DA: r=0.647, MA: r=0.570) but cannot rule out the in-family advantage. Deployability (EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS profile) - Practical: Cannot run on commercially available hardware. All five model components (Gemini 3 Pro, Nano Banana Pro, Veo 3.1, Gemini 2.5 Pro TTS, Lyria 2) are Google-proprietary APIs not generally available; the "open-source" framework is reproducible only for researchers with Google API access. - Efficient: Compute cost is not disclosed. T=4 requires four full pipeline executions, each involving multi-step LLM calls, image generation, and video generation across multiple agents — a substantial and non-trivial cost at scale. - Effective: Evidence for the stated problem (narrative coherence, brand fidelity) is solid within the advertising domain and partially supported in ViStoryBench's consistency metrics; the 12-second output length is a significant constraint on the claims of enabling "short film" production. - Scalable: Tested only at 4-shot, 12-second outputs. No experiments with longer durations or more complex narratives are reported; scaling claims are asserted, not demonstrated. Delivers an open-sourced codebase and GenAd-Bench dataset, but practical reproducibility is gated behind Google proprietary model access. Strengths - The factored MAB formulation is principled and practically motivated: the insight that reward factorization reduces the 36-combination creative space to 10 independent parameters is non-obvious and directly addresses the credit-assignment problem in non-differentiable pipelines. - GenAd-Bench's fictional-entity design (50 fictional brands, 200 products, paired stereotypical/unconventional demographics, 183 global locations) is a carefully engineered benchmark contribution that closes a genuine gap in end-to-end video storytelling evaluation. - The human study is methodologically rigorous by the standards of the field: five independent raters per video, four agreement statistics reported for both human-human ceiling and MLLM-human alignment, with mechanistic explanation for the VQ divergence. - Table 4's ablation cleanly decomposes the system's gains: the base orchestration alone (+3.3 over MovieAgent), warm start vs. cold start (+1.7), factored vs. scalar reward (+2.6), and self-refinement loops (+9.5 total) are all individually attributed. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge: (1) MLLM-as-judge bias risk and partially mitigate it with the human study; (2) VQ metric divergence between MLLM and humans due to frame-level vs. real-time evaluation; (3) OCCM and Inception Score conflicts with the consistency objective on ViStoryBench; (4) minor anatomical artifacts (left-right hand confusion) remaining in the pipeline. These are confronted relatively directly and lend credibility to the framework — the authors do not oversell the MLLM judge. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Circular evaluation (unacknowledged): The Gemini 3 Pro family appears as LLM backbone, GenAd-Bench construction tool, internal MAB reward signal, and final benchmark judge. No ablation tests a non-Gemini judge, making it impossible to determine whether the headline scores reflect genuine quality or model-family affinity. - Proprietary model barrier: Despite open-sourcing the framework code, full reproducibility requires access to five Google APIs (Gemini 3 Pro, Nano Banana Pro, Veo 3.1, Gemini 2.5 Pro TTS, Lyria 2) that are either unavailable or not generally accessible. The "open-source" framing is misleading for the broader research community. - Hand-designed action space not validated: The 3×3×4 creative action space is constructed by the authors using their selection of marketing/media frameworks. Whether this space captures the relevant creative dimensions for domains outside advertising (documentary, narrative film) is not tested; the ViStoryBench ablation bypasses the MAB action space entirely. - No failure rate reporting: The paper showcases selected qualitative successes; the rate of catastrophic failures (logo hallucination, complete character drift across shots) in the full 200-scenario validation set is not reported. - 12-second ceiling unstated: The paper positions Co-Director as enabling the narrative capabilities of a 200-person production team but all experiments are bounded at 4 shots / 12 seconds — a constraint not clearly flagged as a system limitation. Bottom Line The factored MAB reward design is a genuine and transferable idea for optimizing non-differentiable agentic pipelines, but the paper's credibility is substantially constrained by a circular evaluation architecture in which a single Google model family occupies every role — pipeline backbone, benchmark constructor, optimization oracle, and final judge — making the reported numbers internal benchmarking rather than independently reproducible evidence.
Recommended 2604.24842 PDF
#72 — Innovative (64)

TabSCM: A practical Framework for Generating Realistic Tabular Data

arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TabSCM, a synthetic tabular data generator that imposes a structural causal model (SCM) over discovered variable relationships by combining per-variable conditional diffusion models (continuous descendants), gradient-boosted tree classifiers (categorical descendants), and KDE/frequency marginals (root nodes), sampling in topological order. - Why it's important: Dominant tabular generators (TabSyn, TabDiff, GReaT) match marginal statistics but ignore causal structure, causing downstream models to learn spurious correlations and generating samples that violate known domain constraints — a critical failure in regulated settings like healthcare and finance. - How it does it: After orienting a CPDAG (from any causal discovery algorithm) into a DAG, TabSCM fits one independent conditional model per variable using only that variable's causal parents as inputs, so ancestral sampling in topological order guarantees structural coherence by construction. - The good: Up to 583× faster training than diffusion-only baselines while matching or exceeding their statistical fidelity on most of 7 datasets, with near-zero semantic constraint violations on 3 of 4 sanity checks where pure diffusion models fail badly. - The missing: The paper never validates the inferred causal graph against ground truth, so all claims of "causal soundness" rest on an unverified assumed structure; whether TabSCM preserves true causal mechanisms or merely a plausible-but-wrong graph is never tested. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building privacy-preserving data pipelines in regulated domains where interpretability and constraint validity matter more than maximally tight marginal distributions.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Impact (75) drives the score — open-source library, dramatic training-time reduction, near-zero constraint violations by construction, practical CPU/GPU mix. Novelty (55) — integration of CPDAG-conditioned per-variable DDPMs + GBT is new but a composition of known parts. Rigor (50) is the central weakness: the headline causal-soundness claim is never validated against any ground-truth graph, and no synthetic SCM experiment is included. Non-Obviousness (55), Completeness (55) honest about correlation-error tradeoff, Resilience (50) — 583× is cherry-picked, Adult AUC gap unexplained, max 24 columns. Weighted with systems weights ≈ 60–62, rounded to 64 reflecting the genuinely useful Pareto improvement and artifact release.
systems Novelty 55 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 55 Resilience 50 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Regulated industries need privacy-preserving synthetic tabular data with heterogeneous types (mixed continuous/categorical), but dominant deep generative approaches optimize only for marginal and pairwise fidelity while ignoring the causal structure that governs real data-generating processes. This produces two failures: (1) synthetic data encodes spurious correlations that downstream models learn as real signal, degrading generalization and fairness; (2) generators produce out-of-domain records that violate known constraints (e.g., latitude/longitude pairs outside the target state, education-level/years mismatches). Prior causal-aware generators (GOGGLE, Causal-TGAN, DCM) exist but underperform on statistical fidelity, scale poorly, or rely on GAN training instability. The gap is a practical framework that is simultaneously causally coherent, statistically faithful, computationally tractable, and interpretable. Key Idea Decomposing the joint distribution into per-variable structural assignments — one conditional model per variable conditioned only on its causal parents, fitted and sampled in topological order — simultaneously enforces causal coherence (no structurally invalid sample can be generated), reduces each sub-model's input dimensionality (only parents, not all variables), and fragments a single large training problem into many small ones, enabling dramatic speed gains over full joint-distribution diffusion. Methodology TabSCM operates in four stages. (i) Causal discovery: accepts any algorithm outputting a CPDAG; three are tested — PC (constraint-based), GES (score-based), and NOTEARS (continuous optimization). (ii) DAG orientation: standard CPDAG-to-DAG heuristics to resolve undirected edges. (iii) Structural assignment fitting: root nodes (no parents) use KDE with Gaussian kernel for continuous variables or empirical frequencies for categorical; non-root continuous nodes use a per-variable conditional DDPM with standard MSE noise-prediction loss conditioned on parent values and diffusion timestep; categorical non-root nodes use a gradient-boosted tree classifier (Friedman 2001). (iv) Ancestral sampling in topological order. Interventions follow Pearl's do-calculus: set a variable to a fixed value, freeze its mechanism, and propagate downstream. Three critical design decisions: (a) using one DDPM per child variable rather than a global joint diffusion — well-justified for speed and interpretability but explicitly acknowledged to hurt cross-variable correlation recovery; (b) gradient-boosted trees for categorical nodes — pragmatic and fast but not principled within the diffusion framework; (c) causal discovery algorithm and hyperparameters are user-selected and shown in Table 2 to materially affect results (NOTEARS correlation error on Magic ranges from 2.96% to 10.84% across regularization settings). Key Results Training time: 7–24 minutes for TabSCM across 7 datasets vs. 115–2000 minutes for TabDiff and 16–127 minutes for TabSyn; the headline 583× speedup is a best-case figure on the Loan dataset against TabDiff. Density error (KS/TV average, Table 5): TabSCM achieves 1.21% on Loan (best) and 1.73% on Diabetes (best), but TabDiff leads on Adult (0.75%), Magic (0.79%), and Beijing (1.16%). Correlation error (Table 6): TabSCM wins on Loan (6.62%) and ties HELOC best (1.86%), but trails TabDiff/TabSyn on Adult (5.12% vs. 1.59%), Diabetes (6.75% vs. 3.95%), and Magic (2.79% vs. 0.81%). Downstream utility (Table 7): within ~5% of best AUC/RMSE on most datasets; notably weaker on Adult (AUC 0.842 vs. 0.912 for TabDiff) — an 8% relative gap that goes unaddressed. Privacy (DCR): consistently higher DCR than TabDiff/TabSyn (better privacy at modest utility cost). Constraint violations (Table 8): 0.00% on education-number check (S2, vs. 0.22%–86.8% for diffusion models), 0.11% on gender check (S3). C2ST imperceptibility (Table 9): TabSCM 0.869–0.999, generally second to full diffusion models. Baselines are current and strong, including the most recent ICLR 2024/2025 methods. Scale: maximum 252k rows, 24 columns; no test at higher dimensionality. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD, NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The integration of CPDAG-conditioned generation with per-variable DDPM plus gradient-boosted trees for mixed-type tabular data is new. Individual components (causal discovery algorithms, DDPM, gradient boosting, Pearl's SCM framework) are well-established; the contribution is the generation architecture that enables causal interventions, semantic validity by construction, and competitive fidelity at a fraction of the training cost. This is primarily a systems-integration and engineering contribution rather than a fundamental algorithmic advance. Rigor & Reproducibility Code is released at github.com/jsve96/TabSCM. Five trials with mean ± std are reported for most metrics. Ablations on causal discovery algorithm and hyperparameters (Table 2) and diffusion training settings (Figs 4–5) are present and informative. Key concern: the central claim — causal structure preservation — has no quantitative ground-truth evaluation. The paper never measures whether the inferred graphs are correct, and whether structural assignments learned from a wrong graph produce meaningfully different synthetic data. SHAP comparisons (Figs 7–8) are qualitative, domain-plausible, but not a rigorous causal fidelity test. Constraint violation checks (Table 8) test only 4 specific variable relationships. No formal significance tests across datasets. The counterfactual analysis (§8.4) is explicitly labeled "preliminary." Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS — a built tool evaluated empirically on real datasets. - Practical: Runs on a single NVIDIA RTX 6000 Ada 48GB GPU; KDE, frequency estimation, and gradient boosting run on CPU, making deployment practical on commodity hardware without GPU requirements for much of the pipeline. - Efficient: 7–24 minutes training across tested datasets (vs. hours for TabDiff); per-sample generation cost is low since each variable requires only a small conditional DDPM denoising pass over its parents. Memory: succeeded on the 252k-row Loan dataset where GOGGLE ran out of memory. - Effective: Matches or exceeds best diffusion models on constraint validity and privacy; lags by meaningful margins on correlation fidelity and downstream utility on several datasets. Causal intervention capability is demonstrated qualitatively; correctness against known interventional distributions is not validated. - Scalable: Tested up to 252k rows and 24 columns. Causal discovery (PC/GES/NOTEARS) is the likely bottleneck for wide tables — all three scale poorly with feature count (quadratic or worse). No evidence of testing beyond ~25 columns. Software artifact: open-source Python library at github.com/jsve96/TabSCM. Strengths - The modular SCM architecture achieves a genuinely favorable Pareto trade-off: near-top statistical fidelity at dramatically lower compute cost than pure diffusion, plus semantic constraint violation rates near 0% that joint diffusion models cannot guarantee by construction. - Mixed-type handling via DDPM for continuous and gradient-boosted trees for categorical is a pragmatic and motivated design that avoids GAN training instability and LLM tokenization overhead, and explicitly handles columns with high-cardinality categories (LabelEncoder instead of OneHotEncoder). - Sensitivity analysis over causal discovery algorithm, CI-test threshold, and diffusion hyperparameters (Tables 2, Figs 4–5) is unusually thorough for this literature and gives practitioners actionable guidance. - The five-axis evaluation (density, correlation, utility, privacy, faithfulness/detectability) plus constraint violation is among the most comprehensive benchmarking setups in the tabular synthesis literature. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The counterfactual analysis is explicitly labeled "preliminary," with deeper investigation deferred to future work. The authors directly acknowledge that the causal factorization hurts correlation error relative to joint diffusion models: "abolishing this modularity, e.g., TabSyn and TabDiff, can lead to lower correlation errors on average." The causal sufficiency assumption (no latent confounders) is stated as a theoretical prerequisite but never revisited in the experimental discussion. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No ground-truth causal graph exists for any of the 7 datasets, so the paper's central claim — causal soundness — is entirely unverifiable from the experiments. No synthetic experiment with known ground-truth SCM is included to confirm that TabSCM recovers the correct structure. - The causal sufficiency assumption (no unobserved confounders) is foundational yet almost certainly violated in practice for Adult income, HELOC credit, and Loan datasets where unmeasured socioeconomic factors are dominant drivers. The impact of latent confounders on generation quality is never discussed. - The 583× speedup is a cherry-picked best case (TabDiff on Loan); the average speedup is far lower and not reported as a single summary statistic. On HELOC, TabSCM takes 24 minutes vs. TabDDPM's 28 — a marginal difference not reflected in the headline claim. - The downstream utility gap on Adult (AUC 0.842 vs. 0.912 for TabDiff, a full 8% relative) is neither explained nor ablated; it is unclear whether the causal graph quality, the label-encoding choice, or the per-variable diffusion architecture drives this underperformance. - No evaluation on high-dimensional tables (>50 columns): whether causal discovery, per-variable DDPM fitting, and topological sampling remain tractable and faithful at typical enterprise dataset widths is untested and likely a real scaling barrier. Bottom Line TabSCM is a well-engineered integration of causal discovery and conditional generative models that achieves a practical Pareto improvement over both pure diffusion and prior causal generators on speed, constraint validity, and privacy — but its signature claim of "causal soundness" is asserted rather than empirically validated against ground truth, which limits its scientific standing relative to its practical usefulness.
Citations 2604.22337 PDF
#73 — Innovative (64)

Taming Actor-Observer Asymmetry in Agents via Dialectical Alignment (12 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 28 Apr 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ReTAS (Reasoning via Thesis-Antithesis-Synthesis), a 4B-parameter model trained via dialectical chain-of-thought and GRPO to produce perspective-invariant failure attribution in LLM multi-agent systems, along with the Ambiguous Failure Benchmark (AFB) to quantify the underlying bias. - Why it's important: Multi-agent systems assign executor and reviewer roles to improve reliability, but those very roles instill a human cognitive bias that causes agents to reach opposite attribution verdicts for the same failure depending on which role they occupy — eroding collaborative trust and causing incorrect corrective actions. - How it does it: ReTAS trains a backbone (Qwen3-4B) to explicitly reason through a Thesis (role-congruent initial reaction), Antithesis (counter-perspective evidence check), and Synthesis (evidence-grounded conclusion) using GRPO rewards that jointly penalize attribution inconsistency and reward task correctness. - The good: ReTAS (4B parameters) achieves 71.2% attribution accuracy and 5.4% V-AOA on FinQA-TAS, outperforming QwQ-32B (32B) and Qwen3-30B-A3B (30B) — demonstrating that structured cognitive alignment is more parameter-efficient than scaling. - The missing: The entire training signal derives from GPT-5.1-generated dialectical trajectories, making ReTAS essentially a small-model distillation of a proprietary teacher; the paper does not evaluate whether the learned behavior transfers beyond retrieval-augmented question answering to the broader open-ended agent tasks the introduction claims to address. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on multi-agent reliability, cognitive bias in LLMs, or GRPO-based alignment — the benchmark design and the finding that dual-view prompting alone can worsen bias are both practically useful.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (75) and Novelty (70) are the strengths — framing actor-observer asymmetry from social psychology onto LLM agents and showing that naive dual-view prompting is *worse* than single-view is a genuinely surprising, practically actionable result. Rigor (55) is weakened by the GPT-5.1 self-referential training/eval pipeline (training trajectories AND the AFB benchmark are GPT-5.1-generated, while GPT-5.1 is also the strongest AFB baseline), single-run Sales Arena with no variance, and class imbalance in attribution labels. Completeness (60) reflects honest scope acknowledgment but unaddressed concerns about distillation vs. principled reasoning. Resilience (55) suffers from the synthetic-benchmark and pattern-matching concerns. Impact (60) reflects a small deployable model with weights/dataset but limited to structured RAG attribution. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*55 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*60 ≈ 61.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 60 Resilience 55 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multi-agent LLM frameworks assign functional roles (executor, reviewer) to decompose complex tasks and enable self-correction and peer auditing. A systematic problem arises: the assigned role acts as a cognitive prior that biases attribution of failure. An executor facing a timeout tends to blame infrastructure; a reviewer auditing the same failure tends to blame the executor's logic. This perspective-induced inconsistency — Actor-Observer Asymmetry (AOA), a well-established concept in social psychology — causes multi-agent systems to reach contradictory diagnoses, take incorrect corrective actions, and fail to converge on collaborative decisions. Prior mitigation strategies (self-reflection, cross-critique, simple dual-role prompting) treat the symptom and often make the problem worse. Key Idea: The core insight is that perspective-invariant reasoning cannot be achieved by prompting an agent to "be objective" or by introducing a second viewpoint, because the role prior overrides surface-level instructions. Instead, bias must be structurally broken by forcing the model to explicitly instantiate the opposing perspective (Antithesis) before synthesizing a verdict — and then reinforcing this three-step process through RL so it becomes a behavioral habit rather than a prompt-following reflex. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages. (1) Attribution data generation: a retrieval-augmented QA pipeline built on Qwen-2.5-7B generates failures on FinQA (hybrid financial reasoning) and Spider (text-to-SQL). Attribution labels (FalseExt = missing evidence, FalseInt = sufficient evidence but wrong reasoning, True = correct) are assigned deterministically by comparing retrieved evidence against gold evidence and the correct answer — these are not LLM-generated labels. (2) Dialectical synthesis: GPT-5.1 generates two TAS reasoning trajectories per example, one starting from a Defensive Actor prior and one from a Critical Reviewer prior, both required to converge to the same attribution label. This produces FinQA-TAS (6,251 train) and Spider-TAS (7,000 train). (3) Dialectical alignment: supervised fine-tuning on these trajectories for format learning, followed by GRPO reinforcement learning with a composite reward R = αR₁ + βR₂ + γR₃ (α=1 format, β=2 attribution label match, γ=4 downstream answer correctness). GRPO rolls out 8 group trajectories per input and optimizes relative rewards within the group. The backbone is Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507, fine-tuned 3 epochs for SFT and 750 steps of GRPO. Critical design decisions: (a) using objective, non-LLM-derived attribution labels for training ensures ground truth quality; (b) weighting γ highest prioritizes task correctness and prevents the model from gaming format/attribution rewards; (c) the GRPO alignment stage (9 hours on 2× H200) is shown via ablation to be essential — SFT alone gives attribution accuracy of 67.7 vs. 71.2 with GRPO. Key Results: On FinQA-TAS (held-out test set): ReTAS achieves 71.2% attribution accuracy, 5.4% V-AOA, 12.4% Flip rate, and 72.1 F1. Baselines for comparison: QwQ-32B gets 54.9% Acc / 14.7% V-AOA / 71.0 F1 (Dual View), Qwen3-30B-A3B gets 52.9% / 13.5% / 66.5 F1. On Spider-TAS (dev set): ReTAS achieves 61.4% Acc, 10.2% V-AOA, 21.9% Flip, 63.5 F1; vs. DeepSeek-V3.2 (671B) at 64.0 F1 prompting-only. GPT-5.1 closed-model prompting achieves 76.9 F1 (FinQA) and 61.5 F1 (Spider) but Acc/Flip/V-AOA metrics are not applicable as it is not fine-tuned. Cross-domain generalization to the AFB benchmark (not seen during training) shows ReTAS reduces V-AOA to ~11% in Agent-Agent settings, matching the consistency of top-tier proprietary models. The Sales Arena negotiation experiment shows Reflection_TAS achieves $168 total profit vs. $157 baseline (NONE) and $135 for Reflection_Dual — importantly showing that introducing an opposing reviewer role without synthesis hurts performance below the no-reflection baseline. Ablation confirms removing R₂ (attribution reward) causes V-AOA to spike from 5.4 to 16.8 on FinQA-TAS, and removing GRPO reduces accuracy from 71.2 to 67.7. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The application of Fichtean dialectics as a structured reasoning template and the pairing with GRPO to internalize it as a behavioral habit — rather than a prompting pattern — is the genuine novelty. The use of attribution theory from social psychology to frame LLM agent failure is a reframing that is intellectually novel even if the individual components (RAG pipelines, GRPO, SFT) are standard. The finding that dual-view prompting without synthesis can be worse than single-view reflection is a counter-intuitive empirical result with direct practical consequence. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and meaningful: each of the three reward components is removed individually, and the results directionally confirm the design choices. Error bars and significance tests are absent — this is a single-run evaluation. The training dataset statistics are detailed (Table 8). The code is referenced implicitly ("Full prompts are available in our code repository") with a URL (unikcc.github.io/ReTAS/) but no explicit GitHub link with released weights is stated in the body. One methodological concern: the dialectical training trajectories are generated by GPT-5.1, making ReTAS effectively a small-model distillation of a proprietary model's reasoning style. The AFB benchmark scenarios are also GPT-5.1 generated — while GPT-5.1 is simultaneously one of the models evaluated on AFB. The Sales Arena experiment appears to be a limited single-configuration run with no repetitions or variance reported. Spider evaluation follows the convention of reporting on the dev set (no held-out test), which is standard but worth noting. This is an arXiv preprint; no peer review. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Training runs on 2× NVIDIA H200 GPUs with 9 hours for the GRPO stage and ~45 minutes for SFT (3 epochs × 15 min); inference runs on a 4B model deployable on a single consumer GPU. - Efficient: At 4B parameters, ReTAS is roughly 8× smaller than the 32B baselines it outperforms on attribution, making it substantially more efficient for deployment; the GRPO training compute is not trivial but is within reach of academic labs. - Effective: On the structured FinQA and Spider attribution tasks the evidence of effectiveness is solid; on the AFB generalization test the results are encouraging but the benchmark is synthetic and the improvement in absolute numbers is more modest. - Scalable: The paper demonstrates TAS prompt improvements scale from 4B to 32B (Figures 4–5), but all training is done only at 4B; whether GRPO-trained dialectical alignment at 7B or 13B yields further gains is untested. The work delivers a trained model (weights referenced but not explicitly confirmed released) and the FinQA-TAS / Spider-TAS datasets; it stops short of a production-ready system for open-ended agent tasks. Strengths: - The benchmark design for AFB is clever: by constructing scenarios with no deterministic ground truth, any systematic directional shift in attribution is cleanly attributable to role-induced bias rather than model accuracy variance. - The finding that introducing a dual-role reviewer without a synthesis mechanism makes performance worse than single-view reflection (GLM-4.6 Dual View: 43.1% Acc vs. Single View: 43.7% Acc on FinQA-TAS; $135 vs. $157 profit in Sales Arena) is a counter-intuitive and practically important result that directly challenges the naive "add a critic" design pattern prevalent in multi-agent frameworks. - Attribution labels in the training/evaluation sets are derived from a deterministic fact-checking process rather than LLM judgment, which anchors the main quantitative claims to objective ground truth rather than self-referential evaluation. - The evidence complexity analysis (Figure 6) showing ReTAS (4B) maintains performance at 4+ evidence pieces while QwQ-32B degrades sharply reveals a genuine robustness advantage, not just average-metric gains. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly acknowledge that the evaluation is restricted to FinQA and Spider — structured, verifiable-attribution tasks — and that open-ended settings with long-horizon planning or subjective fault attribution are out of scope. They note AFB relies on synthetic data for isolating bias and that in-domain real-world data would strengthen validation. They treat the Sales Arena as a preliminary probe, explicitly reserving broader negotiation settings for future work. These are substantive and honest scope boundaries. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The entire TAS corpus is generated by GPT-5.1, whose own reasoning biases are thus baked into ReTAS's learned behavior. This makes it impossible to determine whether ReTAS has learned principled dialectical reasoning or has learned to mimic GPT-5.1's surface-level text patterns for these specific task formats. - The AFB benchmark used to demonstrate AOA across models is also generated by GPT-5.1, and GPT-5.1 is simultaneously the strongest baseline on AFB (Table 2, V-AOA = 5% Human-Agent). This self-referential setup means the benchmark may be inadvertently calibrated to GPT-5.1's reasoning style, giving it an unfair structural advantage. - The V-AOA metric on FinQA-TAS/Spider-TAS is meaningful only if the ground-truth labels are well-calibrated across the three categories (FalseExt: 984/6251 = 16% of FinQA train; FalseInt: 2952/6251 = 47%); the heavy internal-fault skew means accuracy and V-AOA metrics may be driven largely by the dominant class rather than genuine bias correction. - The Sales Arena evaluation lacks statistical rigor: no repetitions, no confidence intervals, and the economic parameters (fixed buyer acceptance thresholds) are hand-designed in ways that may favor the TAS approach structurally. - The claim that ReTAS "generalizes" to the AFB benchmark is weakened by the fact that FinQA-TAS and Spider-TAS training trajectories are generated by GPT-5.1 using the same dialectical prompt structure as the AFB evaluation setup — the model may be pattern-matching to prompt format rather than internalizing attribution reasoning. Bottom Line: A practitioner building multi-agent LLM systems will find the empirical finding about dual-view prompting backfiring and the TAS reasoning template directly actionable, but should treat the full ReTAS model as a proof-of-concept on structured RAG tasks rather than a deployable solution for open-ended agent attribution.
Recommended 2604.19548 PDF
#74 — Innovative (64)

HoPart: Hop-Constrained Partitioning with Routing Support for Multi-FPGA Systems

past.date-conference.com · hmetis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces HoPart, a hop-constrained partitioning and congestion-aware routing co-framework for multi-FPGA systems that enforces per-path hop limits throughout all partitioning phases and allocates edge counts to routing paths proportionally to their timing criticality. - Why it's important: Timing closure in multi-FPGA VLSI emulation and prototyping is directly limited by inter-FPGA hop delays and TDM overhead; prior partitioners enforce hop constraints only at assignment and ignore routing-phase congestion, leaving substantial delay on the table. - How it does it: An alternating two-phase Violation-Aware Node Relocation (VANR) heuristic interleaves movement of assigned nodes with reassignment of violating nodes during refinement, while an adaptive edge-count router computes each path's allowed hops as k = ⌈delay_P(s,t) × (MH+1) / MD⌉, steering non-critical paths to shorter routes and timing-critical paths to lower-delay ones. - The good: Across 30 Titan23 benchmarks, HoPart reduces maximum path delay by 30% and runtime by 59% versus MaPart while cutting the cut size to 43% of MaPart's, with consistent improvement across all 30 circuits. - The missing: Only one baseline (MaPart) is compared on the delay metric; SPARK, EasyPart, and TritonPart are cited as related but not evaluated on delay, and no ablation separates the contributions of the partitioning and routing stages. - Read it or not: Worth reading for EDA researchers working on multi-FPGA partitioning and routing co-optimization — the VANR mechanism and criticality-proportional edge-count allocation are concise, reproducible ideas with strong empirical support, but confirm claims against additional baselines before adopting.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Strong consistency across all 30 Titan23 benchmarks (30% delay reduction, 59% runtime, 43% cut size) supports Rigor (65) and Resilience (65), but no ablation isolating VANR vs routing, hand-tuned α with no sensitivity analysis, MaPart numbers not re-run, and no code release temper this. Novelty (65) for VANR's alternating two-phase scheme and criticality-proportional edge-count allocation; Non-Obviousness (65). Completeness (50) is hurt by the absence of any acknowledged limitations and single-baseline scope (SPARK/EasyPart/TritonPart not compared on delay). Impact (60) — concrete algorithmic recipe but no released artifact or physical timing-closure validation. Composite ≈ 0.20·65 + 0.25·65 + 0.15·65 + 0.15·50 + 0.15·65 + 0.10·60 = 62.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 50 Resilience 65 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Multi-FPGA platforms for VLSI logic emulation and prototyping face a fundamental bottleneck: limited inter-FPGA I/O bandwidth forces the use of time-division multiplexing (TDM), which adds delay proportional to the TDM ratio, and circuits too large for direct partitioning must route signals across intermediate FPGA nodes (hops), multiplying that delay. Signal path delay is therefore a joint function of hop count and TDM ratio, and both depend on the partitioning and routing decisions. Prior approaches either avoid hops during partitioning but cannot guarantee hop-free solutions on large circuits (TopoPart, TopoPart+), optimize delay post-hoc via net-weight adjustment within an unmodified partitioner (Liou et al.), or — most relevantly — minimize maximum hop count as a proxy for delay (MaPart) but enforce hop constraints only during the assignment phase, leaving the refinement phase unconstrained and failing to account for routing-stage congestion. The gap is a partitioning-and-routing co-flow that (1) enforces hop and resource constraints throughout all refinement stages and (2) directly optimizes maximum path delay as the primary objective. Key Idea The central insight is that hop-constraint violations left unresolved during partitioning refinement — because existing nodes occupy the only feasible FPGAs — can be cleared by an alternating two-phase relocation: first move assigned nodes with positive hop gain (or that are blocking sensitive violating nodes) to free space, then place the violating nodes into the freed slots. Coupled with a routing stage that proportionally allocates the number of allowed hops per path based on its current delay relative to the global maximum delay, the system converts a constraint-satisfaction problem into a delay-minimization problem end-to-end without requiring a global re-solve. Methodology The pipeline has two main stages. In the partitioning stage, KaHyPar performs initial hypergraph partitioning into regions ignoring hop constraints, followed by a greedy pairwise FPGA-mapping swap that minimizes a distance-weighted cut cost (Eq. 5). Nodes violating resource or hop constraints are marked unassigned; a candidate FPGA propagation algorithm (adapted from MaPart with BFS deduplication) computes feasible FPGAs for each unassigned node. The VANR algorithm (Algorithm 1) then proceeds in two alternating phases: Phase 1 iterates over assigned nodes, moving each to the maximum-gain FPGA in its movable set and opportunistically placing sensitive violating nodes into the vacated slot; Phase 2 iterates over remaining violating nodes, selecting the minimum-hop candidate FPGA and evicting existing nodes to that FPGA's highest-gain destination to free capacity. A memory-pointer mechanism avoids redundant traversal. A timing-driven FM-style refinement then refines the partition using gain(u, fi→fj) = Δhop + α × (CUT(i,j) − CUT̄), balancing hop reduction against equalization of inter-FPGA cut distribution (α = 0.01, empirically set). In the congestion-aware routing stage, source-to-sink pairs are sorted by criticality (higher k first), and each path is routed in a k-layer FPGA graph via Dijkstra's algorithm, where k = ⌈delay_P(s,t) × (MH+1) / MD⌉ (Eq. 13); k increments if no feasible path exists. Edge weights follow the PathFinder negotiated-routing rule (Eq. 12), incorporating a criticality factor A_ij = D_ij / MD that directs timing-critical paths to core resources. A rip-up and rerouting loop targets the maximum-delay path, removes all sharing paths, and reroutes until delay values converge. Critical design decisions: (1) using KaHyPar for initial partitioning before applying hop constraints — justified by its ability to cluster highly correlated nodes; (2) the two-phase alternating VANR structure — motivated by the observation that greedy one-pass assignment saturates core FPGA resources; (3) α = 0.01 — stated as empirically determined, with no sensitivity analysis. Key Results Evaluated on 30 benchmarks from the Titan23 suite, ranging from small circuits (~5,000 nodes, e.g., neuron) to large designs (~1.5M nodes, case8). Compared exclusively against MaPart [13] (results taken directly from the MaPart paper, not re-run). Average ratios over all 30 benchmarks: cut size 0.43×, maximum delay 0.70× (30% reduction), runtime 0.41× (59% faster). In the violation-reduction experiment (MH=1), HoPart reduces violating nodes by 95.79% on average; two benchmarks (stereo_vision, segmentation) reach zero violations. Representative headline numbers: sparcT1_core delay 15 vs. 24 (MaPart); gsm_switch delay 29 vs. 65. No weak baseline concern for MaPart itself, which is the published state-of-the-art — but SPARK, EasyPart, and TritonPart are not compared on delay. All experiments on a single AMD Ryzen 5700 3.4 GHz Linux workstation. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The genuine novelty is (1) the VANR two-phase alternating violation-correction heuristic integrated within partitioning refinement, which is not found in prior multi-FPGA partitioners, and (2) the criticality-proportional edge-count allocation for routing (Eq. 13). The remaining components — KaHyPar initialization, PathFinder-based negotiated routing, FM-style refinement gain, rip-up and rerouting — are standard applications of well-established techniques applied to the multi-FPGA setting. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablation study is presented; it is impossible to determine what fraction of the 30% delay reduction comes from the partitioning improvements (VANR, timing-driven refinement) versus the routing improvements (adaptive edge-count routing, rip-up/reroute). No error bars, multiple seeds, or statistical tests are reported. The α = 0.01 hyperparameter is set empirically with no sensitivity analysis. MaPart results are taken from its original publication rather than re-run on the same machine, which confounds the runtime comparison. No public code repository or benchmark release is mentioned. The evaluation is confined to Titan23, the same benchmark suite on which MaPart was tuned and evaluated, raising a mild Goodhart's Law concern. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (methods evaluated by running experiments on benchmark circuits). - Practical: Runs on a commodity AMD Ryzen workstation in C++; no special hardware or accelerator required. - Efficient: 59% faster than MaPart at 43% of the cut size; the (MH+1)-layer graph construction is explicitly argued to be low-memory because the FPGA graph is much smaller than the circuit graph. - Effective: The 30% delay reduction is measured directly as maximum path delay after routing, the stated objective — though no conversion to actual FPGA operating frequency or timing closure report is provided, so the metric is a proxy. - Scalable: Tested on circuits up to ~1.5M nodes (case8, ~7,600 max delay in MaPart); no evaluation beyond Titan23 or discussion of asymptotic scaling behavior at 10×–100× larger designs. No public software artifact is released; the paper stops at publication form. Strengths - The VANR alternating two-phase mechanism is a practically motivated and well-specified algorithm that directly addresses the gap in MaPart's refinement phase, achieving 95.79% constraint-violation reduction across 30 diverse benchmarks. - The criticality-proportional edge-count formula (Eq. 13) is a clean, dimensionally consistent, and implementable adaptation of PathFinder that differentially allocates routing resources based on path urgency — concise and generalizable. - Simultaneous improvement in all three metrics (delay, cut size, runtime) across every single one of the 30 benchmarks is a strong consistency signal that the gains are not cherry-picked or artifact-specific. - The memory-pointer mechanism in VANR Phase 2 is a practical engineering optimization that prevents O(n²) redundant traversal during iterative violation correction. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors do not explicitly acknowledge any limitations, failure modes, or scope boundaries. The conclusion section restates the positive experimental results without identifying conditions under which HoPart might underperform, cases where hop constraints remain unsatisfied, sensitivity to hyperparameter α, or directions for future work. This is a credibility negative. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Single-baseline comparison: SPARK [12], EasyPart [15], and TritonPart [14] are cited as related work with limitations but are not compared on the delay metric. The claim that HoPart is state-of-the-art on delay is unsubstantiated against these methods. - No component ablation: Without experiments isolating VANR, timing-driven refinement, adaptive edge-count routing, and rip-up/reroute individually, the attribution of the 30% delay improvement to any specific contribution is speculative. - α = 0.01 is hand-tuned with no sensitivity analysis; circuits with different inter-FPGA connectivity ratios may require different values. - MaPart re-run concern: taking baseline numbers from a different paper without verifying on the same hardware conflates algorithmic and platform differences in the runtime comparison. - No actual timing closure validation: the delay metric (sum of TDM ratios) is a model, not a measured clock frequency on physical FPGA hardware or after place-and-route. - No code release, limiting independent verification. Bottom Line EDA researchers working on multi-FPGA partitioning and routing should read this paper for the VANR violation-correction mechanism and adaptive edge-count routing, which deliver consistent, large-magnitude gains over MaPart on a standard benchmark suite, but should reserve judgment on whether HoPart is the field's best method until ablations and comparisons against SPARK and EasyPart on the delay metric are available.
Search Results
#75 — Innovative (63)

OMIBench: Benchmarking Olympiad-Level Multi-Image Reasoning in Large Vision-Language Model

arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces OMIBench, a 1,322-problem benchmark that evaluates Olympiad-level scientific reasoning when required evidence is distributed across multiple images, spanning biology, chemistry, mathematics, and physics with 100% expert-verified step-by-step rationale coverage. - Why it's important: Existing Olympiad-level multimodal benchmarks restrict problems to single images, yet real scientific reasoning routinely requires integrating evidence across multiple interdependent figures — a capability gap that current evaluation suites leave unmeasured. - How it does it: Problems are sourced directly from international Olympiad archives (IMO, ICHO, CPHO, etc.) and verified by competition-level annotators; evaluation uses both exact-match accuracy and GPTScore (LLM-as-judge semantic equivalence), with a clean information-equivalent single-image ablation to isolate cross-image integration as the specific bottleneck. - The good: The information-equivalent single-image control — concatenating all images into one composite while keeping text unchanged — isolates cross-image organizational difficulty cleanly: GPT-4o improves from 24.05% to 33.71% ACC when all images are merged, confirming that multi-image structure itself, not visual volume or OCR noise, causes the performance gap. - The missing: The human baseline covers only 52 problems with 4 participants, and the error analysis reports inconsistent percentages between the main text (13% visual, 29% cross-image, 41% logical) and Appendix F.2.1 (35%, 30%, 25%), an unaddressed discrepancy that undermines confidence in failure-mode attribution. - Read it or not: Researchers working on multimodal reasoning evaluation or cross-image integration in VLMs should read this — the ablation design is sound and the scaling analysis is thorough; those focused on model architecture rather than benchmarking can skim the key results.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. The information-equivalent single-image ablation (Non-Obviousness 70) is the standout — concatenating all images into one composite while holding text constant cleanly attributes the bottleneck to cross-image organization rather than visual volume, and the text-only ICL outperforming multi-image ICL is genuinely counterintuitive. Novelty (65) reflects the first benchmark jointly requiring Olympiad-level difficulty and multi-image evidence integration, with moderate Spearman correlations (<0.7) confirming it reshapes rankings. Rigor (65) is supported by kappa=0.86, GPTScore-vs-human validation (ρ≥0.86), and a thorough scaling/enhancement matrix, but pulled down by inconsistent error-distribution statistics between main text (13/29/41%) and Appendix F.2.1 (35/30/25%) — an unaddressed discrepancy that undermines failure-mode attribution. Completeness (60) suffers from a thin 52-problem/4-participant human baseline and unresolved chemistry-specific confounds. Practical Impact (55) reflects a released dataset and evaluation scripts, no trained artifact. Weighted: 0.20*65 + 0.25*65 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*55 ≈ 63.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 60 Resilience 60 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing multimodal Olympiad benchmarks — OlympiadBench, MathVista, MathVerse — frame every problem with a single image. Existing multi-image benchmarks — MMIU, MuirBench, Blink — target basic visual perception, narrative reasoning, or cross-image reference resolution at low reasoning depth. Real scientific problems in exams and research routinely require synthesizing evidence across multiple interdependent diagrams (e.g., a geometry problem with three differently configured figures, a chemistry problem with reaction scheme plus structural diagrams). No benchmark simultaneously requires both Olympiad-level multi-step quantitative reasoning and multi-image evidence integration. The result is that LVLM evaluation systematically misses this joint capability, potentially masking severe weaknesses in cross-image reasoning. Key Idea: Olympiad difficulty and multi-image evidence distribution are orthogonal axes that interact multiplicatively in difficulty. OMIBench captures problems where the images are not redundant or decorative but carry distinct, complementary sub-evidence that must be jointly integrated to derive the answer. The core construction insight is to source problems from Olympiad archives where multi-image structure arises organically from the problem design, rather than artificially splitting single-image problems — ensuring the multi-image requirement is inherent to the reasoning task. Methodology: Data construction proceeds in four phases. Phase 1 collects problems from international Olympiad archives (IMO, CMO, EGMO for mathematics; ICHO, Chemistry Race for chemistry; ASO, INBO for biology; CUPT, CPHO for physics) and integrates existing benchmarks (OlympiadBench, Mv-MATH, EMMA), using Mathpix OCR to convert PDFs to Markdown with expert manual verification. Phase 2 generates expert-verified step-by-step rationales via a two-stage pipeline: Gemini-2.5-pro-thinking samples up to 16 candidate solutions per problem, retaining those with correct final answers; annotators who have competition-level domain training (bronze-medal threshold) verify and refine via a three-tier protocol (minor edits / substantive revision / complete rewrite), with annotation kappa=0.86. Phase 3 applies dual-review quality control with weekly 5% random sampling. Phase 4 assigns fine-grained topic labels via GPT-4o pre-labeling + K-means clustering on RoBERTa embeddings + manual correction. Evaluation uses exact-match accuracy (LCS ratio ≥75% for text; |v̂−v| ≤ 10⁻⁴ for numeric) and GPTScore (GPT-4-mini as binary judge assessing both answer correctness and reasoning soundness). Models are evaluated zero-shot with boxed-format CoT prompting. Enhancement experiments span: 4 CoT prompting variants (standard CoT, VoT, Least-to-Most, Plan-and-Solve); 3 test-time scaling axes (sequential via token budget 512→16,384; parallel via self-consistency k∈{1,3,4,8,16}; parameter scaling 1B→235B); 3 ICL conditions (no-image, single-image, multi-image demonstrations); 2 think-with-image approaches (tool-based: GPT-4o + VisualSketchpad; internal: EMU-3.5-34B); SFT on CMMCoT and MMDU; external tool integration (Visual Sketchpad, SlowPerception, CogFlow on GPT-4o and GPT-5). Key Results: Gemini-3-Pro-Preview is the top performer at 42.79% ACC / 50.53% GPTScore overall. On a 52-problem human baseline subset, human experts reach 80–83% and trained non-experts reach 58–62%, leaving a >30-point gap to the best model. Gemini-3-Pro drops from 75.67% on single-image OlympiadBench to 50.53% on OMIBench (>25% absolute, ~33% relative). Spearman correlations with related benchmarks are moderate: ρ=0.614 vs. OlympiadBench and ρ=0.535 vs. MMIU, both below the 0.7 strong-correlation threshold, confirming OMIBench reshapes model rankings. Performance degrades sharply with image count: ~40% for single-image problems, dropping below 15% for ≥6 images. The information-equivalent single-image control (GPT-4o: 33.71% vs 24.05% ACC) cleanly attributes the bottleneck to cross-image organization. Standard CoT prompting variants show no statistically significant differences. Thinking-mode Qwen3-VL variants outperform instruct variants by ~10% (e.g., Qwen3-VL-32B-Thinking: 47.34% vs 35.87% ACC) — a larger gain than the same comparison yields on MathVista (<5%). Sequential token scaling yields near-monotonic gains from 512 to 16,384 tokens. MoE models plateau on total parameter count but improve near-linearly on activated parameter count. SFT on CMMCoT yields <2% improvement; SFT on MMDU slightly degrades. Both tool-based and internal think-with-image approaches degrade performance. Human error analysis (100 problems) finds logical errors in 46% of key reasoning steps in o4-mini and Gemini-3 outputs. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The benchmark construction methodology (Olympiad collection + rationale annotation) applies known techniques. What is genuinely novel is (1) the first benchmark requiring simultaneous Olympiad-level difficulty and multi-image evidence integration, and (2) the empirical isolation of cross-image organizational difficulty — rather than visual volume — as the primary performance bottleneck via a controlled ablation. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present: the information-equivalent single-image control is methodologically clean and directly answers the confounding question. GPTScore reliability is validated via Spearman correlation ≥0.86 (p<0.05) against human ratings from 200 Gemini-3-Pro-Preview outputs, and stability is confirmed across three independent sampling runs (total Std <0.5% GPTScore, <0.3% ACC). Annotation kappa=0.86 is reported for rationale correctness. Dataset and evaluation scripts are released at https://github.com/LightChen233/OMIBench and HuggingFace. Methodological concerns: (1) partial results for OlympiadBench comparison are borrowed from other papers, introducing heterogeneous evaluation conditions; (2) GPTScore evaluators (GPT-4o-mini, GPT-5-mini, Gemini-2.5-flash) may lack the domain knowledge to verify Olympiad-level chemistry or biology reasoning steps — reliability is only checked against each other and against GPT-4-class models, not domain-expert step-level human annotation; (3) the paper is a preprint with no peer review. Reported as arXiv:2604.20806v1. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Evaluation runs on commercially available A800/A100 GPUs for open-source models; closed-source models via standard APIs. The benchmark itself requires no special infrastructure. - Efficient: Each evaluation requires model inference plus one GPTScore API call per instance (1,322 × 2 operations per model).
