Rigor & Reproducibility The paper provides component-level ablation: shared memory vs. none (Figures 9–10, Table 7), graph transformation vs. none (Table 5), different partition schemes (Table 6), and shared memory size sensitivity (Figure 7, Table 8). GPU profiler metrics from NVIDIA Nsight Compute are reported for IPC, stall cycles, L1 hit rate, SM occupancy, and thread block occupancy across varying shared memory sizes — a thorough micro-architectural justification. Results are averages of 3 runs; no variance, standard deviation, or confidence intervals are reported. No code or artifact link is provided. The correctness proof (Section 4.3) covers two canonical cases (all-local triangle and 3-partition-spanning triangle) and is informal but structurally sound. No public code repository is mentioned. Deployability SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: Runs on a single commodity NVIDIA GPU (V100 16 GB or A6000 48 GB) with standard CUDA; a hidden practical constraint is that the full graph plus the largest partition must simultaneously fit in CPU RAM, which is unquantified for graphs approaching CPU memory limits. - Efficient: Workload partitioning runs on CPU (e.g., 78.22s for twitter including 3.38s transformation overhead); kernel execution is fast (16.84s twitter on 1 GPU) and consistently outperforms baselines on 2–4 GPUs for 4 of 5 large graphs. - Effective: Empirically outperforms multi-GPU baselines on both total and kernel time for 4 of 5 large datasets; twitter kernel time does not beat Tricore's 2-GPU kernel time due to dense connections causing more surviving duplicate edges. - Scalable: Tested on graphs up to 6.6B edges (uk-2007); multi-GPU MPI extension achieves only 2.31–2.38× on 4 GPUs (sub-linear), indicating the communication-free design partially offsets reduced per-partition parallelism. No software artifact is publicly released or referenced in the paper. Strengths - The zero-in-degree proxy removal insight is clean, provably correct, and practically effective — reducing vertex count by up to 41.87% and enabling graphs that cannot fit GPU memory even with 16 partitions (without transformation) to fit with 4 partitions (with transformation), as shown in Table 5 for uk-2007. - The systems result is compelling: fewer hardware resources (1 GPU) beating more resources (2–4 GPUs) on both total time and kernel time for most large graphs, which directly addresses a real deployment cost concern. - The micro-architectural analysis (Table 7–8, Figures 9–10) is unusually thorough for a systems paper — reporting IPC, stall cycles per instruction, L1 hit rate, SM occupancy, and thread block occupancy across five shared memory size configurations provides strong causal evidence for performance claims. - The partitioning framework is generic: it accepts any vertex-partition scheme as a plug-in, demonstrated with random, METIS, and contiguous partitioning. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly note: (1) twitter kernel time does not beat Tricore (2-GPU) due to dense connections causing more surviving duplicate edges post-transformation; (2) METIS partitioning overhead dominates and makes it impractical despite potential locality benefits; (3) shared memory size has an optimal point at 128 neighbors for their evaluated graphs — beyond this, reduced SM occupancy degrades performance. These are addressed directly with profiler and timing data, which is credible. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The primary comparison's hardware asymmetry is underemphasized: LSTC (1 GPU) vs Tricore/TRUST (2–4 GPUs). The paper frames this as a resource advantage without explicitly stating that baselines use multi-GPU only because they out-of-memory on 1 GPU — readers may misread the 2.8× speedup as algorithmic superiority rather than a combined hardware+algorithm effect. - TC-Stream (Huang et al., TPDS'22, reference [20]) explicitly targets large-graph triangle counting on a single machine with multiple GPUs and is cited but never compared against; this is the most directly relevant omission. - CPU RAM requirements are unquantified: the full graph in CSR plus the largest partition must fit simultaneously in CPU RAM. For graphs like friendster (1.8B edges, ~27 GB in CSR) this constraint may be binding on workstations, but no CPU memory usage figures are reported. - The correctness proof covers the all-local (Case 1) and 3-partition-spanning (Case 2) configurations, but does not explicitly address the 2-partition case where A is in partition Pi and B, C span Pi and Pj — this is subsumed by Case 2 but should be made explicit for rigor. - Sensitivity analysis for partition count n is only demonstrated for uk-2007 and gsh-host; no systematic evaluation of how n affects runtime, memory balance, or transformation effectiveness for graphs with skewed degree distributions is provided. Bottom Line This is a solid systems paper with a clean partitioning insight, well-executed GPU optimizations, and strong empirical evidence — practitioners who need triangle counting on large graphs with limited GPU hardware will find LSTC directly actionable, though comparison against TC-Stream and clearer hardware-normalized baselines would strengthen the contribution claim.

#88 — Innovative (62)

HGNN-Part: A High-Quality Hypergraph Partitioner Based on Hypergraph Generative Model

past.date-conference.com · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces HGNN-Part, a VLSI hypergraph partitioner that replaces GNN-based clique expansion with native HypergraphConv layers inside a variational autoencoder, adds dual-expansion-graph spectral features, and applies randomized matrix decomposition to accelerate preprocessing. - Why it's important: Prior deep learning partitioners convert hypergraphs to ordinary graphs before applying GNNs, losing high-order connectivity and producing dense adjacency matrices that exhaust GPU memory on large VLSI netlists — the exact instances where quality matters most. - How it does it: HypergraphConv message-passing operates directly on the sparse incidence matrix H (vertex→hyperedge→vertex), and a GPU-optimized scatter-multiplication algorithm computes the normalized hyperedge cut loss without materializing expanded adjacency structures. - The good: On the ISPD05 test benchmarks HGNN-Part achieves 5.02% average cutsize reduction over hMETIS versus GenPart's 3.13%, uses 2.0–4.4× less GPU memory than GenPart (enabling a 24 GB RTX 4090 where GenPart required a 32 GB V100), and handles Titan23 instances GenPart could not run, achieving 15.89% cutsize improvement over hMETIS there. - The missing: The improvement over GenPart on the ISPD98 training set is only 0.11% with no error bars or multi-seed variance reported, making statistical significance unclear; and there is no ablation isolating the HGNN encoder change from the new features and loss redesign. - Read it or not: Worth reading for EDA/physical-design researchers and ML-for-combinatorial-optimization practitioners who need memory-efficient deep learning partitioning on large VLSI netlists; those needing k > 2 or weighted hyperedges will find the current scope too narrow.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Practical Impact (70) is the strongest contributor — code, weights, and a 164-hypergraph dataset are released, and the 2–4.4× GPU memory reduction enables consumer 24 GB hardware plus Titan23 instances GenPart cannot run. Novelty (65) is moderate: native HypergraphConv on the incidence matrix is a clean substitution rather than a fundamental advance, with the algebraic star-expansion-to-SVD reduction adding a nice engineering insight (Non-Obviousness 60). Rigor (60) is undermined by no error bars or seeds, the 0.11% ISPD98 margin being within plausible variance, prior baselines cited from GenPart rather than re-run, and no ablation isolating the HGNN encoder from feature/loss changes. Resilience (55) is limited by the small ISPD98 margin and decades-old benchmarks; Completeness (65) is saved by honest k=2/uniform-weight scope acknowledgment. Weighted: 0.20*65 + 0.25*60 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*70 ≈ 62.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 65 Resilience 55 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation VLSI circuit netlists map naturally to hypergraphs (cells = vertices, nets = hyperedges), and minimizing hyperedge cut across balanced partitions reduces interconnect delay, power, and area. Multilevel methods (hMETIS, KaHyPar) lack global information; spectral methods (SpecPart, K-SpecPart) scale poorly to millions of nodes. Recent deep learning approaches — GAP, MedPart, GenPart — all expand hypergraphs into ordinary graphs before applying GNNs, which (a) discards the exact multi-way connectivity encoded in high-order hyperedges and (b) produces clique-expanded adjacency matrices with nnz(Ac) >> nnz(H), saturating GPU memory on large instances. GenPart (ICCAD 2024) is the strongest prior work but is limited by this GNN dependency and by training exclusively on 18 ISPD98 benchmarks. A secondary gap is open-source VLSI hypergraph data scarcity, which constrains all learning-based approaches. Key Idea The core insight is that HypergraphConv message-passing on the raw incidence matrix H preserves native high-order relationships while being far more memory-efficient than clique-expanded GraphConv, because H is sparse (nnz(H) corresponds to pin counts) whereas the clique expansion Ac can be dense. An algebraic observation enables efficient preprocessing: eigenvectors of the star expansion graph Gs equal left singular vectors of H, so computing Gs spectral features reduces to randomized SVD on H rather than eigendecomposition of a larger matrix. Methodology The pipeline has three stages. In preprocessing (CPU), a 7-dimensional node feature vector is built per vertex: first two eigenvectors of the clique expansion graph Gc (randomized eigendecomposition, Algorithm 2), first two eigenvectors of the star expansion graph Gs (= left singular vectors of H via randomized SVD, Algorithm 1, with oversampling s=10, power iterations q=5), node degree, pin count, and an initial partition ID from hMETIS. Both randomized algorithms follow Halko et al. (2011). The model (GPU) is a Variational Hypergraph Autoencoder (VHGAE): an encoder of five HypergraphConv layers (hidden dim 256, latent dim 64) outputting per-node µ and σ, with reparameterization sampling z = µ + σ⊙η; and a decoder of three linear layers plus softmax producing k-way partition probabilities. At inference, m=12 latent samples are decoded, each producing a candidate partition, then parallel V-Cycle refinement via KaHyPar is applied, iterating τ=11 rounds. Training optimizes L = αL_KL + βL_NCut + γL_Bal with α=5×10⁻⁴, β=0.5, γ=100. L_NCut uses a scatter-multiplication strategy (Algorithm 3) over sorted hyperedge indices, avoiding explicit net expansion. L_Bal is normalized by expected block size |V|/k to stabilize training across variable-size graphs (GenPart's original balance loss was not normalized). Adam optimizer, lr=5×10⁻⁴, 50 epochs, RTX 4090. Key Results On ISPD98 (training, ε=2%): HGNN-Part achieves 3.43% average cutsize improvement over hMETIS, versus GenPart's 3.32%, K-SpecPart's 2.48%, SpecPart's 1.83% — a 0.11% margin over GenPart. At ε=10%, the margin widens to 0.06% (4.85% vs. 4.79%). On ISPD05 (test, ε=2%): HGNN-Part achieves 5.02% improvement versus GenPart's 3.13%, tested on 8 benchmarks ranging from 211k (adaptec1) to 2.17M vertices (bigblue4). At ε=10%: 1.97% vs. 1.13%. Training on the extended 164-hypergraph dataset (Ours+) further improves ISPD05 ε=2% performance to 5.20%. On Titan23 instances (up to 3.07M vertices), GenPart cannot run due to memory limits; HGNN-Part achieves 15.89% cutsize improvement over hMETIS. Randomized preprocessing saves up to ~80 seconds (~25% of total time) on the largest ISPD98 instances. The runtime of HGNN-Part is broadly comparable to hMETIS, with V-Cycle refinement dominating and inference being nearly negligible. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The first application of native HypergraphConv in a variational generative framework for hypergraph partitioning, with an accompanying GPU-efficient normalized hyperedge cut loss, is genuinely new for this problem setting. The 164-hypergraph VLSI dataset and the algebraic reduction of star-expansion eigenvectors to randomized SVD are also original contributions. The building blocks — VGAE, HypergraphConv, randomized SVD, V-Cycle refinement — are all prior work; novelty lies in their integration and VLSI-specific adaptation, not in any single algorithmic advance. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations in Table II cover four feature configurations (s-only, c-only, sc, sc+rand), confirming both expansion graphs contribute complementary information and that randomized decomposition does not degrade quality. However, there is no ablation isolating the HGNN encoder from the other changes (new features, normalized balance loss, redesigned cut loss), making it impossible to attribute the quality gain to any single component. No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported anywhere; margins over GenPart on ISPD98 (0.11%) are within plausible run-to-run variance. Results for all prior methods on ISPD98 are cited directly from GenPart [9] rather than re-run, introducing potential hardware/version confounds. Code is released at https://github.com/THU-numbda/HGNN-Part and the dataset at https://github.com/THU-numbda/VLSI-Hypergraph-Benchmarks — both are positive for reproducibility. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (method + built system evaluated on standard benchmarks). - Practical: Runs on a 24 GB RTX 4090 consumer GPU; prior GenPart required a 32 GB V100, so the hardware barrier is lower and commercially accessible. - Efficient: 2.0–4.4× GPU memory reduction over GenPart due to sparse incidence matrix representation; randomized SVD cuts preprocessing by ~25% on large instances; overall runtime is comparable to hMETIS on ISPD98 though preprocessing dominates for large graphs. - Effective: Evidence on ISPD05 test benchmarks (8 instances, up to 2.17M vertices) shows consistent cutsize reduction over all baselines; handles Titan23 instances competitors cannot. Evaluation is convincing for bisection with uniform weights. - Scalable: Tested up to ~3M vertices/hyperedges (Titan23); no experiments at 10×–100× beyond this scale, and the authors acknowledge extension to k > 2 and non-uniform weights is future work, limiting real VLSI deployment today. The work delivers a software artifact (code + dataset on GitHub) with working PyTorch/PyG implementation. Strengths - Native HGNN on the incidence matrix is both technically cleaner than clique expansion and practically impactful: the 2–4.4× memory reduction enables instances that were previously out of reach, and the Titan23 result (15.89% over hMETIS) is the strongest quantitative evidence in the paper. - The algebraic reduction of star-expansion eigenvectors to randomized SVD on H is a compact, well-motivated engineering insight that halves preprocessing cost without accuracy loss. - Releasing 164 VLSI hypergraphs (spanning multiple contest sources and up to 3M vertices) is a concrete service to the community given the acknowledged data scarcity. - The normalized balance loss fix (dividing by expected block size |V|/k) is a genuine stability improvement over GenPart's formulation, with principled motivation. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state the method is currently limited to k=2 (bisection) and uniform vertex/hyperedge weights. They identify extension to arbitrary k and non-uniform weights as future work. They acknowledge V-Cycle refinement via KaHyPar dominates runtime and can be slow when the initial solution quality is poor. The paper handles these limitations directly and does not minimize them. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The 0.11% margin over GenPart on ISPD98 (training set) is the primary comparison for most reported ablations, but with no variance estimates across multiple runs, it is not possible to determine whether this margin is statistically meaningful. - There is no controlled ablation that swaps only the GNN encoder for HGNN while holding features and loss identical; the reported improvements could arise from the new star-expansion features or the loss normalization rather than the HGNN encoder itself. - Prior method baselines are cited from GenPart [9], not re-run; cutsize can vary with software versions and random seeds, so the 0.11% gap is potentially within that uncertainty. - The generalization claim is based on ISPD98 → ISPD05 transfer, but both are decades-old academic benchmarks from the same EDA contest series; out-of-distribution generalization to modern sub-10nm VLSI designs (e.g., FinFET netlists with high-fan-out nets) is not evaluated. - The model still depends on hMETIS to produce the initial partition ID feature, creating a dependency on a closed-source commercial tool and making performance partly contingent on hMETIS quality. Bottom Line Practitioners working on large-scale VLSI hypergraph bisection will find HGNN-Part a direct upgrade over GenPart — meaningfully better on test benchmarks, more memory-efficient, and with released code and data — but the contribution is an incremental architectural substitution rather than a fundamental advance, and real deployment still awaits extension to multi-way partitioning and non-uniform weights.
Search Results
#89 — Innovative (62)

Layer-wise MoE Routing Locality under Shared-Prefix Code Generation: Token-Identity Decomposition and Compile-Equivalent Fork Redundancy

arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper measures inter-request MoE expert routing overlap across parallel code-generation branches sharing a common KV-cache prefix, revealing a layer-dependent crossing pattern between token-identity-driven and context-driven routing, and quantifies semantic redundancy of generated codes via gcc assembly equivalence classes. - Why it's important: Best-of-N sampling and multi-candidate code completion waste GPU compute if generated candidates are semantically redundant; understanding routing locality could enable non-approximate expert sharing across concurrent MoE requests and inform pruning strategies for beam search. - How it does it: Tree-search branching generation from a shared prefix using Qwen3.5-35B-A3B-FP8 produces 851 completed codes; per-token, per-layer expert IDs are extracted via SGLang, and gcc -S -O0 assembly equivalence classes control for token-identity confounds in pairwise Jaccard similarity analysis across 4 conditions (within/between O0-group × same/different token). - The good: Even at the strictest condition — different tokens generated between codes in different semantic equivalence classes — Jaccard similarity is 0.175 (11× the random baseline of 0.016), confirming that a shared prefix induces strong structural routing bias that persists across semantically distinct outputs. - The missing: The entire study uses one C sorting task on one model (Qwen3.5-35B-A3B-FP8) with only 20 sibling pairs for the decay analysis, leaving generalizability to other tasks, languages, and MoE architectures entirely unverified. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on MoE inference optimization (expert offloading, cross-request sharing, beam-search pruning), but engineers expecting actionable deployment improvements should note that no throughput gains are demonstrated — the paper stops at empirical characterization.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (70) — measurement methodology and layer-wise decomposition contradicting OpenMoE is a genuine new empirical finding. Rigor (50) — no error bars, decay analysis on only 20 sibling pairs, severely unbalanced token-type samples, no code release, single task/single model, thinking-skip confound. Non-Obviousness (70) — layer-dependent crossing pattern is non-obvious. Completeness (65) — author confronts six limitations directly, a positive, but actionable claims (cross-request expert sharing) remain entirely theoretical. Resilience (55) — narrow scope and small samples make findings vulnerable to scrutiny. Impact (55) — diagnostic only, no throughput improvement, no artifact. Composite ≈ 0.20·70 + 0.25·50 + 0.15·70 + 0.15·65 + 0.15·55 + 0.10·55 = 60.5 → 62.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 65 Resilience 55 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation In best-of-N sampling and multi-candidate code generation, parallel requests share a common KV cache prefix but execute MoE decode phases independently, activating experts separately per request. It is unknown how much expert routing overlaps across these branches or whether generated candidates are semantically diverse or redundantly similar. Prior MoE routing work (OpenMoE) concluded that routing is "context-independent" based on token-ID-level aggregation and a single-layer visualization, without verifying cross-request routing similarity or decomposing by layer. Separately, inference engines (SGLang, vLLM) share attention KV caches but not expert computation across parallel requests. Without characterizing inter-request routing locality and output semantic diversity, neither offloading strategies nor search algorithms can exploit structural redundancy in parallel generation. Key Idea The central technical insight is that a shared generation prefix induces strong structural bias in per-layer MoE expert activation across branched sequences — measurably at positions where different tokens are generated, not just where the same token recurs — and that this bias follows a layer-dependent crossing pattern: token identity dominates routing in input layers while context-dependent routing peaks in middle layers (L14–20), contradicting the OpenMoE "context-independent" claim through layer-wise decomposition. Paired with a compiler-output-based semantic grouping that strips superficial source variation (comments, whitespace), the paper shows that most parallel code generation output is semantically collapsed into a handful of equivalence classes. Methodology The study runs Qwen3.5-35B-A3B-FP8 (256 routed experts, top-8, 40 layers: 30 GDN linear-attention + 10 full-attention) via SGLang 0.5.9 on a single NVIDIA GH200 node. Tree-search branching generation uses Ptarget=0.40 cumulative probability threshold (temperature 0.7, top-p=0.95, top-k=20, max 2048 tokens), producing 5,745 forks and 851 completed codes within a 30-minute timeout. Expert IDs are extracted per token per layer via SGLang's --enable-return-routed-experts flag. A "thinking skip" (injecting an empty <think>\n\n</think>\n\n prefix) suppresses reasoning tokens from contaminating routing data. Compile-equivalence alignment classifies codes into O0-equivalent groups via gcc -S -O0 (aarch64) assembly; within-group token-sequence alignment uses Python difflib.SequenceMatcher to label positions as same-tok or diff-tok. Routing overlap is measured as Jaccard similarity J = |A∩B|/|A∪B| over 8-expert sets, reported per layer under 4 conditions: W-same, W-diff (within O0 group), B-same, B-diff (between O0 groups). Three design decisions: (1) Assembly equivalence rather than source AST for semantic grouping — well-justified to control for comment/whitespace noise. (2) Tree-search sibling pairs for exact shared-prefix control — correct approach, though only 20 sibling pairs were obtained, severely limiting statistical power of the decay analysis. (3) Thinking skip for prefix controllability — necessary but introduces a confounded generation condition where reasoning leaks into post-code comments, acknowledged by the authors. Key Results W-same mean Jaccard: 0.649 (40× random baseline 0.016), peaking at L0 = 0.828. B-diff mean Jaccard: 0.175 (11× random) — the strictest condition. Layer-wise crossing pattern: same-tok dips in middle layers (L14–20 ≈ 0.60–0.62) while diff-tok peaks at L14 = 0.223 (14× random). GDN vs. full-attention layers show no significant difference in routing characteristics (Mann-Whitney U, p > 0.8 for both conditions); layer position, not attention type, drives routing. Routing decay after fork (20 sibling pairs): same-tok Jaccard drops from 0.578 immediately post-fork to 0.294 after 200 steps (18× random); diff-tok stabilizes at ~5× random. Token-type dependence: keywords have highest same-tok J (0.685, n=23); identifiers highest diff-tok J (0.193, 12× random, n=7,112). At L14, one expert (id=132) appears in 67% of 244,216 token positions; 38.8% of expert pairs have zero co-activation. Code diversity: 691/851 codes compiled successfully (81%); 189 O0-equivalent groups; top 3 groups hold 67% of successfully compiled codes; 172 singletons (20%). Within-group: 99.6% of differences are comments or blank lines; execution-code differences account for 0.04%. Codes contain 12–30 post-code reasoning comment lines despite the prompt instructing "Do not include comments," attributed to the thinking-skip side effect. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW METHOD (measurement methodology). The layer×token-identity×O0-equivalence decomposition for cross-request MoE routing analysis is genuinely novel — prior work measured only intra-request routing. The compiler-output-based semantic grouping applied to LLM code diversity quantification is a non-obvious methodological combination. The layer-dependent crossing pattern (same-tok and diff-tok Jaccard crossing in middle layers) and the quantification of beam-search waste (99.6% comments/blanks within equivalence groups) are new empirical findings. No component in isolation is novel; the contribution is the combination and the specific measurement setting. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablations in the traditional sense — this is a measurement study. The GDN vs. full-attention comparison uses Mann-Whitney U tests (the one statistical test reported). No confidence intervals or bootstrapped error bounds accompany Jaccard values, including the decay analysis over only 20 sibling pairs — far too small for robust statistical claims. Token-type results (Table III) have severely unbalanced sample sizes (keyword same-tok n=23 vs. identifier same-tok n=11,058), making the keyword result unreliable. No code repository or data release is mentioned. Published as arXiv preprint (arXiv:2604.17182v1, April 2026), not peer-reviewed. Methodological concern: the thinking-skip condition is non-standard, and the branching diversity findings may be artifacts of this injection rather than properties of the model's normal generation — authors acknowledge this but do not provide a comparison with thinking enabled. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (measurement study collecting inference routing and code generation data). - Practical: Runs on a single NVIDIA GH200 node (96 GB HBM3); the measurement methodology transfers to any SGLang-compatible MoE deployment on server-grade hardware, though the --enable-return-routed-experts flag is non-standard. - Efficient: The measurement infrastructure is expensive (5,745 forks, 30-min timeout per task); production integration of routing extraction would add per-token overhead not quantified in the paper. - Effective: Routing locality measurements are internally consistent and exceed random baseline by large margins across all four conditions, but no throughput improvement from exploiting this locality is demonstrated — §V-C discusses implications qualitatively only. - Scalable: One task, one model, one temperature setting. The decay analysis uses 20 sibling pairs. No scale testing across tasks, models, or request concurrency levels. The paper delivers no software artifact; it stops at paper form. Strengths - The 4-condition (within/between O0-group × same/diff-tok) layer-wise decomposition is a principled design that isolates token-identity effects from context effects, providing a cleaner refutation of the OpenMoE "context-independent" claim than any prior work. - Using gcc -S -O0 assembly equivalence to define semantic code groups is rigorous — it strips all superficial source variation without relying on heuristics like edit distance or AST similarity, and the -O0 flag prevents the compiler from masking behavioral differences through optimization. - The comment-induced algorithm branching finding (natural-language text in comments triggers downstream dead-code structure divergence even when comments are explicitly prohibited) is a specific, mechanistic observation about LLM code generation behavior not previously documented. - Quantifying that 99.6% of within-group differences are comments or blank lines, with 67% of codes in 3 semantic groups, gives concrete evidence that current top-P and beam search strategies are severely inefficient at the semantic level — directly actionable for search algorithm design. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors enumerate six limitations explicitly: single C task with no cross-language or cross-domain generalization; tree search incomplete at timeout (851 of an exponentially larger space); alignment coverage ~26% (low); single MoE model; no throughput measurement translating routing locality to actual speedup; and the thinking-skip injection as a non-standard condition that may inflate comment-driven branching relative to normal generation. These are confronted directly and specifically — a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The 20-sibling-pair sample for the routing decay analysis is severely underpowered; decay curves from Fig. 4 lack error bars and could shift substantially with more pairs. No power analysis or confidence bounds are provided. - The actionable claim — that routing locality enables non-approximate expert sharing across parallel requests — is entirely theoretical. No prototype, no memory savings, no latency measurement exists. The gap between the measurement and a deployable system is large and unaddressed. - The keyword same-tok Jaccard (0.685) is presented as the highest-confidence result but rests on only n=23 positions versus n=11,058 for identifiers — the ranking may invert with adequate sample size. - All branching and diversity conclusions depend on the thinking-skip condition. Since the model outputs reasoning as post-code comments under this condition, the "comment-induced branching" finding is partly an artifact of the prompt engineering, not necessarily a property of normal deployment. No control experiment with thinking enabled is provided. - The critical branching threshold Pc ≈ 0.36 is stated as an empirical estimate from one run with no validation, and its sensitivity to temperature, model, or task is not characterized. Bottom Line This is a well-executed empirical characterization study that delivers specific, reproducible measurements of MoE routing locality in parallel code generation and introduces a compiler-alignment methodology that researchers in MoE inference optimization and code generation diversity can directly build on — but it stops at measurement, and practitioners expecting deployable throughput improvements will find none.
Citations 2604.17182 PDF
#90 — Innovative (61)

Thinking Like a Botanist: Challenging Multimodal Language Models with Intent-Driven Chain-of-Inquiry

arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PlantInquiryVQA, a benchmark of 24,950 plant images and 138,068 QA pairs organized as severity-conditioned, intent-driven multi-turn diagnostic chains for evaluating MLLMs on botanical disease reasoning. - Why it's important: Current MLLMs are only assessed on single-turn VQA; real-world plant diagnosis requires sequential, evidence-conditioned questioning, and failing to detect disease (false reassurance) carries direct agronomic costs to food security. - How it does it: A Chain-of-Inquiry (CoI) framework maps observed disease severity (mild/moderate/severe) to a latent epistemic intent (diagnosis/prognosis/management), which then selects the question-template trajectory; visual cues are extracted by Qwen3-VL-4B and QA chains assembled by Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct. - The good: Structured question-guided inquiry improves diagnostic correctness ~48% over direct diagnosis for two tested models, and the Cascading (auto-regressive) condition retains 96.3% of the oracle-history Guided efficiency—showing CoI structure, not perfect memory, drives the gains. - The missing: 94.7% of QA ground-truth answers are LLM-synthesized rather than independently verified by domain experts, creating a circularity risk: the benchmark largely measures agreement with Qwen2.5-7B's clinical vocabulary, not with independent human judgment. - Read it or not: Worth reading for multimodal benchmark designers and agricultural-AI researchers; those seeking a clinically authoritative plant pathology benchmark should note the synthetic ground-truth limitation before drawing deployment conclusions.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (65) and Non-Obviousness (65) are the strongest dimensions — operationalizing severity→intent→question-trajectory as a dataset-level structural property (rather than a prompting strategy) is a conceptually clean contribution, and the Cascading vs. Guided ablation cleanly separates structural scaffolding from oracle memory. Rigor (60) is moderated by the central circularity: 94.7% of QA ground-truth answers are LLM-synthesized by Qwen2.5-7B and validated by the same expert pool that designed the templates; LLM-as-judge with GPT-5/Gemini-3-Pro replaces one form of LLM circularity with another. Completeness (65) benefits from candid limitation acknowledgments (false reassurance, English-only, single static images) but the headline ~48% PSBT result is quantified for only 2 of 18 models. Practical Impact (55) reflects a released benchmark with no trained model and no fine-tuning experiments to validate the training split. Weighted: 0.20*65 + 0.25*60 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*55 ≈ 61.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 65 Resilience 55 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Existing VQA benchmarks treat each image as an independent input to a static QA set. In plant pathology, expert diagnosis is inherently adaptive: a botanist's questioning shifts based on observed severity—differential diagnosis for ambiguous mild symptoms, prognosis for moderate cases, and remediation planning for severe ones. Widely-used agricultural vision datasets (PlantVillage, PlantDoc, PlantSeg) address classification and segmentation but capture none of this adaptive inquiry structure. The result is that MLLMs are evaluated on a simplified proxy that cannot reveal whether a model sustains coherent, evidence-conditioned reasoning across multiple turns—precisely the capability required to be useful to a farmer or agronomist. Key Idea The core insight is that a botanist's question sequence is not arbitrary but is determined by a latent epistemic intent that is itself a function of observed disease severity. By formalizing the mapping severity→intent→question_type as an explicit dataset structural property (rather than a prompting strategy), the benchmark transforms a static leaf image into the starting point of a deterministic reasoning trajectory. This makes CoI an artifact of the data itself, ensuring models are tested on the cognitive shift—from identification, to prognosis, to management—that expert diagnosis requires. Methodology The pipeline has three phases. First, visual cue extraction: Qwen3-VL-4B (selected after benchmarking against Gemma3-4B and LLaVA-1.5 on 250 images, scoring 73.6% vs. 23.2% and 3.2% on visual grounding) extracts structured descriptors following an expert-designed Symptomatological Diagnostic Protocol covering lesion morphology, spatial distribution, and SAD-based severity quantification. GPT-4V cross-verifies outputs; flagged instances and 5,000 randomly sampled images receive expert Clinical Factuality review, achieving 93.8% accuracy. Second, CoI classification: six PhD/graduate-level botanists analyzed 600 images to derive 12 distinct CoI templates spanning 4 health statuses, 3 severity levels, 2 instance varieties, and 3 epistemic intents, grounded in Agrios (2005) and Strange (2003). Third, QA generation: Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct assembles dialogue chains from templates using a configuration tuple (condition, severity, intent, visual_cues), with reasoning modules (temporal_evolution, remediation_strategy, counterfactual) injected by intent. The dataset is partitioned 80/10/10 at image level to prevent QA leakage. Eighteen MLLMs are evaluated via a Cumulative Context Test. Metrics extend beyond lexical overlap to Disease Identification (Sdis), Safety (Ssafe, penalizing false reassurance), Clinical Utility (Sclin = 0.5·Sdis + 0.3·Sact − 0.2·(1−Ssafe)), Visual Grounding (Svg), Efficiency (E = verified cues per 100 words), Prevalence Bias (B), and Cross-Class Fairness (F). Critical design decisions: image-level partitioning is well-justified; hybrid validation is necessitated by scale and partially credible; the deterministic severity→intent mapping simplifies what is in practice a per-image expert judgment. Key Results Gemini-3-Flash leads all 18 models: F1=0.255, Sdis=0.444, Sclin=0.188, Ssafe=0.147, Svg=0.259, average response length 85.8 words. Grok-4.1-Fast achieves the highest Visual Grounding (Svg=0.498) but the lowest Disease Identification among proprietary models (Sdis=0.224), demonstrating a concrete seeing-vs.-diagnosing gap. The question-guided protocol improves Diagnosis Correctness by ~48.8% for Qwen25-VL-32B and ~47.4% for Ministral-8B over direct diagnosis—tested on only these two models in the main ablation. Cascading (auto-regressive) retains 96.3% of Guided efficiency and 81.7% of Guided Diagnostic Accuracy across all 18 models, with an average Scaffolded→Guided efficiency gain of +22.09%. Metric weight sensitivity for Sclin is robust (Spearman ρs ∈ [0.92, 0.97] across three alternative weight regimes). LLM-as-judge (GPT-5 + Gemini-3-Pro) on the 5,000-image expert-verified subset preserves the top-5 ordering with median rank shift ≤2. Baselines are current and strong (frontier proprietary and open models as of early 2026). All models perform poorly on Safety (best Ssafe=0.214, Llama-3.2-90B), confirming false reassurance as a systemic failure. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW FORMULATION. The genuine novelty is (1) formalizing severity→intent→question-trajectory as a dataset-level structural property rather than a prompting strategy, and (2) the domain-specific clinical metric suite (Ssafe, Sclin, E, B, F). Individual components—VLM-based cue extraction, LLM-based QA synthesis, multi-turn evaluation—are established techniques competently applied to a new domain combination. Rigor & Reproducibility Dataset and code are publicly released (GitHub MIT, Hugging Face CC BY 4.0). The Cascading vs. Guided ablation is the paper's strongest methodological contribution, credibly isolating CoI structure from oracle history. Inter-annotator agreement on a 600-image triple-coded subset reports Gwet's AC1 of 0.992/0.937/0.960—correctly using Gwet's coefficient for high-prevalence conditions. A blind residual-error audit by a fourth expert on 500 previously-unflagged images yields 96.2% correctness and bounds critical errors below 0.5% of the corpus. The metric weight sensitivity analysis is good practice. The primary methodological concern is the synthetic ground truth: 94.7% of QA answers are Qwen2.5-7B-generated, with expert validity assessed at 95.8% on an 8,500-pair spot-check by the same expert pool that designed the templates. No independent external validation by plant pathologists uninvolved in benchmark construction is reported. The LLM-as-judge validation (GPT-5 + Gemini-3-Pro) mitigates lexical rigidity but does not resolve the circularity—both ground truth and judges are LLMs. The Protocol Structure Benefit Test (the ~48% improvement result) is shown for only 2 of 18 evaluated models in quantified form. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Visual cue extraction uses a 4B-parameter model; benchmark evaluation requires API access to frontier MLLMs but imposes no special hardware constraints. - Efficient: The generation pipeline is low-cost; LLM-as-judge evaluation on 5,000 images with GPT-5 and Gemini-3-Pro is API-expensive and not reproducible without significant budget. - Effective: The benchmark credibly surfaces a clinical reasoning gap (best Sclin=0.188) and the CoI structure demonstrably improves diagnostics in ablation, but the LLM-generated ground truth limits clinical authority—effectiveness against real expert judgment is not directly measured. - Scalable: The template-based generation scales mechanically to new crops and diseases, but extension to additional geographies, languages, or sensing modalities (multispectral, field conditions beyond single static images) is not addressed and represents unstated scope. The paper delivers a dataset and evaluation toolkit, not a trained model or deployable system. Strengths - The severity→intent→question-trajectory formalization operationalizes a genuine expert cognitive workflow as an immutable dataset property, making CoI reproducible and resistant to prompting variability—a conceptually clean contribution. - The clinical metric suite, particularly Ssafe (false reassurance penalty) and E (efficiency as grounded cues per 100 words), directly targets deployment-relevant failure modes that F1/BLEU obscure. - The Cascading ablation cleanly separates structural scaffolding from conversational memory, providing the paper's strongest empirical claim: structure, not oracle access, drives diagnostic improvement. - Scale and taxonomic breadth (34 crops, 116 diseases, 204 crop-disease combinations, 24,950 images) are sufficient to differentiate 18 contemporary MLLMs and reveal cross-class fairness failures. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly note: (1) single static images cannot capture holistic sensory inputs (tactile texture, soil moisture) required in real-world plant pathology; (2) even top models produce false reassurance, making autonomous deployment premature—the system is a decision-support tool for experts; (3) the benchmark is English-only, limiting accessibility for non-English-speaking smallholder farmers who would benefit most. These limitations are confronted directly and candidly. Analyst-Identified Gaps - LLM ground-truth circularity (primary): 94.7% of QA answers are synthesized by Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct. The benchmark thus partially measures MLLM agreement with a specific LLM's clinical vocabulary and reasoning style. Expert spot-check validity (95.8%) was assessed by the same team that designed the templates—no independent external pathologist validation is reported. LLM-as-judge with GPT-5 and Gemini-3-Pro cannot resolve this; it replaces one form of LLM circularity with another. - Protocol Structure Benefit Test scope: The headline ~48% correctness improvement from question-guided vs. direct diagnosis is quantitatively reported for only 2 of 18 models; remaining models are shown only in Appendix Figure A.13 without tabulated values, preventing systematic comparison. - Severity classification by VLM, not human: SAD-based severity tiers (mild/moderate/severe) for the full 24,950-image corpus were assigned by Qwen3-VL-4B, not human experts. The 93.8% factuality score covers symptom description, not severity tier assignment, which directly determines which CoI template—and therefore which ground-truth answers—are applied to each image. - No fine-tuning experiments: The paper presents itself as a foundational benchmark for training diagnostic agents but provides no experiments showing the training split improves model performance. Zero-shot evaluation alone cannot validate the benchmark's utility as a training resource. - Geographic provenance bias: The component datasets are predominantly Bangladesh/South Asia-sourced. Disease manifestations vary by cultivar, climate, and region; the benchmark's generalizability to other agricultural contexts is unstated and unvalidated. Bottom Line PlantInquiryVQA is a well-engineered benchmark that credibly exposes a real and important gap—current frontier MLLMs cannot reliably translate visual symptom description into safe clinical diagnosis—but practitioners should treat its Clinical Utility and Safety scores as lower-bounds on what expert-grounded evaluation would reveal, not as direct measures of clinical competence, given that the ground truth is overwhelmingly LLM-synthesized.
Search Results 2604.20983 PDF
#91 — Innovative (61)

TCOD: Exploring Temporal Curriculum in On-Policy Distillation for Multi-turn Autonomous Agents (5 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 29 Apr 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TCOD (Temporal Curriculum On-Policy Distillation), a training framework that progressively expands the trajectory depth exposed to a student model during on-policy distillation, stabilizing multi-turn agent training via two variants: Forward-to-Backward (F2B) and Backward-to-Forward (B2F). - Why it's important: Vanilla on-policy distillation collapses small models (<3B) to near-zero success rates in multi-turn settings, blocking a natural path to deploying capable compact agents; the failure mode had not been systematically identified or addressed before. - How it does it: A linear curriculum schedule restricts the student to only k interaction steps per training episode (growing as k = k_start + ⌊n/η⌋), so early training avoids the error-compounded tail of trajectories where the teacher's supervision signal is unreliable. - The good: On ALFWorld's hard split (tasks where the teacher itself fails under pass@10), TCOD-B2F surpasses the teacher's 6.61% success rate by up to 14 points with a same-capacity student, demonstrating learning beyond imitation. - The missing: No RL baselines (GRPO, PPO) are compared directly — since the teacher is GRPO-trained, the question of whether just training students with GRPO achieves comparable results is unaddressed. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building distillation or post-training pipelines for multi-turn LLM agents; the KL instability diagnosis alone is actionable, and TCOD-F2B is a near-zero-cost drop-in modification to existing OPD pipelines.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (70) and Novelty (65) are the strongest dimensions — the trajectory-level KL instability characterization is a credible new empirical finding, and curriculum-as-trajectory-depth is a non-obvious framing. Rigor (60) pulled down by single-seed runs across the board and no RL baselines (GRPO/PPO) despite the teacher being GRPO-trained — the most important missing comparison. Resilience (55) suffers from the same omission and the train-distribution 'hard split' framing. Resembles the user-positive on-policy distillation paper in topic and methodology. Weighted: 0.20*65 + 0.25*60 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*55 ≈ 61.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 60 Resilience 55 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Training small LLMs as multi-turn agents via on-policy distillation (OPD) fails because each student-generated action and observation is appended to a growing history, causing compounding errors across turns. As trajectory depth increases, the student drifts further from the teacher's distribution: per-turn KL divergence grows monotonically with the turn index, and the teacher assigns progressively lower probabilities to student-generated tokens, rendering its supervision signal unreliable. For models below 3B parameters, the trajectory-level KL escalates to 1,000–3,000 while task success rate collapses to near zero within tens of training steps. For larger models, initial KL values (~1,000) are orders of magnitude above convergence values (~60), indicating severe training instability. Prior OPD work (Jang et al. 2026, Jin et al. 2026, Ko et al. 2026) improved single-turn stability through objective reformulation and RL-style heuristics but did not address the multi-turn trajectory-level failure mode. Key Idea Trajectory difficulty in multi-turn OPD is monotonically governed by depth: early turns are in-distribution for the teacher, while later turns drift out-of-distribution as student errors compound. By defining curriculum difficulty as trajectory length and training the student on progressively longer horizons, TCOD ensures KL supervision is computed only over turns where the teacher's signal remains reliable. The trick is structural — restrict the loss to a growing prefix (F2B) or a growing suffix from teacher-navigated checkpoints (B2F), rather than modifying the KL objective itself. Methodology Both variants use the standard token-level forward KL objective but differ in which trajectory segment receives gradient. TCOD-F2B: the student rolls out from the initial state for k steps; k grows linearly as k = k_start + ⌊n/η⌋ where n is the training step and η is the growth rate. The student first masters early turns, then progressively handles the full horizon. TCOD-B2F: pre-collected successful teacher trajectories serve as scaffolds; the teacher replays the first L–k steps (stop-gradient), placing the student at a vetted near-terminal state; the student handles the remaining k steps and receives gradient updates. As k increases, the student manages progressively earlier portions until it executes the full trajectory end-to-end, closing the train-test distribution gap. Three critical design decisions: (1) linear pacing schedule is shown robust across η ∈ {2, 4, 6} with <2% performance variation; (2) staleness-aware sub-trajectory experience replay with Δ_max=2 balances sample efficiency against the on-policy constraint; (3) asynchronous actor/learner/teacher processes on 8×H20 GPUs via a lock-free ring buffer maximize GPU utilization. Built on Trinity-RFT. Single seed (42) used throughout. Key Results On ALFWorld (Qwen2.5-3B student, GRPO-trained Qwen2.5-7B teacher): TCOD-F2B raises valid-unseen success rate from 60.45% (vanilla OPD) to 79.19% (+18.74 pp) while reducing action rounds by 3.74 steps; TCOD-B2F achieves 77.86%/70.90% (seen/unseen). With a 7B student, TCOD-B2F reaches 86.43% seen (+11.06 pp over vanilla OPD). On the teacher-failure hard split (121 tasks, teacher SR=6.61%), TCOD-B2F with 7B student achieves 20.66% (+7.44 pp over OPD). Cross-benchmark (Qwen3-1.7B student, Qwen3-30B teacher): vanilla OPD collapses to 0.17% average SR across WebShop/ALFWorld/ScienceWorld; TCOD variants recover to ~18.5–18.8% — a near-total recovery from collapse. Gains with Qwen3-4B are modest (1–2 pp over OPD). Training time: TCOD-F2B reduces wall-clock time by ~32% (4.9h vs 7.18h on ALFWorld). Baselines are zero-shot student, SFT on teacher trajectories, and vanilla OPD; no RL-based training baselines (GRPO, PPO, RAFT) are compared. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. Curriculum learning (Bengio 2009) and on-policy distillation are both established; the novel contribution is their specific combination to address trajectory-depth instability in multi-turn agent distillation. The empirical characterization of Trajectory-Level KL Instability — per-turn KL growth, initial vs. convergence KL magnitude gap, and the teacher-capacity-match finding — constitutes a genuinely new empirical finding across multiple model families. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover curriculum growth rate η ∈ {2, 4, 6}, teacher type (domain-specific GRPO-tuned vs. general large model), and student size (0.5B–7B). However, all experiments use a single random seed (42) with no variance estimates, making 1–2 pp gains in the Qwen3-4B setting statistically ambiguous. No code or model weights are released; the framework depends on Trinity-RFT (Pan et al. 2025), which is separately available. Methodological concerns: (1) The hard split is constructed from the training split where the teacher fails — it tests robustness, not held-out generalization. (2) Only binary success rate is reported; ScienceWorld natively provides 0–100 partial-credit scores that are unused. (3) Training runs only 250 steps, leaving open whether vanilla OPD would recover the same performance given longer training. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: Requires 8×H20 96GB GPUs for training; teacher models up to 30B parameters. Not accessible on consumer hardware, though the student artifacts are small models (0.5B–7B). - Efficient: TCOD-F2B reduces training time by 32% versus vanilla OPD; TCOD-B2F adds overhead from pre-collecting teacher successful trajectories via pass@10 sampling. - Effective: Evidence is strong that TCOD resolves KL instability and improves task success rates on the tested benchmarks; the teacher-surpassing result on hard tasks is a credible claim, though that split is drawn from the train distribution. - Scalable: Tested on models up to 7B student / 30B teacher. Behavior at larger student scales (13B, 70B) is not evaluated or discussed. Delivers a training recipe, not a released software artifact or model checkpoint. Strengths - The empirical characterization of Trajectory-Level KL Instability is thorough: per-turn KL divergence plots, initial-vs.-convergence magnitude comparisons across eight student-teacher pairs, and a mechanistic explanation (causal coupling via history accumulation) that is convincing and reproducible from the provided figures. - TCOD-F2B is a genuinely low-friction modification — requiring only a step counter and a max-step cap — making it easily applicable to any existing OPD codebase. - The teacher-surpassing result on hard tasks (TCOD-B2F student outperforms teacher by up to 14 points on tasks the teacher fails) is surprising and suggests the curriculum induces more robust end-game policies, not just better teacher imitation. - The finding that teacher-student capacity match matters more than absolute teacher scale is a practically important design insight, backed by quantitative comparison (3B←30B vs. 3B←7B-RL vs. 7B←7B-RL). Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly state: TCOD-B2F requires pre-collection of successful teacher trajectories (extra overhead); the fixed linear schedule may not be optimal for all environments (suggesting adaptive mechanisms as future work); evaluation is limited to three text-based benchmarks, and extending to multimodal or physically embodied settings is an important open question. The authors confront these credibly rather than minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The most important missing comparison is RL-based training (GRPO, PPO) applied directly to the student models. The teacher is GRPO-trained, so GRPO is demonstrably applicable to these environments; the paper does not show whether students trained with GRPO directly would match or exceed TCOD's success rates. - Single seed with no error bars makes the small gains in Table 3 (1–2 pp for Qwen3-4B) unreliable; multiple seeds are standard in the RL agent literature. - The hard split is drawn from the training distribution (tasks where the teacher fails on the train split); claims about generalization beyond the teacher are therefore claims about robustness on seen-distribution tasks, not true out-of-distribution generalization. - 250 training steps is too short to rule out that vanilla OPD would eventually converge to comparable performance; the training curves in Figure 4 suggest OPD is still improving at step 200. - WebShop and ScienceWorld results are only shown for Qwen3 models, while ALFWorld comparisons use Qwen2.5; this cross-benchmark heterogeneity makes it impossible to directly compare methods across environments at consistent scales. Bottom Line This preprint offers a practical diagnosis of a real multi-turn distillation failure mode and a lightweight fix — TCOD-F2B especially — that practitioners can apply immediately, but the absence of RL training baselines (GRPO/PPO) is a significant omission that leaves the most important empirical question unanswered.
Recommended 2604.24005 PDF
#92 — Innovative (60)

Programming with Data: Test-Driven Data Engineering for Self-Improving LLMs from Raw Corpora (70 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 29 Apr 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ProDa, a closed-loop data engineering framework that maps the domain fine-tuning lifecycle onto test-driven software development by using a three-level knowledge structure (atomic concepts, relational triples, reasoning chains) extracted from raw corpora as a shared specification for both training data synthesis and benchmark construction, enabling model failures to be traced to specific data deficits and repaired through targeted patches. - Why it's important: Current domain fine-tuning is open-loop: when a model fails, there is no mechanism to trace the failure back to a specific data deficiency, so practitioners resort to indiscriminate data scaling that is computationally expensive and provides no structural guarantee of improvement. - How it does it: A three-component pipeline (Builder, Tester, Debugger) extracts L1/L2/L3 knowledge from corpora top-down, constructs benchmarks exclusively from L3 reasoning chains, synthesizes training data from L1/L2 entries to ensure instance-level orthogonality, then classifies each benchmark failure as a concept gap or reasoning deficit and generates targeted repair patches anchored to the responsible knowledge nodes. - The good: After one debugging round, ProDa V2 at only 1K repair samples (68.72%) strictly outperforms the peak accuracy of Alpaca, EasyDataset, and DataFlow at all tested scales up to 10K, and Qwen-3-32B-V2 at 79.52% exceeds all reference Instruct models including GPT-5.4 (76.82%) on the authors' own ProDa-16 benchmark. - The missing: All headline results comparing ProDa-tuned models against frontier closed-source models are measured exclusively on ProDa-16, a benchmark constructed from the same textbook corpus used to train the models, making it impossible to determine whether the reported gains reflect genuine generalization or optimization on a structurally coupled artifact. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers designing domain-specific fine-tuning pipelines or structured curriculum learning systems; those primarily interested in robust evaluation of LLM generalization should treat the benchmark results with significant skepticism until independent validation appears.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (50) and Resilience (45) pull the composite down sharply: the flagship claim of beating GPT-5.4/Gemini-3/DeepSeek-v3.2 rests on ProDa-16, a benchmark co-derived from the same textbook corpus used to train the models — instance-level orthogonality does not eliminate provenance coupling. The LLM judge classifying failures has unquantified error rate, comparison pits base+LoRA against RLHF Instruct models, and no error bars appear in main results. Novelty (75) for the closed-loop traceable-failure paradigm and zero-orphan reachability invariant is genuine, and the sample-efficiency result (1K targeted vs 10K conventional) is striking.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 55 Resilience 45 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Domain-specific fine-tuning of large language models suffers from an open-loop structure: training data and evaluation benchmarks are constructed independently, so when a model fails on a benchmark item, there is no shared representation that connects the failure to a specific deficiency in the training data. Practitioners respond by adding more data indiscriminately, an approach that is expensive, uninterpretable, and structurally guaranteed to miss targeted deficits. The pre-training playbook—scale covers everything—does not transfer to domain fine-tuning where source corpora are limited and every failure carries actionable diagnostic information that the current workflow discards. Key Idea: The core insight is that when training data and benchmarks are both derived from a single structured knowledge representation extracted from the source corpus (the shared specification), benchmark failures become traceable to specific nodes in that structure rather than being opaque scalar signals. This mirrors the role of a shared specification in test-driven software development: without it, a failing test bears no structural relationship to the code responsible for the defect. The key technical move is top-down extraction—reasoning chains (L3) are extracted first, then decomposed into relational triples (L2), then into atomic concepts (L1)—guaranteeing that every L1 and L2 entry is reachable from at least one L3 chain (zero orphan nodes), which in turn guarantees that every benchmark failure can be traced backward to a repairable data deficit. Methodology: ProDa operates through three pipeline components. The Builder extracts a three-level knowledge structure from quality-filtered corpus chunks using LLM-driven prompts: L3 reasoning chains (multi-step inferential pathways), L2 relational triples (typed subject-predicate-object statements derived by sliding-window decomposition of L3 steps), and L1 atomic concepts (harvested from L2 subjects/objects with canonical definitions). Quality filtering applies a six-dimensional scoring rubric (reasoning depth, prerequisite density, scenario applicability, counter-intuitive index, knowledge synthesis, breakpoint smoothness) to retain ~48k high-quality chunks from 117k raw documents. The Tester constructs benchmarks from L3 chains using three perturbation operators: SUBSTADJ (replace a node with a semantically adjacent concept), INVREL (invert the relation type), and TRUNC (truncate the chain at an intermediate step). Training data (S) is synthesized from L1/L2 entries in three formats (open-ended QA, multiple-choice, true/false); benchmark items (B) are constructed from L3 chains. Structural separation B=f_bench(K3), S=f_syn(K1,K2) ensures instance-level orthogonality. The Debugger analyzes each failure using an LLM judge that classifies it as a concept gap (missing/confused L1 or L2 entry) or reasoning deficit (failure to compose known L1/L2 along an L3 pathway), then generates targeted patches: knowledge-reinforcement samples (with contrastive examples against semantically adjacent concepts) for concept gaps, and chain-of-thought scaffolding samples for reasoning deficits. Data mixing uses error-proportional allocation across disciplines and L2-disjoint experience replay to prevent catastrophic forgetting. All training uses LoRA (rank 16–32) on base model checkpoints with the LLaMA-Factory framework. Key Results: Applied to 16 disciplines (physics, engineering, medicine, mathematics, computer science, biology, chemistry, earth science, materials science, education, economics, history, environmental science, sociology, psychology, astronomy), ProDa extracts 43,953 L3 chains, 186,784 L2 statements, and 227,869 L1 concepts from ~1.5B tokens with zero orphan nodes and >99% largest-connected-component coverage in every discipline. ProDa-16 benchmark: Spearman ρ=0.847 mean correlation with 11 established benchmarks, including GPQA (ρ=0.943) and MMLU-Pro (ρ=0.905). On ProDa-16: V1 (first-pass, 160K static samples) achieves Qwen-3-32B at 77.35%, matching or exceeding corresponding Instruct models at 4 of 9 tested scales. After one debugging round (V2/Round 3), Qwen-2.5-32B reaches 78.84% and Qwen-3-32B reaches 79.52%, both exceeding GPT-5.4 (76.82%), Gemini-3-flash (76.60%), and DeepSeek-v3.2 (76.69%) on this benchmark. Gains from V1→V2 range from +0.77 (Qwen-3-14B) to +32.67 (Llama-3.1-8B, baseline was 30.35% due to format-following failure). Sample efficiency: ProDa V2 at 1K targeted repair samples achieves 68.72% vs Alpaca peak 68.12% at 2K; at 5K, ProDa V2 reaches 72.11% vs DataFlow Filter 56.18% (a 16-point absolute gap). General capability preservation: median MMLU change from Base to V2 is +0.27 points across 9 models; C-Eval shows similar or improved results at V2 vs Base. This is a preprint; results are not peer-reviewed. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The core novel contribution is the closed-loop data engineering paradigm with traceable failure attribution, which is structurally distinct from prior self-improvement methods (which operate at inference-time outputs) and data-centric methods (which rewrite samples without knowledge-level grounding). The top-down extraction order that guarantees zero orphan nodes and full reachability is a specific novel design choice. The ProDaLib knowledge base, ProDa-16 benchmark, and ProDa Studio IDE are distinct artifact contributions. The software-development analogy is conceptually framing rather than novel mathematics; the execution of the analogy in a working system is the contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility: The paper provides thorough ablations comparing V1 vs V2 across 9 model families and 16 disciplines, and compares against 3 baseline synthesis methods (Alpaca, EasyDataset, DataFlow) at 4 data scales. However, no error bars are reported in the main Table 1 results (bootstrap CIs appear only in Figure 4b for overall benchmark accuracy by model). No multiple random seeds or significance tests are reported for the training runs. The benchmark validation relies on Spearman correlation at the model level rather than item-level construct validity, which is a weaker form of evidence. The critical methodological concern is that the benchmark is co-derived from the same textbook corpus as the training data—the paper addresses this through instance-level orthogonality (L3 for benchmarks, L1/L2 for training), but the shared provenance still creates structural coupling that could inflate apparent gains. Code is available at github.com/OpenRaiser/ProDa and data at huggingface.co/datasets/OpenRaiser/ProDalib. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Fine-tuning uses LoRA on standard GPU infrastructure (LoRA rank 16–32, batch size 1024, bf16 precision); models from 3B to 32B are tested, all on commercially available hardware via LLaMA-Factory. - Efficient: The knowledge extraction pipeline processes 117k documents through multiple LLM calls (L3, L2, L1 extraction separately); at 160K training samples per round and multiple debugging iterations, the total compute is substantial and not benchmarked against baselines on wall-clock time or GPU-hours. - Effective: On the authors' ProDa-16 benchmark, the closed-loop debugging consistently improves all nine tested models; the +16-point margin over DataFlow at matched data scale is the strongest evidence of effectiveness. Generalization to fully independent benchmarks is not demonstrated for the ProDa-tuned models. - Scalable: The framework is demonstrated at 32B parameters and 16 disciplines; the paper claims discipline-agnostic applicability but does not test beyond these 16 disciplines or beyond 32B parameters. The claim that the LLM judge and patch generation scale to larger error sets is not stress-tested. The paper delivers software (ProDa Studio), a dataset (ProDaLib), and a benchmark (ProDa-16). Strengths: - The reachability invariant (zero orphan nodes by top-down extraction) is a principled design choice that provides a formal guarantee enabling the debugging loop; this distinguishes the framework from ad-hoc data augmentation approaches. - The instance-level orthogonality between training data (from L1/L2) and benchmark items (from L3) is a carefully designed structural safeguard against benchmark memorization, stronger than typical data contamination controls. - Sample efficiency results are striking: 1K targeted repair samples outperforming 10K+ of conventional synthesis methods suggests that diagnostic targeting, not data volume, is the operative variable. - The ProDaLib release (227k concepts, 186k statements, 43k chains, 16k benchmark items, 160k training samples) across 16 disciplines is a genuine community resource with full traceability metadata. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge in the Discussion that the current work establishes only the "macro-level architecture" and that every module (Builder, Tester, Debugger) remains a research problem admitting improvement. They note that domain-specific expertise cannot be acquired through self-play without authoritative grounding. They do not explicitly address the benchmark circularity concern or the limited evaluation on independent held-out benchmarks for ProDa-tuned models. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most serious gap is benchmark circularity: the headline results (ProDa models outperforming GPT-5.4 and DeepSeek-v3.2) are measured on ProDa-16, which is derived from the same textbook corpus used to train the ProDa models. Even with instance-level orthogonality, both artifacts share provenance—models trained on these textbooks may be structurally advantaged on questions about these textbooks. No evaluation of ProDa-tuned models on completely independent domain benchmarks (e.g., MedQA, SciQ, or discipline-specific held-out exams constructed from different sources) is provided. A second gap: the LLM judge classifying failures into concept_gap vs capability_deficit is itself an LLM call with unquantified error rate; misclassification would generate repair patches targeting the wrong data component, yet no analysis of diagnostic accuracy is given. Third: the comparison in Table 1 Panel A pits ProDa-tuned base models against Instruct models that incorporate RLHF and preference optimization—this is not a controlled comparison of data synthesis quality. Fourth: all experiments use LoRA rather than full fine-tuning, and the paper does not investigate whether the traceability property survives at different LoRA ranks or full fine-tuning regimes. Bottom Line: ProDa makes a genuinely interesting structural contribution—grounding training data and evaluation in a shared knowledge graph to enable traceable failure diagnosis—and the sample efficiency results are compelling, but the flagship claim of surpassing frontier closed-source models rests entirely on a benchmark co-derived from the training corpus and cannot be accepted without independent validation on held-out domain benchmarks.
Recommended 2604.24819 PDF
#93 — Innovative (60)

MCAP: Deployment-Time Layer Profiling for Memory-Constrained LLM Inference

arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MCAP (Monte Carlo Activation Profiling), a 60-second load-time per-layer importance estimator, and NVE, a Rust+CUDA inference engine that uses it to jointly drive per-layer precision dispatch (W4A8 vs. W4A16) and three-tier memory placement (GPU/RAM/SSD), enabling LLM inference at memory budgets previously infeasible for standard quantized models. - Why it's important: Even 4-bit 8B models require ∼5–6 GB resident VRAM, excluding most consumer GPUs and edge accelerators, and existing post-training quantization fixes the precision choice offline before hardware is known, locking out heterogeneous deployment targets. - How it does it: A streaming forward pass over 12 calibration prompts computes per-layer activation magnitude (attention proxy + FFN L2 norm), producing a 338-byte architecture-keyed profile that simultaneously drives precision routing and memory tiering without modifying weights. - The good: Llama-3.2-3B (∼6.4 GB BF16) runs in 2 GB at 2.31 tok/s and Llama-3.1-8B (∼16 GB BF16) runs in 4 GB at 1.25 tok/s, both with no observable quality degradation on the evaluated task suite, at memory levels where standard 4-bit quantization cannot reach. - The missing: Throughput is measured only at batch=1 on NVIDIA T4/A10G, with no comparison to Marlin, ExLlamaV2/EXL2, or TensorRT-LLM; quality evaluation at constrained memory budgets rests on an n=8 generative suite the paper itself acknowledges cannot support hypothesis testing; and the key cross-silicon validation on Jetson, Apple Silicon, and consumer RTX is explicitly deferred. - Read it or not: Read if you work on edge or constrained-hardware LLM deployment — the paging + importance-guided precision architecture is substantive and honestly characterized — but verify throughput gains against EXL2 mixed-precision and your target silicon before adopting.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (72) reflects a complete released artifact (NVE engine, 22K Rust + 1,413 CUDA, MIT-licensed) with demonstrated 8B-in-4GB inference, but constrained to NVIDIA Turing/Ampere with deferred edge-silicon validation. Rigor (52) is the main drag: primary throughput baseline is only llama.cpp Q4_0 — Marlin, EXL2, TensorRT-LLM are explicitly missing — and the constrained-memory quality claim rests on an n=8 suite the paper itself notes cannot support hypothesis testing; PMI clustering, claimed load-bearing for 9–58% gains, is unablated. Novelty (60) is moderate: the load-time architecture-keyed profile coupling precision and residency is the genuine contribution, but activation magnitude as importance proxy, W4A8 dp4a, and three-tier paging are established. Non-Obviousness (60), Completeness (62), and Resilience (58) are lifted by unusually honest failure-mode documentation, anomaly flagging, and a claim-to-evidence index — but the missing EXL2 comparison would clearly bite under adversarial review. Weighted: 0.15·60 + 0.15·52 + 0.10·60 + 0.15·62 + 0.15·58 + 0.30·72 ≈ 61.
systems Novelty 60 Rigor 52 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 62 Resilience 58 Impact 72
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation The deployment barrier for LLMs is memory, not compute. Even a 4-bit 8B model requires ∼5–6 GB resident VRAM, excluding consumer GPUs under 6 GB, mobile accelerators, and older cloud silicon. The root constraint is that existing post-training quantization (GPTQ, AWQ, EXL2) fixes precision at offline calibration time and emits a new weight artifact per precision target, locking in the set of viable hardware before deployment. In heterogeneous settings — where target VRAM, thermal budget, and battery state vary across devices and sessions — this offline binding is the primary limiting factor. Separately, current systems treat precision routing and memory placement as independent decisions requiring independent mechanisms (e.g., quantization methods for precision, FlexGen/PowerInfer for offloading), with no unified signal driving both from a single runtime observation. Key Idea The core insight is that a lightweight, gradient-free forward pass over a small set of calibration prompts is sufficient to rank transformer layers by their sensitivity to INT8 activation quantization, because INT8 quantization error is absolute and layers with large activation dynamic ranges incur disproportionate distortion. This per-layer ranking — computable in 60 seconds on the target device, stored as a 338-byte JSON keyed by model architecture (not by weight checkpoint) — can drive two decisions that prior systems make separately: per-layer precision dispatch (W4A8 vs. W4A16) and per-layer memory residency tier (GPU VRAM / CPU RAM / SSD). The profile is hardware-independent and reused across all subsequent loads of the same architecture. Methodology MCAP computes a score s_i for each transformer layer i as the mean over 12 calibration prompts and token positions of the sum of two magnitude signals: an attention proxy (||[Q_i·x_t, V_i·x_t]||₂, the L2 norm of concatenated query and value projections) and an FFN magnitude (||FFN_i(x_t)||₂). Scores are min-max normalized to [0,1], and a threshold τ=0.7 partitions layers into high-importance (W4A16) and low-importance (W4A8). The profiler streams one layer at a time, achieving O(1) peak memory with respect to depth — 203 MB vs. 6,000 MB for full 3B model loading. Three critical design decisions: (1) streaming layer-by-layer during profiling — well justified for constrained targets; (2) combining attention and FFN signals rather than either alone — validated by ablation showing a scale-dependent inversion where FFN dominates at <1B and attention at ≥3B, with the combined proxy covering both without switching; (3) caching by architecture hash rather than weight hash — pragmatically justified for deployment amortization, though potentially stale after fine-tuning. A sub-Gaussian concentration bound motivates 12 prompts as sufficient for top-k layer recovery given the observed outlier gaps (1.6× to 39× ratio between outlier and mean of remaining layers across 8 models). The NVE inference engine (22K lines Rust, 1,413 lines hand-tuned CUDA) exposes three execution modes driven by one profile: Config A (paged — all layers tiered across GPU→RAM→SSD using LRU + PMI-based co-activation clustering for prefetch, quality preserved); Config B (hot-only — layers below threshold are skipped entirely, no paging traffic); Config C (hot-only + AWQ saliency-weighted quantization on retained layers). The W4A8 path uses dp4a INT8 dot-product instructions with CUDA graph capture, 17 fused kernels, and deferred bias correction to avoid per-nibble inner-loop subtracts. Key Results On NVIDIA T4 (sm_75), batch=1, NVE W4A8 delivers 269.1 tok/s on Llama-3.2-1B, 108.8 tok/s on Llama-3.2-3B, and 47.7 tok/s on Llama-3.1-8B — 1.78×, 1.53×, 1.55× over llama.cpp Q4_0 and 2.31×, 2.53×, 2.86× over NVE W4A16. The W4A8 advantage grows with model size, consistent with increasing memory-boundedness of the dominant FFN projections. WikiText-2 PPL is 17.51 for W4A16, W4A8, and MCAP Mixed at 50 sequences on Llama-3.2-1B (Δ=0.00); HellaSwag is 54.0% for all three (n=50). Against AutoAWQ INT4 at matched 4-bit precision, NVE achieves 54.0% vs. 52.0% HellaSwag while AWQ achieves lower PPL (16.64 vs. 17.51), with the authors correctly noting these operate at different granularities and are composable. Memory-constrained: Llama-3.2-3B runs in 2 GB at 2.31 tok/s (100% task accuracy, 9% higher throughput than unconstrained via working-set reduction); Llama-3.1-8B runs in 4 GB at 1.25 tok/s (88% accuracy matching baseline, 58% higher than unconstrained); both llama.cpp and HuggingFace FP16 OOM. Cross-silicon: one 338-byte profile drives 87.5% task accuracy across six independent containers on Turing T4 and Ampere A10G at 0 ms on-device profiling cost. One anomaly is flagged openly: 8B at 8 GB shows 0.55 tok/s, lower than both unconstrained (0.79) and 4 GB (1.25), tentatively attributed to hot-tier cache thrashing against KV cache but without a controlled ablation. The primary throughput baseline is llama.cpp Q4_0; Marlin, ExLlamaV2 EXL2, TensorRT-LLM, DeepSpeed, and vLLM comparisons were attempted but failed to run on T4 at batch=1 due to compatibility issues, and are not reported. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The genuinely novel contribution is the concept of a load-time control layer that couples precision dispatch and memory residency decisions from a single architecture-keyed signal computed on the target device, combined with the PMI-based co-activation clustering for the weight pager. What is standard application of existing ideas: activation magnitude as layer importance proxy (precedent in AWQ at channel level), W4A8 via dp4a (used in llama.cpp and Atom), three-tier weight paging (FlexGen precedent), and EXL2's per-layer bit allocation (offline; the key differentiation is load-time vs. offline). Rigor & Reproducibility This is a preprint (arXiv:2604.21026v2, April 2026), not peer-reviewed.
